product.name: Maybern Sales Teleprompter
product.build_lineage: v6 → v24 (consolidated) → v3 brain + gong patch + v4 foundation + judgment layers
product.description: Live-call sales intelligence engine for Maybern AEs. Combines Corporate Visions methodology, MEDDPICC framework, pattern-cluster detection, priority scoring, vendor intelligence, speaker filtering, and two operating modes (Guided Meeting + Live Copilot) into a single JSON brain that runs locally without APIs.

content_layer.meetings: 12 meeting types (M1-M12) across the full Maybern Sales Lifecycle
content_layer.stages: 18 stages with belief shifts and proof-of-shift criteria
content_layer.discovery_questions: 105 questions tagged with question_type, methodology_tags (MEDDPICC), principle_tags (17 Corporate Visions principles), conversation_signals (20 signal types), meeting_relevance, and stage_relevance
content_layer.reframing_lines: 63 reframes matched to principles and stages
content_layer.proof_points: 45 proof points matched to pain themes
content_layer.opening_statements: 16 (7 canonical scripts for M1/M2 with session editing support)
content_layer.trigger_conditions: 83 with contextual phrase matching and supporting_signal_only flags
content_layer.predictive_queues: 95 entries across all 18 stages (Corporate Visions progression)

technical_playbook.description: Product Q&A playbook sourced from Sophie Mester client presentations. Extensible — enrichable with new feature releases.

intelligence_layer.decision_engine: 10-step evaluation order (speaker filter → cluster match → decision rules → content selection → vendor enrichment → principle filter → type filter → escalation → fallback)
intelligence_layer.priority_scoring: 5 dimensions (stage match, signal cluster match, principle match, advancing/confirming fit, MEDDPICC relevance), max 50 points
intelligence_layer.signal_bridge: 20 entries mapping DQ conversation_signals to cluster confidence_signals
intelligence_layer.speaker_detection: First-message classification + turn alternation + consecutive-turn override with 22 acknowledgment phrases and handoff signal detection
intelligence_layer.trigger_firing_rules: Contextual multi-word matching for generic terms, single-word matching for proper nouns only, supporting_signal_only flag for ambiguous words
intelligence_layer.vendor_asset_integration: Step 4a in decision engine. 6 move-to-asset mappings. Category-aware behavior (competitor, system_of_record, integration_partner, ancillary_tool, friendamie). Session tracking prevents repetition.

presentation_layer.teleprompter_modes: Guided Meeting (6-phase structured playbook) + Live Copilot (reactive, signal-driven)
presentation_layer.question_tracker: 10 categories derived from existing MEDDPICC and conversation_signal tags. Persistent sidebar. Status: complete/partial/not_started.
presentation_layer.momentum_suggestion: Reads buyer's last 1-2 turns, detects directional signals, surfaces one recommended move with HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW confidence.
presentation_layer.session_script_system: Editable session copies of 7 canonical opening scripts. Ephemeral — deleted on session end. Reset-to-default available.
presentation_layer.mode_switching: Single-tap toggle. Preserves all session state (meeting, stage, dq_status, meddpicc_coverage, active_cluster, vendor_shown_assets).

companion_files.vendor_intelligence_registry.json: 42 vendors with SWOT, client feedback, integration examples, and typical workflows. 54 KB.
companion_files.canonical_conversation_assets.json: 182 conversation assets (credibility stories, integration explanations, positioning responses, rebuttals, discovery prompts) across 17 vendors. 40 KB.
companion_files.teleprompter_ui_logic.md: UI design specification for card selection, DQ tracking, trigger thresholds, and tab layout. 24 KB.

manifest.product_description: Real-time sales companion for Maybern. Listens to the room, detects signals, pushes rebuttals, tracks discovery depth, and coaches the rep through M1 and M2 conversations.

meeting_types.belief_shift: I have a structural risk I haven't fully examined.
meeting_types.proof_of_shift: Agrees to Initial Demo with named senior finance stakeholder.
meeting_types.run_of_show: 0-1 min: Set agenda & success criteria | 1-10 min: Hypothesis of pain & discovery Qs | 10-20 min: Deep-dive on top pain, quantify impact | 20-25 min: 8-slide value teaser | 25-30 min: Confirm next step — book Initial Demo live on call

stages.stage_goal: Earn the right to diagnose. Set a collaborative frame — not a pitch. Make the buyer feel heard before you say anything about yourself.
stages.current_buyer_belief: I took this meeting because someone asked me to, or I was curious. I expect a vendor pitch. I'll decide in the first two minutes whether to give this person my real attention.
stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer hasn't articulated a problem yet. They assume their fund operations are adequate because no one has given them a reason to question it. The friction is distributed — no single person feels the full weight.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'I'm here to listen to a pitch' to 'This person is asking good questions — there might be something worth examining in how we operate.'
stages.why_change_frame: Most firms haven't re-examined their fund operations stack in years. The tools haven't changed, but the demands have — more funds, more LPs, more complexity, more regulatory pressure. The gap between what the tools can do and what the business needs has widened quietly.
stages.why_now_frame: Something triggered this meeting. A referral, a fundraise, a team change, a frustration. Surface the trigger in the first 90 seconds. If there's no trigger, create intellectual curiosity: firms with similar complexity are discovering structural risks they didn't know they had.
stages.why_you_frame: Don't position the product. Position the conversation. Most vendors pitch features in the first five minutes. This call diagnoses first. The credibility comes from asking better questions than the buyer has heard before.

opening_statements.text: Appreciate you making the time. Here's how I'd like to use the next 30 minutes: a few questions about how your team handles fund operations today, a quick perspective on what we're seeing across the market, and — if it makes sense — we'll talk about what a next step looks like. Sound fair?

Quick context so you know where we fit: we're the performance book of record for private fund managers. Everything between the GL and the investor — waterfalls, carry, distributions, scenario modeling. Most firms handle that layer in Excel. We replace that layer. I don't want to walk you through a bunch of slides — instead I have a few questions about how your team operates and we'll see if there's something worth looking at.
I want to make this about your setup, not ours. The more I understand how your team works today, the more useful I can be — even if we're not the right fit.

I've been meeting with firms like yours and there's a general theme I see: theme one is oversight — checking the administrator's numbers. Theme two is data availability — owning your own data and the efficiency of having it organized. I'm curious where you fall between those two spectrums.

reframing_lines.text: Most firms we talk to haven't examined their fund operations stack in five-plus years. The market has changed faster than the tools.
The friction is usually distributed — no single person feels the full weight, so it never becomes urgent enough to fix. Until it does.
We wanted to have this call first because our system is broad — I don't want to show you everything when only part of it is relevant. A few questions about your setup tells us both where to focus.

likely_objections.objection: Can you just send me a deck?
likely_objections.context: Buyer wants to skip the conversation. Low engagement signal.
likely_objections.response: Happy to. Five minutes of context from you will make the materials ten times more relevant. What's your team running for fund admin and GL today?
likely_objections.what_happens_next: If they answer, you're in discovery. If they resist, ask what prompted the meeting — reset the frame around their trigger.

likely_objections.objection: We're just exploring — very early stage.
likely_objections.context: Low commitment. Buyer isn't sure this meeting is worth their time.
likely_objections.response: Perfect — early stage is exactly when this conversation is most useful. No agenda other than understanding how your team works and whether there's something worth looking at.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Transition to discovery with a light question: 'Quick lay of the land — who handles waterfall calcs on your team today?'

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer is distracted or checking out in the first minute
objection_handling_paths.path: Pattern-interrupt: 'Before we go further — what specifically made you take this meeting? I want to make sure I'm focused on what matters to you.' This forces engagement and gives you their real reason.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Buyers check out when they sense a pitch. Asking about their trigger puts them in the driver's seat.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer tries to flip into 'tell me about your product'
objection_handling_paths.path: Redirect: 'I want to show you the right thing, not everything. Three quick questions about your setup, then we focus on what's relevant.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Establishes that this is a diagnostic conversation, not a feature tour. Sets the discovery precedent.

proof_points.text: Firms from $2B to $200B+ AUM across PE, RE, credit, and venture are having this exact conversation right now.
Most teams we work with said 'things are fine' on the first call. By the third question, they were describing risks they hadn't articulated before.

next_step_language.text: Let me ask a few questions about how your team is set up — that'll tell us both whether there's something worth digging into.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer signals low engagement or wants to shortcut the conversation
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Between oversight of your calculations and speed of getting data to your team — which one is more top of mind for you right now?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer gives context about why they took the meeting
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Transition to m1-advancing discovery
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: That's helpful context. Let me ask a few questions about how your team is set up today.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer tries to flip into product discussion too early
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-002
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: I want to show you the right thing — not everything. A few questions about your setup first so we focus on what's relevant.

predictive_queue.reason: The two-thread diagnostic opener (oversight vs data availability) immediately segments the buyer and frames the conversation around their world rather than your product. Fires when the buyer's initial tone is neutral or waiting. Disrupts the pitch expectation more sharply than a generic market reframe.

Open by disrupting the expected pitch. The Corporate Visions 'Why Change' frame must land before any discovery happens. If the buyer expects a product walkthrough, reframe to a conversation about their world.
After the frame is set, transition to the first discovery question. The two-thread model starts here: thread 1 is workflow, thread 2 is organizational risk.
If the open runs long, prompt the rep to transition to advancing stage.

cost_of_inaction_frames.trigger: Prospect says everything is fine / no urgency expressed
cost_of_inaction_frames.reframe: Most firms we talk to say the same thing on the first call. And they're usually right in the moment. The risk isn't that something is broken today. It's that the process was designed for a smaller, simpler version of the firm. The firms that feel most confident are usually the ones that haven't had the event yet: one LP dispute, one key person leaving, one complex fund launch. We're not here to create urgency. We're here to help you see what's changed underneath the surface.

That's actually common. The pain in fund operations isn't loud. It's distributed across people and spreadsheets so no single person feels the full weight. What we've found is that the problem only becomes visible when you zoom out: total hours spent on quarter-end reconciliation, how many people can reproduce the waterfall logic independently, what happens when an LP asks a question nobody anticipated. The fact that it doesn't feel like a problem is often the problem.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: I've been meeting with firms at your size and there are two themes that come up: one is oversight — making sure the numbers from the admin or the internal models are defensible. Two is data availability — your deal team or leadership needs answers and finance can't turn them around fast enough. I'm curious which one resonates more with how your team operates.
risk_identification_prompts.why: Gives the client two recognizable scenarios they can self-select into without needing to understand the product. Works on outbound where the client has no context. The two themes map to the core value props and tell the rep which discovery thread to follow.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Always open with trigger. Sets the entire frame.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: How many funds are you running right now, and what does your next 18 months look like in terms of launches?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Surfaces scaling pressure. A firm launching a new fund in 12 months has a timeline attached to their problem.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Scoping question. Must know fund count and trajectory before any discovery.

economic_buyer_alignment.scenario: EB sent a subordinate to take the meeting
economic_buyer_alignment.approach: The EB knows something the champion may not. Surface the trigger immediately: 'What made [CFO name] suggest this meeting?' The answer reveals the EB's hypothesis. If the champion doesn't know, that's data too: the EB has a concern they haven't shared with the team. Your job in M1-open is to validate the EB's instinct by finding evidence in the champion's world. End the call with ammunition the champion can carry back to confirm the EB was right to send them.

stages.stage_goal: Create the belief that the buyer has a structural risk they haven't fully examined. The shift happens when the buyer articulates a fragility in their own words — not when you tell them one exists. Use the pain ladder: surface → impact → personal.
stages.current_buyer_belief: Our process works. It's not elegant, but it gets the job done. We've handled quarter-end, we've handled audits, we've handled distributions. If it were really broken, we'd know.
stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer sees fund operations as manageable — inconvenient at times, but not strategically costly. They don't see key person dependency as a structural risk. They don't see admin validation burden as redundant work. They don't see Excel as a fragility — they see it as flexibility. The cost of the status quo is hidden because the friction is distributed across people, processes, and quarters.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'our process works fine' to 'I have a structural risk I haven't fully examined.' The proof is when the buyer says something like: 'Yeah, if [person] left, that would be a real problem' or 'We've actually caught errors in the admin's numbers before.'
stages.why_change_frame: The issue isn't whether the current process works — it clearly does. The issue is whether it scales cleanly when fund count doubles, when LPs demand more granular reporting, when the person who built the Excel models eventually leaves, and when auditors ask for traceability that doesn't exist. The cost of the status quo is hidden because the friction is distributed. Nobody owns the full problem, so nobody escalates it.
stages.why_now_frame: Key person risk isn't theoretical — teams lose their lead fund accountant mid-quarter. Fund admin errors aren't hypothetical — six-figure discrepancies surface on real distributions. The trigger isn't a crisis. The trigger is recognizing the risk before it materializes. Every quarter of delay is another cycle where institutional knowledge accrues in spreadsheets that nobody else can maintain.
stages.why_you_frame: Don't sell the product at this stage. Sell the category: the performance book of record. Every fund has an accounting book of record — the GL. Ask what their performance book of record is. If the answer is Excel, that's a process held together by people, not a system with traceability. That distinction is the insight that reframes the entire conversation.

reframing_lines.text: Every fund has an accounting book of record. The question is: what's your performance book of record? If the answer is Excel, that's a process, not a system.
What we usually see is that teams tolerate this longer than they should — because the friction is distributed across people and quarters. Nobody owns the full problem.
The real cost shows up after the information has already been gathered — in the reconciliation, the validation, the shadow work nobody budgeted for.
Fund admins play an important role. The question is which services should be handled by software versus humans — and who checks the checker.
LP demands will keep increasing as private markets try to match public market infrastructure. The pipes are broken at the GP level.
Finance teams can end up at 20% of the firm's headcount if technology doesn't absorb the incremental complexity from new funds and investors.
The question isn't whether Excel works. It's whether it's defensible, scalable, and not dependent on one person.
The opposite of control is acceptance. If you don't own your own logic, you kind of just have to accept what the admin provides. And they won't take fiduciary responsibility on those numbers.
The whole point of getting an admin is to have less people — but most firms end up stacking more people on top to validate what the admin sends.

Nobody wants to change accounting systems. It happens once a decade. We sit in between whatever version of the GL exists and bring the fund to a modern workflow without displacing the accounting engine.
You've got an octopus of systems — plenty of tentacles. The question is how you get intelligence out of that without five people gluing it together in Excel every quarter.

Fund complexity outgrows spreadsheets. When it does, someone builds an internal system — and that system is rigid. The next time the fund structure changes, the team has to hack the system to make it work. That cycle repeats with every new fund.

We sit between the GL and the finance team — a middle layer that complements both. We don't replace the general ledger, we don't replace the investor portal, and we don't replace the finance team. We make all three more efficient.

discovery_questions.question: Walk me through who actually owns the waterfall logic today — and what happens when that person is unavailable for a quarter-end.
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces key person risk and process fragility in a single question.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Ask this after the 'performance book of record' reframe. Let them describe the dependency themselves.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Waterfall ownership is THE discovery question. Surfaces key person risk and process fragility.

discovery_questions.question: When an LP asks you to defend a specific calculation, what does the process look like to trace it back to the source?
discovery_questions.purpose: Exposes auditability gaps and traceability issues.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If they hesitate, the gap is real. Don't fill the silence.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Fire when LP scrutiny or audit risk is signaled. Skip if buyer has < 20 LPs.

discovery_questions.question: If I asked your team to show me every manual override made in the last distribution cycle, how quickly could they produce that?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests process maturity and auditability. Most teams can't answer this.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: The answer is almost always 'we couldn't.' That's the moment.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Manual override question. Devastating when it lands but requires the buyer to already be open about process gaps.

discovery_questions.question: How many of your fund structures have side-letter economics that live outside your primary Excel model?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces hidden complexity invisible to leadership.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Side letters are the complexity that breaks templates. Use this to bridge to the 'purpose-built' concept.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Side letters are the complexity multiplier. Differentiates serious operational pain from simple setups.

discovery_questions.question: What would happen to your next distribution cycle if your lead fund accountant left tomorrow?
discovery_questions.purpose: Key person risk. The most emotionally resonant question. Hits Level 3 on the pain ladder.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Slow down when asking this. Let the weight of it land. If they name a specific person, write the name down — that's your champion's boss or peer.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Key person risk. Most emotionally resonant question. Lands in every scenario.

discovery_questions.question: When you think about scaling from your current fund count to double that, where does the process start to feel fragile?
discovery_questions.purpose: Connects current pain to future risk. Creates urgency without being pushy.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Future projection questions bypass the 'we're fine' defense. They can't argue with a scenario they haven't tested.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Scaling question. Fire when buyer mentions fund growth, new launches, or hiring pressure.

discovery_questions.question: Has anyone on the leadership team ever raised concerns about the defensibility of your waterfall outputs to auditors or LPs?
discovery_questions.purpose: Elevates the conversation from operational to strategic. Tests whether pain has reached leadership.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If yes — you have a path to the EB. If no — the pain hasn't been escalated yet, and you may need to help them frame it.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: EB detection question. Tells you whether pain has reached leadership. Critical for M2 framing.

discovery_questions.question: How would you describe your relationship with your fund admin — what's working and what isn't?
discovery_questions.purpose: Opens the admin tension naturally. Don't lead negative.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Let them tell you what's working first. The 'what isn't' part opens the maker-checker conversation.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Admin relationship question. ONLY fire when buyer uses an external fund admin. Skip entirely for in-house operations.

discovery_questions.question: How long does your distribution process typically take end-to-end?
discovery_questions.purpose: Quantifies the operational burden. Sets up ROI.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Anchor the number. You'll use it in the Value Creation workshop later.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Quantifies the operational burden. The number you get here becomes the ROI anchor for the entire deal.

discovery_questions.question: Have you ever caught errors in your admin's calculations?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces admin trust issues. If yes, major pain amplifier.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If yes, follow up: 'How did you catch it? What would have happened if you hadn't?' This escalates from anecdote to systemic risk.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Admin error question. ONLY fire when buyer uses an external admin. Most powerful when buyer already trusts the admin — the error story creates cognitive dissonance.

discovery_questions.question: On a scale of 1-10, how would you prioritize fixing those issues?
discovery_questions.purpose: Forces them to commit to a priority level.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Only ask after they've articulated real pain. If they say 7+, the deal is real.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Commitment test. Fire AFTER buyer has articulated real pain (3+ pain points surfaced). Premature if asked before pain is established.

discovery_questions.question: If you fast-forward six quarters, what part of your current process worries you most?
discovery_questions.purpose: Future-state anxiety. Connects today's tolerable friction to tomorrow's risk.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: This closes the discovery phase. Whatever they say here is the narrative you carry into the close.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Future anxiety question. Closes the discovery phase. Whatever they say here becomes the narrative for the deal.

discovery_questions.question: How do you think about budgeting for something like this — you have a working process, time is a pressure point, but it's not broken. What's the mindset internally on investing in this pocket of the business?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces how the EB thinks about ROI before you present pricing. Learns their internal justification framework.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: CRITICAL: Ask this whenever the buyer indicates a senior leader sent them. The answer maps the funding path before M2. Listen for: 'cost benefit analysis,' 'fund expense,' 'management company budget,' 'need board approval.' Each one tells you a different decision process.

fire_when: buyer mentions CFO, COO, MD, or GP asked them to take the meeting
buyer uses words: referred, asked me to, told me to look at, wanted me to evaluate
trigger_conditions.rationale: When an EB refers a subordinate to evaluate, budget authority exists one level up. This question must be asked before booking M2 so you know whether the EB is showing up with budget intent or just curiosity. Skipping it means walking into M2 blind on funding path.

discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Budget framing. MUST fire when EB referred the champion. Otherwise situational based on budget signals.

discovery_questions.question: When the product or deal team needs performance data — capital call percentage, inception-to-date IRR, deployment levels — how fast can the finance team turn that around?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces the decision latency pain. If the answer is 'days,' data accessibility is the real problem.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: This is the data accessibility play. Rob at Golub described sending requests to finance and waiting — 'that part drives me nuts.' If the buyer describes a similar pattern, this is the core value proposition.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Data accessibility play. Fire when buyer has a deal/portfolio team separate from finance. Skip for small teams where everyone wears multiple hats.

likely_objections.objection: Excel works fine for us.
likely_objections.context: Status quo defense. Buyer hasn't been stress-tested.
likely_objections.response: It does — until it doesn't. The question isn't whether it works today. It's what happens when fund count doubles, when the model builder leaves, or when LPs ask for data that takes days to produce.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Follow with the scaling question: 'If you doubled fund count in 18 months, where does it feel fragile?'

likely_objections.objection: Our admin handles all of this.
likely_objections.context: Buyer sees admin as the solution.
likely_objections.response: They handle the calculation. Who checks it? When they get it wrong on a distribution — and we've seen six-figure errors — the admin isn't liable. The fund is.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Follow with: 'How does your team validate the admin's output before distributions go out?'

likely_objections.objection: We already have a system for that.
likely_objections.context: Conflating GL with performance book of record.
likely_objections.response: Most firms have an accounting system — the GL. The question is what handles investor-level waterfalls, fees, carried interest. If that's Excel, that's a process, not a system.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask: 'When you need investor-level performance output, where does that calculation actually happen?'

likely_objections.objection: We're too small for this.
likely_objections.response: At $2B+ with institutional LPs, there's already meaningful complexity — custom fee arrangements, side letters, ILPA reporting. It only grows with each new fund.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask about fund count trajectory: 'How many funds are you expecting in the next 18-24 months?'

likely_objections.objection: The CFO is skeptical of new software.
likely_objections.response: Most CFOs are skeptical — they should be. The best approach is getting them in front of it with their hardest waterfall. If it handles it, they'll know. If it can't, you'll know too.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Capture the CFO's name: 'Who specifically would need to see this?'

likely_objections.objection: What happens if your company goes away?
likely_objections.response: Once data is structured, it's exportable and not dependent on any individual. Unlike Excel where knowledge is trapped in one person's head, a system makes it explicit and portable.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Reframe the risk: 'The bigger portability question is what happens to the knowledge in your current Excel models if the person who built them leaves.'

likely_objections.objection: Timing isn't right.
likely_objections.response: Best time to implement is between fund closes. If you wait until the next fund, the team will be stretched. Most firms wish they'd started a quarter earlier.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask: 'Is there a fund raise or audit coming up that would create a natural deadline?'

likely_objections.objection: We're happy with how things work.
likely_objections.response: That's what most teams say before seeing the alternative. The question isn't whether it works. It's what happens at scale, when people leave, or when LPs ask for data that takes days.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Try the future projection: 'If you fast-forward six quarters, what part of today's process worries you most?'

likely_objections.objection: We're in the middle of fundraising.
likely_objections.response: That's usually when pain is highest — team stretched between operations and fundraising. Materials can be shared now, deeper dive after close.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Get a specific date: 'When are you targeting the close? Let me put something on the calendar for right after.'

likely_objections.objection: We just implemented something new.
likely_objections.response: If the new system is a GL or portal, a performance book of record completes the stack. The timing might be ideal while the team is in improvement mode.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask: 'What did you implement — GL, portal, CRM? That tells me where the gap might be.'

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer says 'we're fine' before any real discovery
objection_handling_paths.path: Don't challenge directly. Use the future projection: 'If you doubled fund count in the next 18 months, where does the process start to feel fragile?' This bypasses the 'fine' defense by projecting into a scenario they haven't tested.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Nobody can argue with a hypothetical future. It shifts the frame from 'is it broken now' to 'will it scale.'

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer deflects to the fund admin
objection_handling_paths.path: Acknowledge, then surface the checker problem: 'The admin does the calc. Who checks it? And what happens when the checker's Excel model disagrees with the admin's output?' Reframes admin dependency as structural risk.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Moves from 'the admin handles it' to 'but who handles the admin.' Introduces the maker-checker concept without attacking the admin relationship.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions a specific person by name who owns the models
objection_handling_paths.path: This is the moment. Slow down. 'What happens to the next distribution cycle if [Name] is unavailable?' Write the name down. Let the silence work. They'll articulate the risk themselves.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Named key person risk is visceral. The buyer feels it. Don't rush past it — let the weight land.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions a recent error or near-miss with the admin
objection_handling_paths.path: Go deeper: 'How did you catch it? What would have happened if you hadn't?' This moves from anecdote to systemic risk. Then: 'How often does that happen — and are you catching every one?'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Escalates a story into a pattern. One error is a mistake. A pattern is a structural problem.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer asks about pricing too early
objection_handling_paths.path: Defer without dodging: 'Basis points on NAV plus unfunded — and most firms pass it through as an LP expense. But pricing is more meaningful after you see what it does. Can I ask a few more questions about your setup first?'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Gives a real answer (doesn't dodge) but redirects to discovery where the value case is built.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer describes a complex multi-system environment
objection_handling_paths.path: Map it back to them: 'So let me paraphrase — you've got [system A] for accounting, [system B] for portfolio, and Excel connecting them. And the people who hold it together are your key risk.' This shows you understood their complexity and positions the platform as the connective layer.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Buyers with complex environments feel nobody understands them. Paraphrasing their stack accurately builds instant credibility.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer has a dedicated outsourced team (BPO / lift-out)
objection_handling_paths.path: Don't position against the outsourced team — that's a bigger fight than you need. Instead: 'That dedicated team is an asset. The question is what happens if key people on that team leave — and whether their work is trapped in their heads or in a system you control.' Position around the outsourced team's key person risk.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: The buyer chose to outsource for a reason. Attacking the model attacks their judgment. Attacking the risk within the model is safe and resonant.

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Carta
competitor_response_paths.frame: Acknowledge Carta's lane, surface the complexity ceiling.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Carta is strong for venture with pro rata structures. When complexity increases — deal-by-deal waterfalls, catch-ups, clawbacks, side letter variations — firms find they need purpose-built depth.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: What are the most complex waterfall structures across your fund family?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Juniper Square
competitor_response_paths.frame: Complementary positioning. Never compete on portal.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Roughly a third of similar clients use both. JSQ handles portal and CRM. The calculation engine is a separate layer.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: Are you using JSQ primarily for the portal, or relying on it for calculations?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Allvue / Investran
competitor_response_paths.frame: GL vs. performance book of record. Complementary.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Those are strong general ledgers. The gap is at the investor level — waterfalls, carry, scenario modeling. That's where Excel lives.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: When you need investor-level waterfall output, where does that calculation happen?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions admin platform (SS&C, SEI, Geneva)
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: That's the admin's system. The fund's visibility is limited to whatever gets exported. An independent system of record gives the fund direct control.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: How much visibility do you have into how your admin arrives at the numbers they send?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Yardi
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Yardi handles property-level accounting. The gap is fund-level — waterfalls, promotes, co-invest allocations, feeder/blocker rollups.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: After data comes out of Yardi, where do fund-level calculations happen?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions building internally
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Internal builds work with engineering resources. The question is whether that time is better spent on things that differentiate the firm versus maintaining waterfall logic.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: If the tech team didn't maintain fund calc tools, what would they build?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Anaplan
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Powerful for FP&A. Configuring it for fund accounting requires significant consulting and ongoing internal maintenance. Purpose-built platforms handle LPA terms natively.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: Has the team scoped what an Anaplan implementation would require?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Chronograph / iLevel
competitor_response_paths.frame: Complementary. Portfolio management vs. fund performance.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Portfolio management tools are strong at deal-level data. They don't do investor-level performance or waterfall calculations. Complementary.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: How does your portfolio data connect to investor-level reporting today?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions Quantrix or a niche waterfall/allocation tool
competitor_response_paths.frame: Acknowledge the tool, then surface the scope gap.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: Quantrix handles the waterfall calculation. The question is whether it also handles capital calls, distributions, management fees, investor-level allocations, admin reconciliation, and LP reporting — or whether those are still separate processes in Excel.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: Would you classify that as your waterfall engine, your allocation engine, or both?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer mentions a proprietary internal system built years ago
competitor_response_paths.frame: Acknowledge the investment. Surface the rigidity problem.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: That proprietary system was built for the fund structures that existed when it was designed. The question is what happens when the fund structure changes — new waterfall types, new fee models, new investor terms. If the answer is 'we have to hack it to make it work,' the system has become a constraint rather than an enabler.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: When was the last time the team had to modify that system to accommodate a new structure — and how long did it take?

proof_points.text: One client found a six-figure error on a single distribution in their first quarter using the platform. The admin wasn't liable — the fund was.
A $5B+ real estate manager reduced quarter-end close from three weeks to days. Controller changed how they think about hiring.
One firm's lead model builder for 15 funds gave notice. CFO called in a panic. Not an edge case — we see it constantly.
After six months on the platform, one CFO told their admin: just book what's in the system. Stopped shadowing in Excel entirely.
A $16B+ VC's CFO described back office as 'getting out of the nineties.' Chose the platform because it doesn't force outsourcing.

A $40B+ institutional real estate manager had a dedicated team at their admin that solely served their funds. When Riley asked about key person risk on that team, the response was: 'That would definitely impact the timing.' The risk isn't the admin — it's the people inside the admin.

trigger_conditions.linked_content: rf-004 — 'Every fund has an accounting book of record. The question is: what's your performance book of record?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: If you doubled fund count in the next 18 months, where does the process start to feel fragile?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions manual process or Excel
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-003 — 'If I asked your team to show me every manual override...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How many people can actually maintain those models — and what happens if they're unavailable?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions fund admin
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-008 — 'How would you describe your relationship with your fund admin — what's working and what isn't?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Do they handle the waterfall calculations, or does your team still do that in-house?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions a specific competitor or alternative
trigger_conditions.linked_content: competitor_response_paths — match by competitor name
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What's been your experience with them — and what's still in Excel on top of it?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions a specific person who owns the process
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-005 — 'What would happen to your next distribution cycle if your lead fund accountant left tomorrow?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What happens if that person is unavailable for a quarter-end cycle?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions quarter-end, audit, or reconciliation pain
trigger_phrases: lost count of how many tools
every system has its own version

trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-009 — 'How long does your distribution process typically take end-to-end?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How many person-hours does a typical quarter-end take — and how many people are involved?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions errors or near-misses
trigger_conditions.linked_content: pp-003 — 'One client found a six-figure error on a single distribution...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How did you catch it — and what would have happened if you hadn't?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions scaling, growth, or new funds
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-006 — 'When you think about scaling from your current fund count to double that...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: When fund count doubles, does the process scale linearly — or does complexity compound?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions LP pressure, reporting, or investor demands
trigger_conditions.linked_content: rf-008 — 'LP demands will keep increasing as private markets try to match public market infrastructure...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: When an LP asks for a specific number, how long does it take your team to produce it?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer asks about pricing or cost too early
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-007 — defer pricing, redirect to discovery
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Basis points on NAV plus unfunded — and most firms pass it through as an LP expense. Pricing is more meaningful after you see what it does. Can I ask a few more questions first?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions building something internally
trigger_conditions.linked_content: competitor_response_paths — build vs. buy trigger
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How much of your tech team's time goes to maintaining it versus building new capabilities?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions skepticism or past bad experience
trigger_conditions.linked_content: likely_objections — match by context
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What specifically went wrong in that experience — and what would make this evaluation different?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer shows interest in moving forward
trigger_phrases: my CFO would want to see
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Transition to m1-confirming stage
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Who on your team would need to be in the room for a demo to be productive — and what does the calendar look like?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer describes data spread across multiple systems with no single source
trigger_conditions.linked_content: reframing_lines — 'octopus of systems' reframe
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: You've got this octopus of systems with plenty of tentacles. The question is how you get intelligence out of that without five people gluing it together every quarter.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions outsourced team or BPO arrangement
trigger_conditions.linked_content: objection_handling_paths — BPO key person risk path
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How many of the original people who were lifted out are still there — and what happens when they leave?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions being constrained in product design by operational limitations
trigger_conditions.linked_content: The cost isn't just efficiency — it's revenue left on the table because the infrastructure can't support the product.
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Has there ever been a fee structure or investor term you wanted to offer but couldn't because of the operational complexity it would create?

predictive_queue.reason: Primary move: ask the next unasked discovery question. MEDDPICC pain is the dominant tag in this stage (13 of 14 DQs). Keep diagnosing until the buyer has described at least 3 specific pain points.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked advancing question, prioritized by priority_scoring engine

predictive_queue.reason: If pain is well-covered (3+ pain questions asked), surface a question that addresses an uncovered MEDDPICC element — metrics, economic_buyer, or decision_process. The scoring engine's meddpicc_relevance dimension handles this automatically.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked question where methodology_tags addresses an uncovered MEDDPICC element

predictive_queue.reason: If the buyer hasn't described their end-to-end workflow yet, surface a question tagged workflow_fragility. This is the richest discovery vein in M1.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked question with conversation_signals including manual_process, workflow_fragility, or shadow_models

predictive_queue.reason: After every 2-3 discovery questions, surface a reframing line. Top reps alternate between asking and reframing. If the buyer has answered 3+ questions describing a 'fine' process, reframe to surface hidden friction.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: reframing_lines — match to the last answered question's principle_tags

predictive_queue.reason: After a reframe lands and the buyer signals recognition, a credibility story makes the pain real. Deploy sparingly — one per advancing stage is ideal.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: customer_story_bank — match by pain theme. Also check canonical_conversation_assets for vendor-specific credibility stories if a vendor has been mentioned.

predictive_queue.reason: Competitors surface in ~40% of M1 calls. If no competitor has been mentioned yet, this drops to lowest priority. If one has, the trigger layer handles it.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: competitor_response_paths — match by name. Vendor asset integration (Step 4a) enriches with canonical assets.

predictive_queue.reason: Proof points validate the reframe. Most effective after a reframe lands and the buyer signals recognition. Deploy sparingly — 1-2 per advancing stage.
Transition to confirming when buyer shows interest signals — leaning in, asking about next steps, naming stakeholders. Don't rush. Premature closing loses more deals than slow closing.

cost_of_inaction_frames.reframe: Every firm reconciles manually at some level. The question is how many hours that costs per quarter and what happens to that number when fund count or LP count doubles. We had a client running three funds who told us reconciliation took two days. When they launched Fund IV with side letters, it took eleven days. Same team. Same tools. Different math.

The model working isn't the same as the model being transferable. What you've built in that person's head is institutional knowledge that lives outside the system. The fund owns the capital, the LPs, the legal obligations, but the logic that governs distributions lives in one person's Excel file. That's not a process. That's a single point of failure dressed up as expertise.

Excel is brilliant for what it was designed to do. The issue is that fund economics have outgrown what a spreadsheet can safely govern. Side letters, multi-tier waterfalls, cross-fund netting, LP-level fee breaks: each one adds a layer of logic that Excel handles but can't audit, can't version-control, and can't hand off. The spreadsheet becomes the system of record by default, not by design. That's where the risk concentrates.

If an LP or auditor asks you to reproduce a specific calculation from two quarters ago, including every override and assumption, how long does that take? Most firms tell us the answer is somewhere between half a day and we honestly aren't sure we could. That gap between the output and the audit trail is the liability. It doesn't show up until someone asks, and when they ask, speed matters.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: If [key person] wasn't available for a month, what would break?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Key-person risk question. Forces the prospect to name the dependency out loud. Most can't answer it comfortably.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Variant of dq-005. Use when you know the name — personalizes the risk.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: Who would step in — and how long before they could run a distribution independently?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Quantify the ramp time for a replacement
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: What would that cost you — in delayed distributions, overtime, or a rushed hire?
risk_identification_prompts.l3_goal: Put a dollar figure on the key person gap

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: Walk me through what happens the day after a distribution goes out. Who checks what?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Surfaces the validation layer — or absence of one. If the answer is 'we reconcile against the admin,' you've found the friction.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Process mapping question. Rich but time-consuming. Use when buyer is operationally detailed and engaged.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: How many people touch that process, and where do the handoffs slow down?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Map the bottlenecks in the post-distribution workflow
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: If an LP called the next day and challenged a number, how fast could you trace it back?

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: When was the last time an LP asked a question about their distribution calculation that took longer than an hour to answer?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Grounds abstract risk in a real incident. If they can name one, it becomes a proof point for urgency.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: LP scrutiny probe. Fire when LP count > 30 or buyer mentions LP reporting pressure.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: What did the process look like to get them the answer — who was involved and how long?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Quantify the LP inquiry burden in people and hours
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: How did the LP react to the turnaround time — and has that affected the relationship?
risk_identification_prompts.l3_goal: Connect data latency to LP trust

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: How long does audit prep take your team right now?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Quantifies time cost. Gets them to say a number out loud, which you can reference when discussing ROI.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Audit pain quantifier. Fire when buyer mentions auditors, SOC compliance, or recent audit issues.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: What percentage of that time is gathering data versus actually reviewing it?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Separate data collection burden from analysis burden
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: If audit prep was two days instead of two weeks, what would your team do with that time?

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: What happens to that process when you launch Fund V?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Forces them to project current pain forward. They usually realize the answer is 'it gets worse.'
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Scaling trigger. Fire when new fund is confirmed or imminent.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: Who else on your team fully understands how the waterfall logic works end to end?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Surfaces knowledge concentration. If the answer is one or two people, that's the key-person risk story.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Key person depth. Second angle on dq-005. Quantifies the bench depth.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: If you asked them to reproduce a waterfall calc from scratch without help, could they?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Test whether bench depth is real or theoretical
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: What happens to your quarter-end if both of you are unavailable at the same time?
risk_identification_prompts.l3_goal: Escalate from single point of failure to no-coverage scenario

economic_buyer_alignment.scenario: Champion is engaged but EB is invisible
economic_buyer_alignment.approach: The champion is describing real pain but hasn't connected it to an executive priority. Your job is to help them frame the problem in EB language. After surfacing key-person risk or audit exposure, ask: 'Has anyone on the leadership team raised this concern?' If no, plant the seed: 'The firms where this moves forward are usually the ones where the CFO or COO has already been thinking about operational risk. Would it be worth raising this with Maria before we schedule the next step?' You're giving the champion a reason to escalate.

economic_buyer_alignment.scenario: Champion mentions budget constraints or competing priorities
economic_buyer_alignment.approach: Budget language from a non-EB is a proxy for 'I don't have authority.' Don't negotiate with the champion on price or priority. Instead, redirect: 'How does your team typically evaluate investments like this? Is it a fund expense, a management company G&A line, or something that needs board-level approval?' The answer maps the decision process and tells you whether the EB is the CFO, the GP, or a fund governance committee. Then ask dq-013 (budget framing question) to understand how the EB thinks about ROI before you're in the room with them.

objection_recovery_protocol.description: When objection handling consumes significant call time (5+ minutes on a single objection), the brain must triage remaining questions. This protocol defines which questions are non-negotiable even under time pressure, and which can be sacrificed.

when_objection_consumed_5_plus_minutes.never_skip_rationale: These 9 questions are the spine of M1. Waterfall ownership, key person risk, cycle time quantification, future anxiety, belief shift test, champion test, M2 ask, stakeholder mapping, and calendar close. A rep who hits only these 9 has a viable M1.
when_objection_consumed_5_plus_minutes.drop_first_rationale: Manual overrides, data accessibility, and 1-10 scale are depth questions. They enrich but dont gate.

compress.dq-004_and_dq-006: Combine side letters + scaling into one question: "How many of your funds have custom LP economics, and what happens to that complexity when you launch the next fund?"
compress.dq-015_and_dq-017: Combine AUM/team size + fund raise into one: "We have you at $X AUM with Y funds. Are you currently raising?"

minimum_viable_m1.note: If the call is running hot with objections, hitting these 9 in this order produces a viable M1 with key person risk surfaced, cycle time quantified, belief shift tested, champion qualified, and M2 booked.

stages.stage_goal: Verify the belief shift landed. Capture specific intel: AUM, fund types, team size, stakeholder names. Secure commitment for an Initial Demo with a named senior finance stakeholder — on the calendar, with a date.
stages.current_buyer_belief: That was a good conversation. There might be something here. But I'm not sure it's worth pulling my CFO or controller into a meeting yet. I need to think about it.
stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer now sees a structural risk worth examining — but hasn't committed to doing anything about it. The gap between 'interesting' and 'action' is bridged by a low-risk, high-value next step that feels like progress, not a purchase commitment.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'that's interesting' to 'I should get my CFO / controller in front of this.' Confirmed when they name a specific person and agree to a specific date.
stages.why_change_frame: The structural risks surfaced in discovery won't resolve themselves. The question is whether the buyer wants to examine them with the right people in the room — or go back to the process they know and hope nothing breaks.
stages.why_now_frame: The context from this conversation is freshest in the next two weeks. Getting the right people into a demo now means one efficient meeting. Waiting means re-explaining everything from scratch when the context has faded.
stages.why_you_frame: The demo is built around firms with similar complexity. The people who use these tools day to day will have better questions than either of us. Getting them in the room is what makes the next conversation productive — not more slides.

reframing_lines.text: The people who actually use the system day to day will have much better questions than either of us. Getting them in the room is the real unlock.
A quick overview followed by a live demo gives the team both the 'why' and the 'how' in one 45-minute session.
Firms that take the demo with their finance team move faster because the right people assess fit directly — no game of telephone.
I wanted to have this call first because the system is broad — now I know what to show and what to skip. That makes the demo 10x more relevant.

discovery_questions.question: We have you at roughly X dollars AUM — how large is the finance and accounting team that supports this?
discovery_questions.purpose: Quantifies operational footprint. Sets up efficiency conversation.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Use their actual AUM from Preqin. Shows you did homework.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: AUM + team size ratio. Shows you did homework and sets up the efficiency conversation.

discovery_questions.question: Our understanding is you're running closed-end funds — is that the full picture, or do you have open-ended vehicles too?
discovery_questions.purpose: Confirms fund type scope and product fit.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If open-ended: be honest about current roadmap. If closed-end only: green light.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Product fit. Fire when fund type is ambiguous. Skip when ICP research already confirms closed-end only.

discovery_questions.question: Are you currently raising a new fund?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies timing trigger. New fund = best implementation window.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: A fund raise in progress means stretched bandwidth but high urgency. Time the next step accordingly.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Timing trigger. New fund = best implementation window AND best urgency lever.

discovery_questions.question: Based on what we've discussed, it sounds like there are a few structural risks worth examining more closely — would you agree?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests whether the belief shift landed. If they agree, you've earned the right to propose next steps.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: This is the single most important confirming question. If they say yes, go to close. If they hedge, go back to discovery.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Belief shift test. THE gating question before any close attempt. If they dont agree, go back to discovery.

discovery_questions.question: When you go back to Maria after this call, how would you describe what we talked about?
discovery_questions.purpose: Champion litmus test. If they can articulate the pain in their own words, they're a champion. If they say 'I'll forward the recording' or 'I'll tell her it was interesting,' they're a scheduler and M2 is at risk.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Ask this before proposing the M2 attendee list. Their answer tells you whether to coach them on framing before the next meeting. If they struggle, offer: 'Would it help if I sent you a one-paragraph summary you could forward?' That gives you control of the narrative.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Champion test. Non-negotiable. Must know if this person will sell internally or just schedule a call.

discovery_questions.question: Have you looked at anything else for this, or is this more exploratory at this point?
discovery_questions.purpose: Competitive landscape awareness. Tells you if you're in a bake-off, a solo evaluation, or an information-gathering exercise. Each requires a different M2 strategy.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Ask casually. If they name competitors, don't react. Write the names down silently. If they say 'just exploratory,' test it: 'What would have to be true for this to move from exploratory to a real evaluation?' That tells you the decision criteria before M2.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Competitive landscape. Must know if you are solo or in a bake-off before M2.

discovery_questions.question: If we could show you how firms with similar complexity have addressed these exact gaps, would it be worth 45 minutes with your CFO or Head of Fund Ops?
discovery_questions.purpose: Proposes the next meeting with a specific stakeholder. Frames as value, not sales.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Name the role specifically. 'Your team' is vague. 'Your CFO' or 'your controller' is a commitment.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: The M2 ask. The entire point of M1 confirming.

discovery_questions.question: Who on your team would need to be in the room for that conversation to be productive?
discovery_questions.purpose: Stakeholder mapping. Starts building the buying committee.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Write down every name. This is your M2 attendee list.
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Stakeholder mapping. Names in the room = committed next step.

discovery_questions.question: What does your calendar look like in the next two weeks to schedule that?
discovery_questions.purpose: Book the meeting live on the call. Don't leave it to email.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If they won't commit to a date live, the deal is already at risk. Push gently: 'Early or late next week?'
discovery_questions.tier_rationale: Calendar close. Book it live. Never leave to email.

likely_objections.objection: I need to talk to the team first.
likely_objections.context: Stall. Buyer wants cover before committing.
likely_objections.response: Totally makes sense. Materials can go out today with context. The demo works best with finance in the room — who specifically would need to be there?
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Turn the stall into stakeholder mapping. Get names, not 'the team.'

likely_objections.objection: Send me some materials and we'll circle back.
likely_objections.response: Happy to send everything. To make the follow-up useful, it helps to have the people who'd actually use it in the room. Can we get something on the calendar in the next two weeks?
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Attach a date to the materials. Without a date, 'circle back' means 'never.'

likely_objections.objection: I'm not sure this is a priority for leadership.
likely_objections.response: What would make it a priority — a fund raise, an audit finding, a team capacity issue? Understanding what leadership cares about helps frame the next conversation around what matters to them.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Surface the compelling event that would make leadership pay attention. Then frame the demo around it.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer agrees but won't name a specific person
objection_handling_paths.path: Push gently: 'Who on the finance team would have the strongest opinion on waterfall accuracy and audit readiness? That person should see this.' A vague 'the team' is not a commitment. A name is.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Forces specificity. Vague commitments evaporate. Named individuals get calendared.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer agrees but won't commit to a date
objection_handling_paths.path: Create urgency without pressure: 'The context from today is freshest in the next week or two. Can we find a slot before [date] so we don't lose the thread?' Book it live.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Two-week window is a natural deadline. Beyond that, the momentum from this call is gone.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer says 'let me think about it'
objection_handling_paths.path: Surface the real concern: 'What would you need to see or know to feel confident bringing your team into the next conversation?' This uncovers the blocker hiding behind 'think about it.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: 'Think about it' is never the real answer. There's always a specific concern underneath. Find it.

proof_points.text: Firms that book the demo within two weeks of the initial call close 3x faster. Momentum from the first call compounds.
One 45-minute demo with the right people in the room replaces three months of email back-and-forth.

next_step_language.text: Here's what makes sense: a quick overview followed by a live demo with your finance team. They get the context and see the product in one shot.
Let me send the materials today. You tee it up internally, we coordinate on getting the right people into a 45-minute session.
Who specifically would need to be there — and what does the calendar look like in the next two weeks?
I've got a really good idea of how to frame the demo now. We probably need about 45 minutes. Your convenience — what does the week of [date] look like?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer agrees there's something worth examining
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-019 — 'If we could show you how firms with similar complexity have addressed these exact gaps...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Who on your team would need to see this — and what does the calendar look like in the next two weeks?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer stalls with 'let me think about it'
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-012 — surface the real concern behind 'think about it'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What would you need to see or know to feel confident bringing your team into the next conversation?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer names a specific stakeholder
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-020 — 'Who on your team would need to be in the room...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Is there anyone else who should be in the room — and what does their calendar look like?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer asks for materials
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ns-003 — 'Send materials + book demo on calendar'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Happy to send everything. To make it useful — can we get a demo on the calendar in the next two weeks with your finance team?

predictive_queue.reason: The belief shift must be verified before advancing. Surface the next unasked confirming question. These test whether the buyer sees the structural risk, not just the surface pain.
The primary objective is booking the demo with a named senior finance stakeholder. Every confirming question should build toward this commitment.
'Let me think about it' and 'send me materials' are the two most common M1 stall patterns. The speaker filter ensures these only fire on buyer speech.
If the buyer needs one more push, a credibility story about a similar firm's decision to move forward can provide the final proof.

cost_of_inaction_frames.trigger: Prospect wants to think about it / no urgency to book next step
cost_of_inaction_frames.reframe: Completely fair. Here's what I'd encourage: the context from this conversation is freshest right now. The risks you described, the names you mentioned, the scenarios we walked through. In two weeks, you'll be back in quarter-end mode and this gets pushed. One 45-minute session with the right people in the room while the thinking is sharp is worth more than three months of email threads. What would make it easy to get that on the calendar this week?

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: On a scale of one to ten, how confident are you that your current setup can handle double the fund count without adding people?
risk_identification_prompts.why: Scaling confidence check. Any answer under 8 is an opening. Ask them to explain why they didn't say 10.
risk_identification_prompts.tier_rationale: Confidence test. Fire when buyer is hesitant about next step. Provides a commitment number to anchor against.
risk_identification_prompts.l2_question: What makes it that number and not a ten?
risk_identification_prompts.l2_goal: Surface what is holding them back from full urgency
risk_identification_prompts.l3_question: What would have to change for it to become a ten?
risk_identification_prompts.l3_goal: Get them to name their own activation trigger

economic_buyer_alignment.scenario: Champion won't name the EB or commit to including them in M2
economic_buyer_alignment.approach: This is a gate-blocker, not a soft ask. If the champion says 'I'll check with my team' or 'Let me run it by Maria first,' they're either not a champion or they're not confident the EB cares. Test directly: 'What would Maria need to hear from you to make this worth 45 minutes of her time?' If they can't answer, coach them: 'Based on what you described today, the story I'd tell Maria is: your team is running three fund waterfalls in Excel with side-letter carve-outs, one key person owns the logic, and Fund V adds complexity the current process wasn't designed for. Does that land?' You're writing their internal pitch for them.

special_moves.description: When the buyer has expressed complexity skepticism (especially after naming failed vendor evaluations), offer to model their single most complex fund structure live in the M2 demo. This shifts the dynamic from "vendor pitch" to "technical validation challenge" where the buyer owns the test criteria.
trigger_conditions: buyer names 1+ vendors they evaluated and rejected
buyer expresses complexity skepticism ("I doubt anyone can handle our structures")
buyer describes a specific complex structure (hybrid waterfalls, side letters, multi-tier pref)
buyer has in-house models they are proud of but worried about

execution.step_1: Ask: "What is the single most complex waterfall structure in your portfolio right now? The one your team dreads at quarter-end."
execution.step_2: Let them describe it. Write down every detail. Do not interrupt.
execution.step_3: Reframe the M2 proposal: "I am not asking you to trust me. I am asking if it is worth 45 minutes to put [Fund X] on the table and see whether we can model it. Your structure. Your rules. If we cannot handle it, I will tell you — and you will have spent 45 minutes confirming what you already believe. If we can, that changes the math."
execution.step_4: Ask who needs to be in the room to validate the output.

special_moves.why_this_works: Buyers who have been burned by vendors have built a wall of skepticism. Generic demos bounce off that wall. The Hardest Fund Test turns the buyer into the test designer. They own the criteria. If the product passes their test, they sold themselves. If it fails, you lose nothing because the deal was already dead.
special_moves.tier_rationale: When complexity skepticism is detected, this becomes the most important move in the call. It replaces the standard M2 ask (dq-019) with a higher-conviction version.

champion_coaching_toolkit.description: Tools to help the champion sell internally after M1. Fires when champion test (dq-022) reveals the champion needs support framing the conversation for the EB.
champion_coaching_toolkit.trigger: Champion struggles to articulate the value back in their own words during dq-022, OR champion says they will "send the recording" or "forward some info" rather than actively pitching.

one_paragraph_summary.description: Offer to send a one-paragraph summary the champion can forward to the EB. You write it; they send it. Gives you control of the narrative that reaches the EB.
one_paragraph_summary.offer_language: I noticed you were taking notes — I will send you a clean summary after this. Two paragraphs, the key points from today. You can forward it to [EB name] directly or just use it as a reference. Save you the write-up.
one_paragraph_summary.template: Hi [EB name], I had a good conversation with [rep name] from Maybern today about [specific pain point the champion articulated]. Given [timing trigger — fund raise, quarter-end, team change], it seems worth 45 minutes to see whether they can model [specific fund/structure] live. Their platform sits above [Investran/admin/Excel] — it is not a replacement. I think it is worth a look. Does [proposed date] work?
one_paragraph_summary.why_this_works: The champion does not have to write the internal pitch from scratch. You wrote it for them in their voice. The EB receives a message that hits the right pain points because you controlled the framing.

internal_pitch_coaching.description: When the champion can articulate the pain but needs help framing the ask, coach them live on the call.
internal_pitch_coaching.coaching_language: Based on what you described today, the story I would tell [EB name] is: your team is running [X] fund waterfalls in [Excel/system], [key person count] people can reproduce the logic, and [timing trigger] adds complexity the current process was not designed for. The ask is 45 minutes to see whether anyone can solve it at your level of complexity. Does that framing land for you?
internal_pitch_coaching.why_this_works: You are writing their script while making them feel like they own it. The "does that framing land" question lets them edit it, which increases their ownership.

pre_meeting_brief.description: For M2 meetings with multiple stakeholders, offer to send a one-page brief the champion can distribute beforehand.
pre_meeting_brief.offer_language: I will put together a one-pager for the group so nobody walks in cold. Takes the pressure off you to brief everyone individually.
pre_meeting_brief.why_this_works: Reduces the champion burden. Every attendee arrives with the right frame. The champion looks organized without doing the work.

tone_principles.core: Every coaching move must feel like a service, not a sales tactic. The champion should feel helped, not handled.

language_shift.before: "Here is how to position this internally."
language_shift.after: "Let me make this easier. I will send you a summary you can forward — save you the note-taking."

tone_principles.posture: Colleague helping a colleague, not vendor arming a champion. The moment it feels strategic, trust erodes.
delivery: Offer the summary because you noticed them taking notes — not as a closing technique
Frame the pre-meeting brief as saving them effort, not as controlling the narrative
If they decline the help, do not push. Some champions want to own the internal sell.

why_now_gate.description: Before proposing M2, the rep MUST establish a timing anchor. If no fund raise, no audit deadline, no team change, or no competitive pressure has been identified, the deal has no urgency and M2 will stall.
why_now_gate.gate_question: Before we talk about next steps — what does the next 18 months look like for you in terms of fund launches, raises, or team changes? I want to make sure the timing makes sense for both of us.
why_now_gate.fire_when: No timing trigger has been surfaced in the conversation (dq-017 not asked or not answered with specifics)
why_now_gate.if_no_trigger_found: If the buyer cannot name a timing event, ask: 'What would have to change in the next six months for this to feel urgent enough to act on?' Their answer either creates urgency or tells you the deal is exploratory.

buyer_profile_routing.description: Buyer profile archetypes that modify question routing, objection handling, and story selection. Detected in m1-open based on trigger signals and early answers.

in_house_operations.description: Firm runs fund accounting internally. Has Investran, Allvue, or custom GL in-house. Bigger team, more control, different pain profile.
detection_signals: mentions running Investran, Allvue, eFront, or custom system in-house
describes team of 4+ on fund ops
says "we do our own fund accounting"
no mention of external fund admin

reframe.dq-005: Shift from "what if admin gets it wrong" to "what if your analyst leaves." Transferability risk replaces accuracy risk.
reframe.dq-001: Emphasize institutional knowledge trapped in individuals rather than admin dependency.

add_questions.question: Your team built the models in-house, which means you own the logic. But does the firm own it, or does one person own it? If your senior analyst built the waterfall templates, what happens to those when they move on?
add_questions.purpose: In-house variant of key person risk. Reframes "we control everything" into "one person controls everything."

in_house_operations.competitor_context: Investran/Allvue/eFront complement positioning. GL stays. Calc layer adds.
in_house_operations.pain_hypothesis: Key person risk + documentation debt + scaling ceiling. The in-house team is both the strength and the vulnerability.

outsourced_admin.description: Firm outsources fund accounting to a third-party admin (Citco, SS&C, Apex, CSC). Smaller internal team. Pain centers on checking the admin.
detection_signals: names Citco, SS&C, Apex, CSC, Gen II, Standish, or other admin

reframe.dq-001: Focus on "who checks the admin" rather than "who owns the logic." The logic is shared responsibility, which means nobody owns it.

add_questions.question: When your admin sends over the distribution calculations, what does your review process look like? How long does that take, and what are you checking for?
add_questions.purpose: Quantifies the shadow validation burden. Most firms spend 3-5 days checking admin math in Excel.

outsourced_admin.competitor_context: Admin complement positioning. Platform plays Switzerland. Admin becomes a booking agent.
outsourced_admin.pain_hypothesis: Shadow validation in Excel + admin dependency + error exposure. The firm is paying twice: once for the admin and once for the team that checks the admin.
