likely_objections.response: That's fine — we're estimating, not auditing. Rough numbers are more useful than no numbers. If your team spends 'a lot of time' on quarter-end, let's estimate what 'a lot' means. Is it 40 hours? 100? 200? We can bracket it.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Make the math collaborative and low-pressure. Start with time estimates, not dollar amounts.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer dismisses the exercise as unnecessary
objection_handling_paths.path: Reframe: 'The reason we do this isn't for us — it's so you have something concrete to bring to your CFO. Intangible benefits get stripped out of every internal conversation. Hard costs survive. Let's build the version that survives.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Positions the exercise as arming the champion, not justifying the vendor's price.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer can't estimate hours or costs
objection_handling_paths.path: Guide the math: 'How many people touch the quarter-end reconciliation? How many hours each? Multiply by four quarters. That's the baseline.' Break it into small, answerable questions.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Nobody knows the total. Everyone knows their piece. Build the total from the pieces.

proof_points.text: A healthcare PE controller called the platform pricing 'not that expensive' once the cost of the existing validation process was quantified — roughly $37K/year was a fraction of what the manual process cost.
Most firms find the ROI case is straightforward once the numbers are on paper. The harder part is sitting down to do the math.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer questions the need for quantification
trigger_conditions.linked_content: obj-030 — CFO needs a number, not a feeling
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Knowing it's inefficient and putting a dollar figure on it are two different things. The CFO needs a number that survives the back-of-napkin test.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer says they don't have the data
trigger_phrases: off the top of my head
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-026 — guide the math in small pieces
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Rough numbers work. How many people touch the quarter-end process? How many hours each? We'll bracket it.

predictive_queue.reason: The opening frame sets the tone for quantification. Corporate Visions 'Cost of Inaction' framing: the question isn't what Maybern costs — it's what the status quo costs.
After framing, transition to the first quantification question. Walk through activity-by-activity estimation.
If the buyer isn't ready for quantification yet, surface a pain question to build the emotional case before the numerical case.

stages.stage_name: Diagnose — Map Workflow, Quantify Cost, Surface Risk
stages.stage_goal: Walk through the buyer's workflow systematically. Quantify time and effort at each step. Surface operational risk. Build a rough ROI model collaboratively. This is the core of the meeting.
stages.current_buyer_belief: The process takes time, but everyone's used to it. We've never calculated the total cost because each person only does their part. The risk feels theoretical — nothing has actually gone wrong recently.
stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer hasn't mapped the full process end-to-end and totaled the hours, the handoffs, and the risk. Each team member sees their piece — nobody sees the aggregate. When the cost is finally calculated, it's almost always larger than expected.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'this is just how it works' to 'the total cost of this process is $X per year, it scales worse than linearly, and there's a key person risk we haven't addressed.'
stages.value_hypothesis: Three cost channels to quantify: (1) Direct labor: person-hours × fully-loaded rate across all analysts, controllers, and finance staff who touch the process each quarter. (2) Risk exposure: cost of a single distribution error (six figures), key person departure (months of recovery), or audit finding (remediation + reputation). (3) Scaling penalty: what happens to the labor cost when fund count doubles — it doesn't double, it triples, because complexity is nonlinear.
stages.why_change_frame: The cost isn't just the hours. It's the carry leakage from delayed distributions — because late distributions accrue pref. It's the LP trust erosion from corrections. It's the hiring pressure from manual processes that should be automated. And it's the opportunity cost of a finance team that spends 80% checking numbers and 20% analyzing them.
stages.why_now_frame: Every quarter the team runs the current process is another $X of unrecovered cost. Every new fund launched without a system adds a permanent layer of manual complexity. The compounding is real — and the sooner it's quantified, the sooner the team can make an informed decision.
stages.why_you_frame: The quantification framework is grounded in what similar firms have measured: hours saved, errors caught, distribution timing improvement, headcount avoidance. These aren't projections — they're documented outcomes from firms with similar complexity.

reframing_lines.text: What we often find is that the total hours are 3-5x what anyone initially estimates — because each person only sees their slice of the workflow.
The real impact usually shows up when the process needs to scale. Adding one more fund shouldn't require adding one more person.
The cost that's hardest to see is the opportunity cost — what would your team do with those hours back? That's usually where the strategic value lives.
Distribution timing is the sleeper ROI. Firms that distribute 14 days late — think about the pref accrual. At scale, that's real money returned to the GP.
Management teams strip out intangible benefits. What survives the CFO filter: headcount equivalence, error cost reduction, distribution timing improvement. Those three build the case.
The question isn't whether the ROI is there. It's whether the team has sat down to calculate it.
Any delay in distributions is a drag on pref, which translates to a drag on carry. The question is whether the team can quantify exactly how much that delay costs per event.

When someone leaves, there's a real cost: time to notice, time to hire, time to ramp. Most firms estimate $75K per departure. The question is whether that knowledge is in a system or in someone's head.
The cost of the status quo isn't fixed. As fund count grows, every one of these numbers gets bigger — sometimes more than linearly.
Even without the intangibles, the hard numbers — headcount equivalence, distribution timing, error reduction — usually clear the bar on their own.
When in doubt, be conservative. This model should be directional, not precise. Management will run their own math — if we're conservative and the numbers still work, the case is stronger.
The review time probably stays consistent. What changes is the preparation — that goes from hours to minutes. So we're not eliminating your oversight, we're eliminating the setup work.
Risk mitigation and unlocking data — that's what clients tell us the real value is. The workflows are nice, but having the data accessible is the night-and-day difference.

cost_of_inaction_frames.setup: Map the full quarter-end cycle: how many people, how many hours each, four times a year.
cost_of_inaction_frames.diagnostic_prompt: How many person-hours per quarter does your team spend on waterfall calculations, validation, and LP-facing reconciliation?
cost_of_inaction_frames.escalation: If you translated those hours into fully-loaded annual cost, what does that number look like?
cost_of_inaction_frames.scaling_test: What happens to that number if your fund count doubles in the next 18 months — does it scale linearly or worse?

cost_of_inaction_frames.setup: Surface the cost of errors, key person dependency, and audit defensibility.
cost_of_inaction_frames.diagnostic_prompt: When an LP challenges a distribution and your team has to reconstruct the logic, what does that cost you in time, credibility, and trust?
cost_of_inaction_frames.escalation: Have you ever quantified what a single distribution error costs — including the correction, the communication, and the trust impact?
cost_of_inaction_frames.scaling_test: As fund count grows, does the probability of an error stay the same or increase?

cost_of_inaction_frames.setup: Surface what the team would do with the time back — strategic analysis, LP relationships, fundraising support.
cost_of_inaction_frames.diagnostic_prompt: If your finance team got 30% of their time back from manual reconciliation and validation, what would they spend it on?
cost_of_inaction_frames.escalation: Is the finance team seen as a strategic asset internally, or primarily as an operational function?
cost_of_inaction_frames.scaling_test: What does the team's value to the firm look like if they're spending 80% on operational validation versus 80% on strategic analysis?

cost_of_inaction_frames.setup: Late distributions accrue pref. Quantify the financial impact of delays.
cost_of_inaction_frames.diagnostic_prompt: How many days does a typical distribution take from decision to funds out the door?
cost_of_inaction_frames.escalation: If distributions went out 7-14 days faster, what does that mean in terms of pref accrual saved?
cost_of_inaction_frames.scaling_test: As the number of investors and side letter provisions grows, does the distribution timeline compress or expand?

discovery_questions.question: How many person-hours per quarter does your team spend on waterfall calculations, validation, and LP-facing reconciliation?
discovery_questions.purpose: Quantifies the labor cost of the status quo.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Start here. This is the anchor number. Everything else builds on it. If they can't estimate, break it down: 'How many people touch the process? How many hours each?'

discovery_questions.question: If you translated those hours into fully-loaded annual cost, what does that number look like?
discovery_questions.purpose: Converts hours to dollars. Makes the cost tangible.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Do the math together. If they won't say the rate, use $150K fully-loaded as a proxy and let them correct it.

discovery_questions.question: What happens to that number if your fund count doubles in the next 18 months — does it scale linearly or worse?
discovery_questions.purpose: Projects future cost. Usually worse than linear.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: The answer is almost always 'worse.' Each new fund adds side letters, unique fee arrangements, and another set of Excel models. Complexity is nonlinear.

discovery_questions.question: When an LP challenges a distribution and your team has to reconstruct the logic, what does that cost you in time, credibility, and trust?
discovery_questions.purpose: Adds intangible costs — trust, reputation, LP relationships.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: This is Level 3 on the pain ladder. Connects operational failure to personal and relationship stakes. Let them feel it.

discovery_questions.question: If you had to justify investing in a new platform to your CFO next week, what ROI would they need to see?
discovery_questions.purpose: Learns the CFO's decision framework. Shapes the proposal.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Whatever number they say becomes the bar. Then use the cost data from this session to show whether the numbers clear it.

discovery_questions.question: Have you ever put a number on what the status quo is costing you — headcount, distribution delays, or carry leakage?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests whether they've already quantified the problem.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If yes — use their number as the starting point. If no — this meeting is the first time. That's significant: it means nobody has ever forced the calculation.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: How long does the full quarter-end process take end-to-end — from when it starts to when everything is finalized?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Total cycle time per quarter. Multiply by four for annual burden.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: How many people are directly involved when that workflow runs — analysts, controllers, fund accountants?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Headcount exposed to the process. Basis for fully-loaded cost calculation.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: If that activity doubled in volume — twice as many funds, twice as many investors — what would happen?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Scaling trajectory. Reveals whether cost growth is linear (unlikely) or exponential.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: What portion of your team's time goes to checking the admin's work versus original analysis?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Shadow work ratio. Most teams estimate 30-40%. Actual is usually 50-60%.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: How many hours does a single distribution cycle take — from calculation to notice to wire?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Per-event cost. Multiply by distributions per year for annual distribution burden.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: If the finance team had 30% of their time back, what's the first thing they'd work on?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Opportunity cost. Reveals what strategic work is being displaced by operational validation.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: What would it cost to hire one additional analyst to absorb the growth in fund count?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Headcount avoidance value. Platform cost vs. incremental hire cost is the simplest CFO math.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: How many days late are distributions typically going out — and what's the pref accrual impact?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Distribution timing ROI. Late distributions accrue preferred return. Quantifiable in dollars.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: Let's start with your team — how many senior staff and how many junior staff handle fund operations?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Team composition. Basis for fully-loaded cost allocation by seniority level.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: For management fees specifically — how many hours per event, and how many times per year?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Per-activity cost for fees. Multiply across all activities for total annual burden.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: For capital calls — how many hours each, and what's the annual frequency?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Cap call operational cost. Usually lower per-event but multiplied by frequency.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: For distributions — how many hours, and how many per year? This is usually where the heaviest lift is.
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Distribution operational cost. Typically the highest-hour activity due to waterfall complexity.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: How about ad hoc LP requests — how many per year, and how long does each one take?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Reactive work burden. Often underestimated. Can be 25-60 hours per year.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: If we add up those hours, does the total feel right as a sanity check — could one person running all of these activities handle roughly 10 funds?
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Sanity check on the aggregate number. Validates the model against the buyer's intuition.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: Should we run this for a stabilized fund or a fundraising fund? The hours will look very different — fundraising funds have closings, equalizations, catch-up interest.
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Lifecycle-stage calibration. Fundraising funds have 2-3x the operational burden of stabilized funds.

roi_construction_prompts.prompt: Does the preparation time look different from the review time? Let's separate those — review probably stays, but preparation should shrink significantly.
roi_construction_prompts.what_it_quantifies: Isolates the automatable portion. Review time is human judgment; prep time is system work.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: What happens if the information your team needs for a distribution arrives late from the admin?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: Downstream cascading delays. One late input delays the entire distribution cycle.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: Where does the process usually break under pressure — quarter-end crunch, audit season, fundraising?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: The weakest link in the chain. Usually a single person or a single spreadsheet.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: If your lead fund accountant left tomorrow, how long would it take someone else to pick up those models?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: Knowledge concentration risk. If the answer is 'months,' the risk is material.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: Has the team ever had to scramble to reconstruct how a number was calculated — for an LP, an auditor, or a board member?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: Audit and governance risk. If the answer is 'yes,' the traceability problem is real.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: When multiple people are working on the same fund calculations, how do you manage version control?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: Excel version conflicts. Usually the answer is 'carefully' or 'we don't.'

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: Has the admin ever sent over numbers that didn't match what your team expected?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: Admin error frequency. If it's happened once, it's happened more than once.

risk_identification_prompts.prompt: What's the biggest operational near-miss your team has experienced in the last two years?
risk_identification_prompts.what_it_surfaces: The story they tell when asked. This becomes a proof point for the internal business case.

economic_buyer_alignment.prompt: If you had to justify this investment to your CFO next week, what ROI would they need to see?
economic_buyer_alignment.purpose: Sets the bar. Whatever they say, build the case to clear it.

economic_buyer_alignment.prompt: What framework does your leadership use to evaluate technology investments — payback period, cost avoidance, or something else?
economic_buyer_alignment.purpose: Learns the CFO's evaluation model. Shapes the proposal.

economic_buyer_alignment.prompt: Could you present this business case internally, or do we need to sharpen any of the numbers?
economic_buyer_alignment.purpose: Tests champion readiness. If they hesitate, the numbers aren't strong enough yet.

economic_buyer_alignment.prompt: Are you comfortable that the ROI justification would hold up with your CFO or investment committee?
economic_buyer_alignment.purpose: Final gut-check on business case credibility.

economic_buyer_alignment.prompt: Before we wrap — which of the cost areas carries the most weight internally, and is there anything your team tends to discount that we should emphasize?
economic_buyer_alignment.purpose: Prioritizes the ROI narrative. Different CFOs care about different numbers.

likely_objections.objection: Management will just compare software cost to headcount cost — nothing else.
likely_objections.response: That math works in our favor for most firms. Translate the hours into fully-loaded headcount. Add the cost of a single distribution error — typically five to six figures. Factor in that the platform scales with new funds without adding bodies. Run the comparison.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Build the headcount comparison on the spot: 'Your team spends X hours per quarter. That's Y% of a full-time person at fully-loaded cost. Plus the platform handles new funds without incremental headcount.'

likely_objections.objection: The intangible benefits are nice but they won't sell internally.
likely_objections.response: Agreed — let's strip those out and focus on what survives the CFO filter. Headcount equivalence, error cost reduction, distribution timing improvement. Those three buckets build the case. Everything else is upside.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Focus the rest of the conversation on the three quantifiable buckets only. Don't try to sell intangibles.

likely_objections.objection: This is an additive cost — we already pay our fund admin.
likely_objections.context: Buyer sees this as a second bill, not a replacement.
likely_objections.response: The admin handles bookkeeping. The question is what it costs to check the admin — in person-hours, in key person risk, in error exposure. That's the cost this replaces. And most firms pass the platform through as an LP fund expense. Net cost to the GP is often zero.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask: 'How much of your team's time goes to validating the admin's output versus original analysis? Let's put a number on that.'

likely_objections.objection: Why does pricing scale with NAV? That doesn't seem fair.
likely_objections.response: The model mirrors how public market infrastructure is priced — Clearwater, Aladdin, Bloomberg PORT all use basis points. As NAV goes down, the fee goes down. The alignment is that when the firm grows, the platform absorbs the incremental complexity without the firm adding headcount.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Bridge to the ROI: 'At your current AUM, the annual cost is roughly $X. We just calculated the current process costs $Y per year. The delta is the case.'

likely_objections.objection: We can't go there yet on pricing — AUM isn't big enough.
likely_objections.context: Firm below the comfortable pricing threshold.
likely_objections.response: At the current AUM, the absolute dollar amount may feel like a stretch. The model is designed so that as AUM grows through fund raises and appreciation, cost stays proportional. Some firms start with a subset of funds to prove value before expanding.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Ask: 'If we could phase the rollout — start with 2-3 funds and expand — would that make the initial math work?'

likely_objections.objection: The ROI isn't clear enough to move forward.
likely_objections.response: What would the ROI need to look like for your CFO to say yes? Give me the bar, and let's see if the numbers we calculated today clear it. If they don't, that's useful information too.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Get the specific number. Then re-run the math against their threshold. If it clears, advance. If not, identify what's missing.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer can't quantify hours or costs — genuinely doesn't know
objection_handling_paths.path: Guide the math in small pieces: 'How many people touch the quarter-end process? Roughly how many hours each? What's a reasonable fully-loaded hourly rate for that team?' Build the total from components. Nobody knows the total — everyone knows their piece.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Breaks an impossible question into answerable sub-questions. The total emerges collaboratively.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer says 'the ROI isn't clear enough'
objection_handling_paths.path: Get the target: 'What would the ROI need to look like for your CFO to approve? Give me the bar.' Then compare the cost data from this session against that bar. If it clears, say so. If not, identify the gap.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Turns a vague objection into a specific threshold. Either the numbers work or they don't — both answers are useful.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer discounts risk as 'theoretical'
objection_handling_paths.path: Make it concrete: 'Have you ever caught an error in the admin's calculations? What would have happened if you hadn't?' If they have a story, that story is the proof point. If they don't, ask: 'How confident are you that you're catching every one?'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Shifts from abstract risk to specific incident recall. One real story is worth more than ten hypotheticals.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Champion says they can't present this to the CFO
objection_handling_paths.path: Arm them: 'What would make this presentable? Specific materials? A one-pager with the numbers? A reference call with a similar firm?' Build what they need. The champion presenting the case is always more credible than the vendor presenting it.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: The champion's ability to sell internally is the single strongest predictor of deal velocity.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer pushes back on LP expense pass-through
objection_handling_paths.path: Clarify: 'The LPA typically covers core fund administration systems as a fund expense. If this is classified as a book of record for investor-level performance, it falls under that category. Worth checking with your legal team — most firms we work with treat it this way.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: LP expense pass-through turns the pricing conversation from 'GP cost' to 'effectively free.' The LPA language is the key.

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer asks 'can't the admin just do this?'
competitor_response_paths.frame: The admin handles the first calculation. The fund re-does it in Excel to validate. That redundant cycle is what you're quantifying in this session.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: The admin does the calculation. The question is the cost of the parallel validation process your team runs on top of it. That's the cost this exercise is designed to quantify.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: How many hours per quarter does your team spend validating what the admin sends over?

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer suggests cheaper alternatives or partial solutions
competitor_response_paths.frame: Partial solutions address 30% of the problem. The remaining 70% — complex waterfalls, side letter compliance, admin reconciliation — is where the cost and risk concentrate.
competitor_response_paths.talk_track: A data warehouse or point solution might cover the reporting layer. The question is whether it handles LPA-compliant waterfall calculations, side letter provisions, and admin reconciliation. If not, the team is still in Excel for the hard part.
competitor_response_paths.follow_up: Which part of the process generates the most hours — the reporting, the calculations, or the reconciliation?

proof_points.text: A $5B+ real estate manager quantified their quarter-end close at 3 weeks of team time. After implementing a system of record, it dropped to days. The controller changed how they think about hiring.
One client found a six-figure error on a single distribution in their first quarter using the platform. The cost of that single error exceeded the annual platform cost.
A $23B manager chose a productized platform over Anaplan + Lionpoint. The ROI case: $1.7M/year for a platform vs. $10M+ for a custom build that would require ongoing consulting to maintain.

Three ROI buckets that survive the CFO filter: efficiency gains (person-hours saved × fully-loaded rate), key person risk reduction (what would a departure cost?), distribution timing improvement (pref accrual on late distributions).
Most firms pass the platform cost through as an LP fund expense. Net cost to the GP is often zero — making the ROI effectively infinite.

In one Value Creation session, a mid-market credit firm's VP of Ops mapped $32K of direct labor cost and 200 hours per fund per year — one-tenth of a full-time person. Across 5 funds, that's half a headcount nobody had budgeted for.

When distribution optimization was calculated with real numbers — 3 days saved × $10M average distribution × 7% pref rate — the annual pref savings alone were material. The IRR uplift was net positive even after including platform fees.

One prospect's VP of Ops said: 'Management is going to throw out the intangible numbers. They'll look at software cost vs. headcount cost — that's it.' The value creation model was rebuilt around those two numbers and the deal advanced.

In one Value Creation session with a $23B RE manager, the accounting team mapped 265 hours per fund per year. The PM team added another 307 hours — more than the AE expected. When the rep said 'that's less than I expected,' the accounting team corrected by adding scenario modeling, ad hoc requests, and catch-up calcs that weren't on the original list.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions time, hours, or duration
trigger_conditions.linked_content: roi-001 — 'How long does the full quarter-end process take?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How many person-hours per quarter does your team spend on that workflow?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions headcount, team size, or hiring
trigger_conditions.linked_content: roi-007 — 'What would it cost to hire one additional analyst?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: If technology absorbed the incremental complexity from new funds, how would that change the hiring math?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions cost, expense, or budget
trigger_conditions.linked_content: coi-001 — direct labor cost channel
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Let's put a number on what the current process costs — then compare. What's the fully-loaded annual cost of the hours we just mapped?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions scaling, growth, or fund expansion
trigger_conditions.linked_content: coi-001 — 'What happens if fund count doubles?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What happens to that cost number if your fund count doubles — does it scale linearly or worse?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions risk, errors, or near-misses
trigger_conditions.linked_content: risk_identification_prompts — match by risk category
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How did you catch that — and how confident are you that you're catching every one?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions key person dependency
trigger_conditions.linked_content: risk-003 — 'If your lead fund accountant left tomorrow...'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How long would it take someone else to pick up those models and run the next distribution?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions distributions or delays
trigger_conditions.linked_content: coi-004 — distribution timing cost
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: If distributions went out 7-14 days faster, what does that mean in terms of pref accrual saved?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions CFO, leadership, or approval process
trigger_conditions.linked_content: economic_buyer_alignment prompts
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: If you had to justify this to your CFO next week, what ROI would they need to see?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions inefficiency or duplicated effort
trigger_conditions.linked_content: rf-042 — 'total hours are 3-5x what anyone initially estimates'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What portion of your team's time goes to checking the admin's work versus original analysis?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer explicitly says management won't value intangibles
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Strip intangibles, focus on three hard buckets: headcount equivalence, distribution timing, error reduction
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What are the two or three numbers your management would need to see to say yes — headcount savings, distribution speed, or something else?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer suggests starting with one fund to test
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Phased approach = higher per-fund pricing. All funds = economies of scale.
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Starting with one fund is an option. The economics work better across all funds — would it help to see the pricing comparison side by side?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer asks whether the cost can be expensed to the fund / passed through to LPs
trigger_conditions.linked_content: All clients expense to the fund as a core book of record solution covered by the LPA.
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Every client we work with expenses this to the fund. If it's classified as a core fund administration system, it's covered in the LPA. Worth confirming with your legal team.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer distinguishes between accounting team time and PM/investment team time on the same workflows
trigger_phrases: accounting does X and PM does Y
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Run separate efficiency calculators for accounting vs. PM teams
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Let's separate accounting time from PM time — the hours and the value look different for each group.

predictive_queue.reason: The core of M4: activity-by-activity cost estimation. Walk through hours → dollars → scaling projection. The ROI construction prompts guide this sequence.
After direct costs, surface the indirect costs: error risk, key person dependency, opportunity cost of manual work. These are the numbers that make the business case compelling.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked question tagged make_risk_tangible or quantify_cost_of_inaction

predictive_queue.reason: Proof points in M4 should validate the numbers the buyer just provided: 'That's consistent with what we see — firms with your complexity typically spend X hours per quarter.'
After the cost picture is built, a credibility story that mirrors their situation makes the ROI concrete: 'A similar firm reduced their quarter-end close from 3 weeks to 4 days.'
After every 2-3 data points, reframe the emerging picture: 'So if I'm hearing you right, we're looking at $X per year in direct costs alone, before we even count the risk.'
EB alignment questions come after the cost picture is built. 'If the numbers we've built together clear your bar, what does the path to a decision look like?'

stages.stage_name: Align & Advance — Lock the Process Review
stages.stage_goal: Confirm the business case landed. Verify the champion can articulate ROI internally. Secure commitment for the Process Review meeting.
stages.current_buyer_belief: The numbers are bigger than I expected. The cost of inaction is real. But I'm not sure how to present this internally or what the next step should look like.
stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer now has the numbers but may not feel confident presenting them. They need a clear, bounded next step and materials that arm them for the internal conversation.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'the numbers are interesting' to 'the cost of inaction is material enough to warrant a process review — and I can make that case internally.'
stages.value_hypothesis: The business case built in this session should be strong enough for the champion to present internally without the vendor in the room. If it's not, the numbers need sharpening.
stages.why_change_frame: The quantification exercise revealed what the status quo actually costs. The question now is whether the buyer wants to understand how that cost could be eliminated — which is what the process review does.
stages.why_now_frame: The numbers are fresh. The champion's conviction is highest right now. Every week of delay erodes the urgency. Lock the process review while the business case is top of mind.
stages.why_you_frame: The process review maps the buyer's specific workflow and identifies exactly where the platform eliminates cost and risk. It's not a product pitch — it's a diagnostic that connects the numbers from today to a specific operational plan.

reframing_lines.text: The numbers we built today are yours, not ours. You can present them however makes sense internally.
The process review takes the cost picture we built today and connects it to specific workflow steps that can be improved.
If the champion in the room can articulate this ROI to their CFO without us in the room, the deal is real.

When you present this to management, lead with the hard numbers — software cost vs. headcount cost. That's the comparison they'll make. The intangibles add to it, but the hard math has to clear the bar on its own.

Here's how the strongest champions present this: 'Here are all the intangible considerations. Here are the additional efficiencies we might see. But just looking at the hard numbers, this is where we're at.' If hard numbers reach parity, the intangibles push it over.
If management takes a 'toe in the water' approach with one fund, pricing won't be as competitive. Expanding to all funds from the start typically unlocks better economics.

discovery_questions.question: Now that we've quantified the cost side — does this feel like a material enough problem to warrant a serious evaluation?
discovery_questions.purpose: Binary confirmation. Tests whether the belief shift landed.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If yes — proceed to close. If 'I think so' — the conviction isn't strong enough. Go back to the number that surprised them most.

discovery_questions.question: Could you present this business case internally, or do we need to sharpen any of the numbers?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests champion readiness to sell internally.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If they hesitate, ask: 'What would make this more presentable? Specific materials? A one-pager with the numbers?'

discovery_questions.question: Are you comfortable that the ROI justification would hold up with your CFO or investment committee?
discovery_questions.purpose: Final gut-check on business case credibility with the EB.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If no — identify the specific gap. Different CFOs care about different numbers. Find out which number matters most.

discovery_questions.question: Have you ever put a number on what the status quo is costing you — headcount, distribution delays, or carry leakage?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests whether this is the first time the cost has been quantified.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: If this is the first time, emphasize that: 'The fact that nobody has calculated this before is itself a signal.'

discovery_questions.question: Before we wrap — which of those cost areas carries the most weight internally, and is there anything your team tends to discount that we should emphasize?
discovery_questions.purpose: Prioritizes the ROI narrative for the internal conversation.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Whatever they say here shapes the proposal narrative. Write it down.

discovery_questions.question: Can we lock in the next meeting date to review your process in detail?
discovery_questions.purpose: Books the Process Review live on the call.
discovery_questions.conversation_note: Don't leave without a date. The Process Review should happen within 2 weeks while the numbers are fresh.

likely_objections.objection: I need to think about this before committing to next steps.
likely_objections.context: Champion is convinced but not confident enough to commit.
likely_objections.response: Completely understand. What would help you feel confident — a summary of the numbers we built today, a reference call with a similar firm, or something else? I'd rather arm you now than have the conviction fade.
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Identify what they need and provide it same-day. Then book a follow-up within one week.

likely_objections.objection: I'm not sure leadership will prioritize this.
likely_objections.context: Champion is uncertain about internal sponsorship.
likely_objections.response: What does leadership prioritize — headcount efficiency, risk reduction, or operational speed? The numbers from today can be framed around whatever matters most to them. Which lens would resonate?
likely_objections.what_happens_next: Reframe the business case around the specific metric leadership cares about. Then offer to help build a one-pager for the internal conversation.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Champion agrees the numbers are real but won't commit to Process Review
objection_handling_paths.path: Make the Process Review feel bounded: 'The process review is 60-90 minutes. We map your workflow, identify where the cost concentrates, and connect it to specific improvements. If it confirms the case, we move to a proposal. If it doesn't, you've still gained a useful process map.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Low risk, high information. The Process Review is positioned as diagnostic, not commitment.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Buyer wants to go straight to a proposal, skipping Process Review
objection_handling_paths.path: Redirect: 'A proposal without understanding the full operating model would be a guess. The process review — 60-90 minutes — gives us the detail to scope a proposal that actually survives the CFO's review.'
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Protects both sides from an inaccurate proposal that gets rejected.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Champion says management will only look at software cost vs. headcount
objection_handling_paths.path: Build that exact comparison: 'Your team spends X hours per fund per year. That's Y% of a full-time person at fully-loaded cost. The platform costs $Z per fund. If Z < Y, the math works on headcount alone — everything else is upside.' Make the back-of-napkin math work first, then layer on distribution timing and risk reduction.
objection_handling_paths.why_this_works: Meets the CFO where they are. If the simple math clears, the intangibles are free upside rather than the whole case.

proof_points.text: Champions who can articulate ROI in their own words close deals 3x faster than those who rely on the vendor to make the case.
The process review connects the cost picture to specific workflow steps — it's the bridge between 'we know it costs us' and 'here's exactly how to fix it.'

One VP of Ops told his AE: 'Ops has clearly identified we need this solution. We're on your team. But management needs hard tangible figures — not reputational risk.' He then presented the efficiency calculator to management, stripped the intangibles, and led with headcount equivalence. The deal advanced.

next_step_language.text: Here's what I'd suggest as next steps: a summary of the numbers we built today — sent to you same-day. Then a process review in the next two weeks to map the specific workflow that drives those costs. From there, a proposal.
Can we lock the process review onto the calendar now? The numbers are freshest this week.
What materials would help you present this internally — a one-pager with the cost breakdown, or a reference call with a similar firm?

trigger_conditions.condition: Champion confirms the numbers are material
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ns-014 — 'Can we lock the process review onto the calendar?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Could you present this business case internally — or do we need to sharpen any of the numbers?

trigger_conditions.condition: Champion says they can't present internally
trigger_phrases: not sure I can sell this
trigger_conditions.linked_content: objection_handling_paths[3]: arm the champion with materials
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: What would make this presentable — a one-pager with the numbers, a reference call, or something else?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer wants to skip to proposal
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-033 — process review first, then proposal
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: A proposal without the process review would be a guess. 60-90 minutes gives us the detail to scope it properly.

predictive_queue.reason: Verify the value case landed: 'The cost of inaction is quantifiable and material.' Test whether the champion can articulate the ROI internally.
Champion coaching: arm the buyer with a story they can retell internally. 'When you present this to your CFO, here's how a similar firm framed it...'
Final proof before advancing: the numbers should be incontestable. 'Based on what you've told me, the annual cost of your current process is $X. Maybern costs a fraction of that.'
Champion coaching: help the buyer frame the internal presentation. 'The way to present this isn't as a software purchase — it's as a risk reduction initiative.'
Lock the Process Review date. Every minute in m4-advance should build toward this commitment.

meeting_types.belief_shift: Our current process has specific fragilities that mirror what Maybern solves.
meeting_types.proof_of_shift: Client aligns on future state activities and confirms EB to join Tailored Demo with tailored agenda.

stages.stage_goal: Map the buyer's process end-to-end. Identify specific fragility points that mirror product capabilities. Create artifacts (process map) that drive the rest of the evaluation.

opening_statements.text: Today we want to map your process end-to-end — from fund terms being set all the way through to an LP receiving their distribution statement. The goal is to find the specific points where the process is most fragile.

discovery_questions.question: Let's map your process end-to-end: starting from when fund terms are set, walk me through every step until an LP receives their distribution statement.
discovery_questions.purpose: Full process mapping. Creates the artifact that drives the rest of the evaluation.

discovery_questions.question: At which points in that flow does data get manually entered or re-keyed from one system to another?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies manual handoff points — each one is an error risk.

discovery_questions.question: Where do overrides happen — and who has visibility into what was changed and why?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces auditability gaps and governance risks.

discovery_questions.question: How does your team reconcile the waterfall output back to the source data before it goes out?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests the QC process. Usually reveals it's one person's judgment call.

discovery_questions.question: Which fund structures have side-letter provisions that require separate handling outside your primary workflow?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces the side-letter complexity that breaks standard templates.

discovery_questions.question: How do you manage version control when multiple people are touching the same calculation?
discovery_questions.purpose: Collaboration and governance risk. Usually the answer is 'carefully' or 'we don't.'

discovery_questions.question: If your lead person on this process were unavailable for an entire quarter-end cycle, what breaks?
discovery_questions.purpose: Key person risk, revisited with more specificity.

discovery_questions.question: Looking at the full map we just built — where do you see the most fragile points?
discovery_questions.purpose: Self-identified. Gets them to own the diagnosis before you prescribe.

next_step_language.text: Now that we've mapped this out, do the fragility points we identified feel accurate — or are there others?
Do you see the connection between these specific gaps and what we showed you in the demo?
For the file review, we'll need sample files, templates, and examples of your most complex structures — can your team pull those together?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer knows their process has friction but hasn't mapped it systematically. Once the fragilities are visible on paper, they become impossible to ignore.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'we manage' to 'our current process has specific fragilities that mirror what a purpose-built system solves.'
stages.why_change_frame: The process map is the artifact. When the buyer sees every manual handoff, every re-keying step, every single-point-of-failure visually laid out — the case makes itself.
stages.why_now_frame: These fragilities compound with each new fund. Mapping them now prevents the next quarter-end crisis from becoming the trigger for a rushed decision.
stages.why_you_frame: The product was built around exactly these fragility patterns — they've been validated across hundreds of fund structures. The process map becomes the implementation blueprint.

proof_points.text: Process mapping sessions consistently surface 3-5 fragility points the buyer didn't consciously recognize as risks until they were documented.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer describes a manual handoff or re-keying step
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-058 — 'At which points does data get manually entered or re-keyed?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Where else in the process does data get manually re-entered between systems?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer identifies a fragility point
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Confirm fragility, connect to next step
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Now that we've mapped this out — do those fragility points feel accurate, or are there others?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions version control or conflicting files
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-062 — 'How do you manage version control?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Who has visibility into what was changed — and can you trace it back?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer describes a workflow step that involves manual re-entry between systems
trigger_phrases: download from one and upload to
trigger_conditions.linked_content: discovery_questions — handoff mapping
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: How many times does data get manually re-entered between systems in this workflow — and who catches it if something gets mistyped?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions Yardi, Investran, or another GL and describes workaround friction
trigger_conditions.linked_content: Platform can produce GL loader files — calculation happens in Maybern, booking happens in the GL
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: We can produce the exact loader file your GL needs. The calculation happens in the platform — the GL just books the result.

stages.current_buyer_belief: We agreed the cost is real, but I'm still not sure exactly where Maybern fits into our specific workflow. I need to see that it addresses our actual process, not a generic demo.

predictive_queue.reason: M5 is a workflow mapping exercise. Start with the systems: 'Walk me through the full flow — from GL export to investor notice. What touches what?'
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked advancing question with conversation_signals including manual_process or workflow_fragility

predictive_queue.reason: Map each handoff point in the process. 'When you move from step X to step Y, is that automated or manual? Who does it? How long does it take?'
predictive_queue.recommended_content: discovery_questions — next unasked question tagged expose_hidden_risk or workflow_fragility

predictive_queue.reason: As fragility points emerge, ground them with a credibility story: 'That manual handoff is exactly where another firm caught a $2M error.'
predictive_queue.recommended_content: customer_story_bank — match by workflow fragility or error theme

predictive_queue.reason: If the buyer uses a system Maybern integrates with (Yardi, Investran), surface the integration story to show how the handoff gets automated.
Proof points in M5 validate the fragility: 'Firms with your complexity typically have 15-20 manual handoff points per distribution cycle.'
At the end of the workflow map, confirm the future state: 'Based on what we've mapped, which of these steps would you want Maybern to handle first?'
Close M5 by confirming the buyer's future state vision and locking the Tech File Review.

meeting_types.belief_shift: Our data is ready (or knowably close) for a tailored demo.

stages.stage_goal: Validate data completeness. Finalize the dataset for a tailored demo that proves the product works with the buyer's actual fund structures.

opening_statements.text: Today is about making sure we have the right data to build a demo that looks like your actual world — not a generic one.

discovery_questions.question: Looking at the files you've shared, how well do these represent the full range of waterfall tier structures across your funds?
discovery_questions.purpose: Tests data representativeness.

discovery_questions.question: Are there any calculations that live off-system — in side spreadsheets, emails, or someone's head — that aren't captured here?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces hidden logic that needs to be in the demo.

discovery_questions.question: When you hit peak volume (quarter-end, audit season), how does the data flow hold up — where does it bottleneck?
discovery_questions.purpose: Stress-test question. Connects data to operational reality.

discovery_questions.question: Do you have any bespoke fund structures or LP arrangements that don't fit neatly into your standard templates?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies edge cases the demo needs to handle.

discovery_questions.question: Where have you seen data inconsistencies or format mismatches cause problems in the past?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies data quality issues that affect implementation.

next_step_language.text: Based on what we've reviewed, do you feel confident the dataset captures enough complexity for a realistic demo?
Are there any gaps we've identified that need to be filled before we can build the tailored demo?
Can we agree that this dataset is finalized — or do you need a few more days to supplement it?
Who on your team should we loop in for any follow-up questions as we build the demo environment?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer wants proof the platform can handle their specific complexity — not just a generic demo. The data review builds confidence that the tailored demo will be representative.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'can this handle our complexity?' to 'our data is ready for a demo that will prove it.'
stages.why_change_frame: A tailored demo with real data is the most efficient way to answer 'does this work for us?' — and it's the step most competitors skip.
stages.why_now_frame: The dataset should be finalized while the process review findings are fresh. Delay introduces context loss.
stages.why_you_frame: The willingness to build a custom demo environment from real data — at no cost — demonstrates confidence in the product's ability to handle actual fund complexity.

proof_points.text: Tailored demos built from actual fund data close at 3x the rate of generic product demos.

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer mentions data gaps or missing files

trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Are there any calculations that live in side spreadsheets or someone's head that aren't captured here?

trigger_conditions.condition: Buyer confirms data completeness

trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Can we agree this dataset is finalized — or do you need a few more days to supplement?

predictive_queue.reason: M6 is about data readiness. Surface questions about data format, completeness, and accessibility: 'What format is your GL export in? How often is it updated?'
Map the data pipeline: 'Walk me through how data gets from your GL to the files you'd send us. Is that automated or manual?'
If data gaps emerge, normalize them: 'Most firms we onboard have similar gaps. Our implementation team handles the cleanup as part of the process.'
Surface the specific integration path for their GL system: 'For firms running Yardi, we have a production SFTP feed that handles this automatically.'
Proof points in M6 should address data completeness concerns: 'Our implementation team has onboarded X funds with this GL configuration.'
Close M6 by confirming the dataset is finalized and locking the Demo Prep meeting.
predictive_queue.recommended_content: next_step_language — dataset confirmation + demo prep scheduling

meeting_types.belief_shift: We need to show up prepared so the exec team sees their world.
meeting_types.proof_of_shift: Team roles, storyline, timing confirmed for Tailored Demo.

stages.stage_goal: Align the internal team on storyline, roles, and executive audience for the tailored demo. Every demo moment should connect to a pain point or decision criterion the exec team cares about.

discovery_questions.question: When the exec team sees the demo, which scenarios will resonate most — where have they personally felt the pain?
discovery_questions.purpose: Anchors the demo to executive-level concerns.

discovery_questions.question: Are there specific side-letter divergences that have caused friction with LPs or auditors that we should feature?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies high-impact demo moments.

discovery_questions.question: What's the single biggest reconciliation burden that, if we solve it live in the demo, would get the strongest reaction?
discovery_questions.purpose: Finds the 'wow moment' for the demo.

discovery_questions.question: How should we anchor the ROI and pricing conversation — what framework does the decision-maker use?
discovery_questions.purpose: Pre-shapes the pricing discussion for M8.

discovery_questions.question: What's the narrative we need the exec team to walk away with — control, defensibility, scalability, or something else?
discovery_questions.purpose: Aligns the demo story to what matters most to the decision-makers.

discovery_questions.question: How well positioned is [champion name] to navigate the internal approval process? What obstacles might they face?
discovery_questions.purpose: MEDDPICC Champion gap fill. Enables the champion before the exec meeting.

next_step_language.text: Do we have the right storyline to land the belief shift with the exec team?
Are the roles and speaking order clear for everyone on our side?
Is the timing realistic for what we need to cover, or do we need to adjust scope?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The internal team is prepared but may not be aligned on which demo moments will resonate most with the executive audience. A poorly targeted demo wastes the exec's time and stalls the deal.
stages.belief_shift_target: Internal alignment: 'We know exactly what to show, who says what, and which moments will drive the decision.'
stages.why_change_frame: The demo that wins is the one that makes the exec team see their world — not a product feature tour.
stages.why_now_frame: This is the highest-leverage meeting in the entire cycle. Preparation quality directly predicts outcome.
stages.why_you_frame: The prep process itself is a differentiator — competitors don't typically invest this level of customization.

meeting_types.belief_shift: This works for our specific configuration — the path to purchase is real.
meeting_types.proof_of_shift: Path to purchase confirmed; proof points documented.

stages.stage_goal: Demonstrate with the buyer's actual data that the platform works for their specific configuration. Confirm the path to purchase.

opening_statements.text: Today you're looking at your actual fund structures in the system — not a generic demo. Everything here is configured from the files your team provided.

discovery_questions.question: You're looking at your actual fund structures — does the output match what you'd expect to see?
discovery_questions.purpose: Validation. Gets them confirming accuracy with their own data.

discovery_questions.question: Where do you see a material difference between what we're showing and how your team produces this today?
discovery_questions.purpose: Gap identification. Let them surface the value themselves.

discovery_questions.question: If you were launching a new fund next quarter, how confident would you feel running it through this vs. your current process?
discovery_questions.purpose: Future confidence test. Connects the demo to a real upcoming decision.

discovery_questions.question: What concerns do you have about rolling this out across your full fund portfolio?
discovery_questions.purpose: Surfaces implementation concerns before they become objections.

discovery_questions.question: After seeing this with your actual data, how does this compare to anything else you've considered?
discovery_questions.purpose: MEDDPICC Competition gap fill at exec level, post-demo.

discovery_questions.question: What would make or break this decision for the people in this room?
discovery_questions.purpose: MEDDPICC Decision Criteria gap fill at exec level.

discovery_questions.question: We quantified the cost of inaction earlier — after seeing this with real data, does that business case still hold up?
discovery_questions.purpose: MEDDPICC Metrics reinforcement. Reconnects demo to ROI.

next_step_language.text: Based on what you've seen with your own data, does this feel like a real solution — or are there gaps?
Is there anything your CFO or Board would need to see that we haven't covered?
Do you see a path to purchase here — and if so, what does that path look like on your end?
Can we agree on next steps to move toward a proposal?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The executive audience has seen demos before and is skeptical. They need to see their own fund structures, their own waterfall logic, their own investor terms. Anything generic gets dismissed.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'this might work' to 'this works for our specific configuration — the path to purchase is real.'
stages.why_change_frame: The tailored demo eliminates the 'but will it work for us?' objection. The buyer is looking at their own data producing the outputs they currently build in Excel.
stages.why_now_frame: The executive team's time is the scarcest resource. This is the one shot to create confidence. Everything before this was preparation; everything after depends on what happens here.
stages.why_you_frame: No other vendor in this space builds a custom demo environment from actual fund data at no cost. The willingness to invest in the tailored demo demonstrates product confidence.

likely_objections.objection: The numbers don't match what we expect.
likely_objections.context: Configuration issue or client model error.
likely_objections.response: Let's trace it. Click the number, see every step. Either we find a configuration issue — which we fix — or we find something in the existing model worth investigating. Both are valuable.

likely_objections.objection: What about the features we don't see today?
likely_objections.context: Product gap concern at decision point.
likely_objections.response: Transparency matters. Here's what's available today, here's the roadmap with timelines. The question is whether today's capabilities deliver enough value to start, with the understanding that the platform continues to ship.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Exec asks 'what would make or break this decision?'
objection_handling_paths.path: Turn it back: 'That's exactly what I'd like to understand from you. What are the two or three things that would make or break it from your perspective?' Let the exec define the criteria — then confirm the demo addressed them.

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Exec mentions what a competitor showed them
competitor_response_paths.path: Don't compare features. Ask: 'Was that demo built from your actual fund data, or was it a standard demo? The difference matters — standard demos show capability, tailored demos show fit.'

proof_points.text: A $23B manager evaluated Anaplan + consulting vs. a productized platform. Chose the product because it worked with their actual data — no customization required.
Firms that see their own data in the system move to proposal in 2 weeks. Firms that only see standard demos take 2 months.

discovery_questions.question: How would you define success at 90 days post-launch — what metrics matter most?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer believes the product works but hasn't visualized what 'live on the platform' looks like for their team day-to-day. The gap between 'good demo' and 'signed contract' is confidence in the transition.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'this works in a demo' to 'I can see what live on this platform looks like for my team — the transition is manageable.'
stages.why_change_frame: The future state workshop closes the imagination gap. The buyer designs their own future operating model with the platform at the center — it's their vision, not the vendor's.
stages.why_now_frame: Success metrics defined now become the accountability framework for implementation. Without them, go-live is a date, not an outcome.
stages.why_you_frame: The implementation team that builds this has done it for firms with similar complexity. The future state isn't aspirational — it's based on documented patterns from live clients.

competitor_response_paths.trigger: Buyer asks 'why you over building ourselves?'
competitor_response_paths.path: Enable the champion: 'Purpose-built platforms represent years of R&D across hundreds of structures. When your CFO asks that question, the answer is opportunity cost — your engineering time is worth more than maintaining waterfall logic.'

proof_points.text: Firms that define 90-day success metrics in the future state workshop report higher satisfaction at go-live — expectations were set, not assumed.
One PE/credit firm added funds to their contract during implementation — they saw value before going live.

reframing_lines.text: Pricing is basis points on NAV plus unfunded — it mimics how public market infrastructure is priced.
Most firms pass this through as an LP expense. If it's a core book of record solution, it's typically covered in the LPA.
Three value buckets justify the investment: efficiency gains, key person risk reduction, and distribution timing improvement.

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer agrees on value but wants to negotiate terms. The risk is that the proposal process introduces new stakeholders or re-opens settled questions.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'we need to review the proposal' to 'the commercial terms match the value we've validated — this is a fair deal.'
stages.why_change_frame: The proposal reflects the scope validated in the process review and tailored demo. It's not a quote — it's the documented version of everything agreed.
stages.why_now_frame: The evaluation has been thorough. Delaying the proposal risks losing stakeholder alignment that was built over months.
stages.why_you_frame: Pricing is basis points on NAV plus unfunded — mirrors public market infrastructure. Most firms pass it through as an LP expense.

likely_objections.objection: Can you do better on pricing?
likely_objections.response: The pricing reflects the scope we validated together. Happy to discuss structure — multi-year commitments, phased rollout, fund count tiers. What structure works best for your organization?

proof_points.text: Three value buckets justify the investment: efficiency gains, key person risk reduction, distribution timing improvement.
Most firms pass the cost through as an LP expense. Net cost to the GP is often zero.

discovery_questions.purpose: Separates real blockers from routine negotiation.

discovery_questions.question: For the redlines flagged, are these commercial concerns or purely legal/compliance positions?
discovery_questions.purpose: Routes issues to the right resolution path.

discovery_questions.question: Is there anything in the commercial terms that shifted during legal review that we need to re-align on?
discovery_questions.purpose: Catches scope creep during legal.

discovery_questions.question: Walk me through your signing sequence — who signs first, and what's the internal routing?
discovery_questions.purpose: Maps the execution process.

discovery_questions.question: Where does procurement stand — tracking in parallel or sequentially after legal?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies if procurement is a separate blocker.

discovery_questions.question: Have you gotten any signals internally that support for this has shifted — from the team or from leadership?
discovery_questions.purpose: MEDDPICC Champion gap fill. Checks for defection at the final stage.

next_step_language.text: Are all open redlines now resolved, or are there items still outstanding?
Do both sides have a clear view of the final document — no surprises?
Can we commit to a target signing date?
Is there anything that could delay execution that we haven't addressed?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: Legal review introduces new stakeholders (legal, procurement) who weren't part of the value discussion. The risk is that routine redlines become deal blockers because the new stakeholders don't have the business context.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'legal has concerns' to 'every open item is resolvable — nothing blocks signature.'
stages.why_change_frame: Separate blockers from redlines. Most legal issues are standard negotiation, not deal-breakers. The business case was validated — legal's job is to protect the terms, not re-evaluate the decision.
stages.why_now_frame: The implementation queue has lead times. Every week of legal delay is a week pushed on go-live. Anchor the signing date to the implementation timeline the team already agreed to.
stages.why_you_frame: Standard mutual NDA, SOC 2 Type II, standard enterprise terms. Nothing unusual. Happy to walk legal through the DDQ and security posture directly.

likely_objections.objection: Our legal team has flagged several clauses.
likely_objections.response: Let's categorize them: which are standard redlines and which are potential blockers? We can address the standard items quickly and focus attention on anything material.

objection_handling_paths.trigger: Champion signals internal support is wavering
objection_handling_paths.path: Address directly: 'Have you gotten signals that support has shifted? If so, better to surface it now and address the concern than discover it after legal is done.' Champion defection at this stage is the leading indicator of deals going dark.

trigger_conditions.condition: Legal flags specific clauses
trigger_conditions.linked_content: dq-096 — 'Are these commercial concerns or legal/compliance positions?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Let's categorize: which are standard redlines and which are potential blockers?

trigger_conditions.condition: Deal momentum appears to be slowing
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ohp-036 — check champion support
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Have you gotten any signals internally that support has shifted? Better to surface it now than discover it later.

predictive_queue.reason: M11 is about resolving every open item. Surface questions about legal and procurement blockers: 'What specific items does your legal team need to review? What's their typical turnaround?'
Proof points in M11 address security and compliance: 'SOC 2 Type II certified. Data encrypted at rest and in transit. Single-tenant architecture.'
De-risk the signing: 'Every open item is resolvable. We've been through this process with X firms in the last year. Here's what typically takes the longest and how we accelerate it.'
Normalize the legal process: 'Most firms complete legal review in 2-3 weeks. The longest was a firm with a 6-person legal team and that still closed in under 5 weeks.'
Confirm signing readiness: 'Is there anything else that would need to be resolved before you're comfortable signing?'
Lock the signing date. Confirm handshake on final documents.

meeting_types.belief_shift: The implementation team has the context to deliver what we bought.

stages.stage_goal: Transfer context from sales to implementation. Confirm the implementation plan matches expectations. Ensure the client team is identified, available, and committed.

discovery_questions.question: What are the top three things the implementation team needs to understand about your environment that might not be in the files?
discovery_questions.purpose: Captures tribal knowledge before handoff.

discovery_questions.question: Who on your side will be the day-to-day point of contact — and do they have the bandwidth?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies the implementation champion.

discovery_questions.question: Are there any upcoming events that create hard deadlines or blackout periods for the rollout?
discovery_questions.purpose: Identifies timing constraints.

discovery_questions.question: What does a successful first 30 days look like from your team's perspective?
discovery_questions.purpose: Sets early success criteria.

discovery_questions.question: Where have past technology implementations stalled or gone sideways in your organization — what should we avoid?
discovery_questions.purpose: Learns from their prior failures. Prevents repeating them.

next_step_language.text: Does the implementation plan match your expectations for scope and timeline?
Is the full team identified and available for the kickoff?
Can we lock in the kickoff date and send calendar invites today?
Is there anything else you need from us before we officially begin?

stages.buyer_problem_hypothesis: The buyer is excited but anxious about the transition. They've seen implementations go wrong before. The sales team needs to hand off context — not just a contract — so implementation starts with the full picture.
stages.belief_shift_target: Move from 'we signed, now what?' to 'the implementation team has the context to deliver what we bought.'
stages.why_change_frame: Implementation success depends on context transfer, not just technical capability. The sales team's deep understanding of the buyer's operating model, pain points, and stakeholder dynamics needs to reach the implementation team intact.
stages.why_now_frame: The first 30 days set the tone. A strong kickoff builds confidence. A weak one erodes the trust that took months to build.
stages.why_you_frame: The implementation team reports to the CTO — not sales. They're technical operators, not project managers. And they've done this for firms with similar complexity.

proof_points.text: Implementation team handles 90-95% of configuration. Client role is validation — confirming output matches what they know to be true.
AI tooling accelerating LPA parsing, chart of accounts mapping, and historical reconciliation. Every quarter the process gets faster.

trigger_conditions.condition: Client raises concerns about past implementations
trigger_conditions.linked_content: pp-044 — impl team handles 90-95% of config
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Where have past technology implementations stalled in your organization — and what should we avoid?

trigger_conditions.condition: Client confirms readiness for kickoff
trigger_conditions.linked_content: ns-043 — 'Can we lock in the kickoff date?'
trigger_conditions.fallback_prompt: Can we lock in the kickoff date and send calendar invites today?

predictive_queue.reason: M12 transfers context from sales to implementation. Surface questions that ensure the implementation team has what they need: 'What are the top 3 things you want live first? What's your biggest concern about the transition?'
Confirm the implementation plan: 'Does the phased approach we've outlined match your team's priorities and availability?'
Final proof: 'Your implementation manager has onboarded X funds with this configuration. Typical time to first live fund is Y weeks.'
Set expectations with a success story: 'A firm with a similar setup told us the go-live was smoother than expected because we'd already mapped every workflow in M5.'
Confirm kickoff is scheduled and implementation plan is agreed.

competitor_responses.customer_concern: We use Carta for fund admin and cap table.
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Acknowledge Carta's strength in venture/simple structures. Surface the complexity ceiling.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Carta is strong for venture-style funds with pro rata structures. When complexity increases — deal-by-deal waterfalls, catch-ups, clawbacks, side letter variations — firms have found they need purpose-built depth. We've seen PE firms try Carta's waterfall functionality and revert to Excel within months.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: What are the most complex waterfall structures across your fund family — and how confident is your team in how those are handled today?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: Carta says they can handle waterfall calculations now.
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Shift from feature claims to depth. Rigid templates vs. configurable engine.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Carta's waterfall approach uses rigid templates that require constant Excel validation. Firms with negotiated side letters, custom fee structures, or multi-tier waterfalls find they need a configurable engine, not a template.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: How many of your investor arrangements have custom terms that wouldn't fit neatly into a standard template?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: Carta is building out a full fund admin — shouldn't we wait?
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Carta's admin expansion targets simpler funds. Institutional complexity is a different market.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Carta is expanding into fund administration, but the target is primarily smaller and simpler fund structures. Institutional-grade complexity — unique investor terms, multi-strategy structures, detailed LPA compliance — requires a different level of depth than what a broad platform can offer.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: What's the most complex LPA provision your team has to manage — and how would you test whether any platform can actually handle it?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: We already use Juniper Square.
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Complementary, not competitive. JSQ = portal/CRM. Maybern = calc engine.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Roughly a third of similar clients use both platforms. Juniper Square handles investor communications and the portal. The calculation engine — waterfalls, management fees, carried interest, admin reconciliation — is a separate layer. There are templated exports that connect the two.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: Are you using JSQ primarily for the portal and CRM, or are you relying on it for fund-level calculations as well?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: We're not sure about sharing data with another vendor.
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Address the data ownership concern directly — Maybern keeps data with the fund, not the vendor.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Some firms have concerns about how portal vendors handle their proprietary data. With a performance book of record, the fund owns the data directly — it's calculated in your system, and you push it wherever you want. The data flows out to JSQ, not the other way around.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: How comfortable is your team with how data flows between your current systems — and who ultimately controls what gets sent to investors?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: We use Allvue — doesn't it do what you do?
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: GL ≠ performance book of record. Complementary.
competitor_responses.talk_track: Allvue is a strong general ledger for partnership accounting. The gap is at the investor level — waterfalls, carried interest, scenario modeling, LP performance. That's where teams end up in Excel. A performance layer sits on top of Allvue and handles everything between accounting and the investor.
competitor_responses.follow_up_question: When your team needs investor-level waterfall output, where does that calculation happen — in Allvue or somewhere else?

competitor_responses.customer_concern: We already run Investran in-house.
competitor_responses.reframe_strategy: Everyone with Investran still lives in Excel for calcs. Complementary positioning.
