Shape the evaluation criteria: 'What criteria is your team using? Let me make sure our process addresses those criteria directly.'
Was the demo built from real data: 'Was that built from your actual fund data, or a standard product tour? The difference matters.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer names a competitor, immediately surface the matching competitor_response_path by matching the trigger field against the transcript. If no stage-level match, fall through to the top-level competitor_responses array. After delivering the response, escalate to an advancing question tagged redefine_decision_criteria to shape how the buyer evaluates alternatives.

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer has a proprietary system, internal tool, or long-standing Excel-based process they've invested in. The system was built for structures that existed when it was designed. When fund structures evolve, the system becomes rigid and requires hacking. Do not attack the build — validate the investment, surface the rigidity cost.
buyer_language_examples: we thought we could do it ourselves
it seemed simpler to just build it
our CTO wanted to keep it in-house
the team preferred to own the code
it started as a side project
it was supposed to be temporary
our data team put something together
engineering built a process for us
our ops team built a workaround
rep_response_patterns: Validate, then surface rigidity: 'That system was built for the structures that existed at the time. What happens when the fund structure changes?'
Opportunity cost framing: 'How much of your tech team's time goes to maintaining it versus building new capabilities?'
Revenue constraint angle (from Golub): 'Has the team ever had to say no to a fee structure or investor term because the system couldn't accommodate it?'
Fund complexity outgrows spreadsheets: 'When it does, someone builds an internal system — and that system is rigid. The cycle repeats with every new fund.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer mentions an internal build or proprietary system, surface a reframing line that validates the investment then surfaces rigidity cost. Do NOT attack the build. Escalate to an advancing question about what happens when fund structures change or the system builder leaves. Preferred DQ signals: internal_build, excel_dependency.

direct_triggers: our data team put something together
engineering built a process for us
our ops team built a workaround

known_conflicts.reason: pc-005 matches 'homegrown tool' (substring of 'homegrown tooling') and has wt=9 > pc-004 wt=8. Not a detection gap — the phrase triggers a cluster. Fix requires either weight adjustment or word-boundary matching.
known_conflicts.workaround: In production, the decision engine's cluster reinforcement and problem type context would resolve this correctly after 1-2 additional turns.

preferred_question_routing.description: When pc-004 fires, prefer questions about maintenance burden, key-person dependency, and rigidity over generic workflow questions.

priority_questions.question: How dependent is that on one person or team?
What happens when the workflow changes — like a new fund with different terms?
How much effort does it take to keep that current?
Is that something you see as strategic or just necessary to keep things moving?

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Workflow Friction — Manual and Distributed Pain
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer describes manual handoffs, re-keying, Excel-based coordination, or distributed effort where nobody sees the total cost. This is the primary discovery vein in M1 and the foundation for M4 quantification. The friction is invisible because it's spread across people.
buyer_language_examples: added one thing at a time
caught it at the last minute
always comes down to the wire
can't take on anything new during close
lost count of how many tools
every system has its own version
different numbers depending on where you look
every fund brought a new system
piled on top of each other
pull it out of the system
reformat it before we can use it
clean it up before it goes anywhere
sanity check before it goes out
one more pair of eyes on it
wrong LP got the wrong number
LPs keep asking for more detail
investor questions take forever to answer
January and February are a write-off
last two weeks of every quarter
the close takes the whole team offline
each new fund adds another layer
we have a version of that
the source of truth is actually
we found a way around it
if you change one thing everything breaks
it works but I don't know why
it's only a matter of time
we're the ones who make it all work together
there's no single place to see everything
depends on which system you look at
every system tells a slightly different story
we spend more time moving data than using it
those two weeks are basically lost
subject to so much human error
investors in our funds that come in
rep_response_patterns: Make the total visible: 'What we usually find is the total hours are 3-5x what anyone initially estimates — because each person only sees their slice.'
Connect to scaling: 'Adding one more fund shouldn't require adding one more person.'
Octopus of systems reframe: 'You've got this octopus of systems with plenty of tentacles. The question is how you get intelligence out without five people gluing it together.'
Separate prep from review: 'The review time probably stays. What changes is the preparation — that goes from hours to minutes.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer describes manual process or fragmented workflow, surface the next unasked advancing question tagged workflow_fragility. This is the primary discovery vein — keep asking. If the buyer has answered 3+ workflow questions, escalate to a reframe that makes the total cost visible. Preferred DQ signals: manual_process, workflow_fragility, shadow_models.

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer mentions that one person owns the process, the models, or the institutional knowledge. This is Level 3 pain — personal and organizational risk. The cost of a departure is $75K+ (search, hire, ramp) plus the risk of errors during the gap. If the knowledge is in someone's head rather than a system, the fund is one resignation away from a crisis.
buyer_language_examples: we'd have to loop people in
need to bring a few people in
a couple people would need to weigh in
run it by a few people
hard to say who's in charge
she just knows how it works
he's the only one who knows
I'd need to get buy-in from
not sure who signs off on that
a few people would need to weigh in
we'd have to figure that out
that's a good question — I'm not sure
it's not really anyone's job specifically
it kind of falls between teams
rep_response_patterns: Make it concrete: 'If your lead fund accountant left tomorrow, how long would it take someone else to pick up those models?'
Quantify the cost: 'Most firms estimate $75K per departure — search, hire, ramp time. The question is whether that knowledge is in a system or in someone's head.'
Story pattern: 'One CFO called in a panic — their lead model builder for 15 funds had just resigned. That's the moment everyone wishes they had a system.'
For outsourced teams: 'A dedicated team is an asset. The question is what happens if key people on that team leave.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer reveals single-person dependency or knowledge concentration, surface an advancing question tagged organizational_risk — specifically dq-001 or dq-005 (M1) or dq-063 (M5). These are the highest-emotion questions in the system. Let the weight land before following up. Escalate to a reframe about the cost of a departure.

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Scaling Concern — 'What happens when we double'
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer mentions growth, new funds, expanding AUM, or raising the next vintage. The cost of the current process compounds nonlinearly — each new fund adds side letters, unique fee arrangements, and another set of Excel models. This is the strongest 'Why Now' frame because the cost trajectory is predictable and accelerating.
rep_response_patterns: Project the cost: 'What happens to that number if your fund count doubles — does it scale linearly or worse?'
Usually worse: 'Each new fund adds side letters, unique fee arrangements, and another set of models. Complexity is nonlinear.'
Headcount scaling: 'Your finance team adds a person every time you add a fund. The platform absorbs the incremental complexity without adding headcount.'
The whole point of getting an admin: 'The whole point of getting an admin is to have less people — but most firms end up stacking more people on top.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer mentions growth, new funds, or scaling, surface an advancing question tagged create_future_contrast — preferably dq-006 (M1) or dq-047 (M4). These questions bypass the 'we're fine' defense by projecting into a future the buyer hasn't stress-tested. If in M4, escalate to an ROI prompt that projects the cost curve.

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer relies on a fund administrator for calculations, reporting, or reconciliation. The admin handles the first pass but the fund re-does it in Excel to validate. That shadow work cycle is the hidden cost. Position Maybern as complementary to the admin — not replacing them — while surfacing the cost of the parallel validation process.
buyer_language_examples: by the time we see it
we trust them to get it right
they've been doing it for years
we wouldn't know how to do it ourselves
we're pretty removed from that process
rep_response_patterns: Surface the shadow work: 'The admin does the calculation. The question is the cost of the parallel validation process your team runs on top of it.'
Don't position against the admin: 'We don't compete with administrators. We enable funds to pick their service level in ways that weren't available before.'
Key person risk on the outsourced team: 'A dedicated team is an asset. The question is what happens if key people on that team leave.'
The opposite of control: 'If you don't own your own logic, you just have to accept what the admin provides. And they won't take fiduciary responsibility.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer mentions admin dependency, surface a reframe that positions Maybern as complementary — not replacing — the admin. Never attack the admin relationship. Escalate to an advancing question about the shadow work cycle (dq-008 or dq-010). Preferred DQ signals: vendor_comparison, shadow_models.

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Stall Pattern — 'Let me think about it'
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer signals interest but won't commit to a next step. This is the most dangerous M1/M2 outcome — the deal dies in 'let me think about it' limbo. The underlying cause is usually one of: weak belief shift, unclear next step, no named stakeholder, or the champion doesn't feel armed to sell internally.
rep_response_patterns: Surface the real concern: 'Totally understand. What specifically would you need to see or know to feel confident?'
Arm the champion: 'What would make this presentable internally — a one-pager, a reference call, or something else?'
Create urgency through materials: 'Happy to send everything. To make it useful — can we get a demo on the calendar in the next two weeks?'
Time-bound the follow-up: 'Can we at least put a tentative hold on the calendar? If the timing shifts, we can move it.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer stalls ('let me think about it', 'send materials'), the belief shift didn't fully land. Surface a confirming question that tests whether they see the risk — dq-018 (M1) or dq-033 (M2) or dq-051 (M4). If they confirm, immediately escalate to next_step_lock. If they deflect, drop back to an advancing question to rebuild the case.

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer is in or near Value Creation. The conversation requires collaborative estimation of hours, costs, and risk. Management will strip out intangibles — the case must be built on hard numbers: headcount equivalence, distribution timing, error reduction. The three cost channels: direct labor, risk exposure, and opportunity cost.
rep_response_patterns: Activity-by-activity estimation: 'Let's start with management fees — how many hours per event, and how many times per year?'
Sanity check the total: 'That's roughly X hours per fund per year — one-tenth of a full-time person. Across all funds, does that pass the sniff test?'
Strip intangibles for management: 'Lead with the hard math. Software cost vs. headcount cost. If the simple math clears, the intangibles are free upside.'
Distribution timing ROI: 'If distributions went out 7-14 days faster, what does that mean in pref accrual saved?'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When in Value Creation and buyer engages with cost discussion, surface the next ROI construction prompt in sequence. Walk through activity-by-activity estimation: hours → dollars → scaling projection. After 3+ ROI prompts answered, escalate to a confirming question that tests whether the business case clears the EB's bar (dq-051, dq-053).

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Executive Alignment — 'My CFO would need to see'
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer references the Economic Buyer, investment committee, or leadership approval. The champion needs to articulate ROI without the vendor in the room. The strongest champions present the case in balance sheet terms — software cost vs. headcount cost — with intangibles as upside, not the core argument.
buyer_language_examples: we'd have to figure that out
that's a good question — I'm not sure
a few people would need to weigh in
it's not really anyone's job specifically
it kind of falls between teams
rep_response_patterns: Learn the EB's framework: 'If you had to justify this to your CFO next week, what ROI would they need to see?'
Shape the internal presentation: 'Lead with the hard numbers. Intangibles are upside, not the core argument. What metric matters most to your leadership?'
Arm the champion: 'What would make this presentable — a one-pager, a reference call, or something else?'
The strongest champions: 'Champions who can articulate ROI in their own words close deals 3x faster than those who rely on the vendor to make the case.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer references CFO, leadership, or approval process, surface an advancing question tagged shift_ownership_to_economic_buyer — dq-007 (M1), dq-049 (M4), or dq-073 (M7). The goal is to learn the EB's framework before proposing anything. Escalate to a confirming question that tests champion readiness (dq-052, dq-055).

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Implementation Fear — 'This feels like a heavy lift'
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer expresses concern about implementation timeline, disruption, team bandwidth, or integration complexity. Common across M2 (post-demo) and M9 (future state). The response should position parallel running, phased rollout, and the implementation team handling 90-95% of configuration.
buyer_language_examples: can't take this on right now
rep_response_patterns: Timeline framing: 'Typical 3-5 fund client is live in about four months. Parallel running — no big-bang cutover.'
Low lift for the client: 'The implementation team handles 90-95% of configuration. Your team reviews and validates.'
Integration is file-based: 'Data comes in from the GL through standardized exports — not a deep technical project.'
Earned trust: 'The system runs in parallel first. Nobody flips until they've verified it themselves.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer expresses implementation fear, surface the matching objection response from likely_objections or objection_handling_paths. Lead with timeline framing ('3-5 fund client is live in four months') and low-lift positioning ('implementation team handles 90-95%'). Escalate to an advancing question about past implementations (dq-105) or rollout concerns (dq-079).

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer reveals a formal procurement, legal, security, or vendor onboarding process. This is a timing risk — parallel tracking of security/legal alongside the technical evaluation saves weeks. The M3 Coordination Meeting is designed to surface and plan around this.
rep_response_patterns: Run security in parallel: 'We have SOC 2 Type II and a standard DDQ package ready. Running security in parallel saves weeks.'
Map the full process: 'Help me understand the decision process — who needs to weigh in, and what does each person care about most?'
Anchor to compelling event: 'Working backward from [their deadline], here's what the timeline needs to look like.'
NDA acceleration: 'We can have the NDA turned around in 48 hours — standard mutual NDA.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer reveals procurement or legal process, surface an advancing question tagged decision_process_visibility — dq-039 (M3), dq-044 (M3), dq-098 (M11). Map the full process before it becomes a blocker. Once mapped, escalate to next_step_lock to run security/legal in parallel with technical evaluation.

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: Data Accessibility — 'I can't get answers fast enough'
pattern_clusters.description: Buyer describes needing data that takes days to get from the finance team — performance by investor, deployment levels, scenario modeling. The data exists but is trapped in spreadsheets, siloed systems, or people's heads. This is the single source of truth play. The cost isn't the calculation — it's the inability to access the answer when a decision needs to be made.
buyer_language_examples: how long to get that data
we're the ones who make it all work together
there's no single place to see everything
depends on which system you look at
every system tells a slightly different story
we spend more time moving data than using it
rep_response_patterns: Make the latency visible: 'When the deal team needs performance data — capital call percentage, inception-to-date IRR — how fast can the finance team turn that around?'

Connect latency to blocked decisions: 'The data exists — it's just not accessible. When you can't see it, your mind doesn't go through the decisions you would have made if the data were in front of you.'
Single source of truth: 'Every stakeholder in the firm can access the system. Not seat-based pricing. Real-time, traceable, sliceable.'
Octopus reframe: 'You've got an octopus of systems — plenty of tentacles. The question is how you get intelligence out.'

decision_rules.selection_logic: When buyer describes data accessibility pain (requests take days, no dashboard, multiple systems), surface an advancing question tagged shift_from_tools_to_outcomes — dq-014 (M1) or dq-027 (M2). Connect the latency to blocked decisions. Escalate to the 'octopus of systems' reframe.

pattern_clusters.description: Buyer casually describes manual workflow steps or operational reality without framing it as a problem. These statements expose hidden inefficiencies, reconciliation work, or spreadsheet dependence.
example_discovery_questions: What happens once the data leaves the system?
Who owns that reconciliation process today?
How long does that step usually take?
What happens if something breaks during that step?
How often do errors show up during that process?

pattern_clusters.canonical_label: EB Gatekeeping — 'Let me build the case first'
pattern_clusters.description: The champion is actively delaying or preventing economic buyer involvement. They want to validate the solution themselves before exposing leadership. This creates two risks: (1) the EB never hears the value story directly from Maybern, and (2) the champion's internal pitch may dilute or misframe the value. The rep needs to understand the EB's priorities and coach the champion on how to present internally — or get the EB in the room.

decision_rules.selection_logic: When the champion signals they want to gatekeep EB access, the rep must NOT accept the gate. Instead: (1) ask what the EB cares about — learn their framework, (2) ask what would make the champion confident enough to bring them in, (3) offer to help the champion build the internal case. The goal is to either get the EB in the room or ensure the champion's internal pitch is as strong as Maybern's own pitch. Escalate to a credibility story about a firm where the champion brought leadership in early and it accelerated the deal.
