reinforcement_rules.trigger: The same pattern_cluster.pattern_id fires on two or more consecutive client segments, or fires again within the reinforcement_window.
reinforcement_rules.cap: Effective weight cannot exceed 16 (base weight + max boost). This prevents a single cluster from permanently dominating.
reinforcement_rules.effect_on_scoring: The boosted effective weight is used in the decision engine's Step 1 (cluster matching) when resolving ties between simultaneously matched clusters. It does NOT change the cluster's stored priority_weight in the brain JSON — only the session-local effective weight.
effect_on_content_selection: When a cluster has reinforcement_count >= 2, the priority_scoring engine skips DQs that have already been asked for this cluster's principle and instead surfaces the next-deepest unasked DQ in the same principle family.

When reinforcement_count >= 3, the system surfaces proof_points and credibility_stories instead of additional DQs — the buyer is clearly dwelling on this topic, so evidence is more valuable than more questions.
When reinforcement_count >= 4, surface a confirming question to test whether the belief shift has landed — the buyer has been talking about this long enough that they may be ready to advance.

content_depth_ladder.behavior: Standard decision_rules.recommended_move from the cluster. No change from baseline.

Same recommended_move, but skip the most recently surfaced item. Select the next-deepest DQ by priority_scoring, preferring DQs with different conversation_signals than the ones already surfaced. This deepens discovery instead of repeating the same angle.

Shift recommended_move to proof_point or credibility_story. The buyer has been on this topic for 3+ turns — they need evidence, not more questions. Select from the cluster's linked_item_ids.proof_points or customer_story_bank.

Shift recommended_move to confirming_question. Test whether the belief shift has landed. If the buyer confirms, transition to the next phase. If they deflect, drop back to reinforcement_count=2 behavior.

data_structure.cluster_fire_history: Map of pattern_id → { last_fired_at: timestamp, reinforcement_count: integer, effective_weight: integer, items_surfaced: array of item_ids }

session_state.initialization: All clusters start at reinforcement_count=0 and effective_weight=base priority_weight.
session_state.increment: When a cluster fires and the same cluster fired within the reinforcement_window, increment reinforcement_count by 1 and add boost_per_repeat to effective_weight (up to max_boost).
session_state.decay: If a DIFFERENT cluster fires, the previous cluster's reinforcement_count drops by 1 (minimum 0). This prevents stale reinforcement from lingering when the conversation moves on.
session_state.reset: When the session stage changes (rep manually advances), ALL cluster reinforcement resets to zero. Fresh stage, fresh state.

example_flow.scenario: Buyer describes workflow pain three times during M1/m1-advancing

turns.buyer: We handle most of this in Excel.
Yeah, it takes about 3 weeks every quarter.
turns.card: dq-009 (deeper — distribution timeline, different angle than dq-001)

turns.buyer: And nobody else can do it if she's out.
turns.count: pc-006 fires fresh (count=1), pc-005 decays to count=1
turns.card: dq-005 (key person risk — new cluster, fresh angle)

turns.buyer: Exactly, it's all in her head.
turns.card: pp-003 (proof point — evidence that key person risk is common, because count=2 deepens to proof)

stage_priority_lock.description: A global rule that prevents the teleprompter from surfacing content designed for a future meeting stage. When a cluster's decision_rules recommend a move that belongs to a later stage, the system blocks the future-stage move and converts the response to a stage-appropriate alternative. This replaces the soft 'fall through to predictive queue' behavior in Step 3 with a hard conversion rule.

lock_rule.trigger: A cluster fires and its decision_rules.preferred_meeting_stages does NOT include the current session stage.
lock_rule.action: BLOCK the cluster's recommended_move. Do NOT fall through silently to the predictive queue. Instead, execute the stage_conversion protocol.

stage_conversion_protocol.description: When a future-stage move is blocked, the system converts the response into two components: a stage-appropriate reframe and a scored advancing question. This ensures the buyer's signal is acknowledged (reframe) and discovery continues (question) without pulling future-stage content into the current conversation.

step_1_reframe.action: Surface a reframing_line from the CURRENT stage that acknowledges the buyer's topic.
step_1_reframe.selection: Match by principle alignment. If the blocked cluster's preferred_principle exists on any reframing_line in the current stage, use that reframe. If no principle match, use the first unshown reframe in the current stage.
step_1_reframe.example: Buyer says 'How much does this cost?' in M1. pc-010 fires (ROI Quantification) but preferred_stages are M4 only. System blocks roi_prompt and surfaces a reframe: 'Before we get into numbers, I want to make sure we're solving the right problem. The question isn't what Maybern costs — it's what your current process costs you.'

step_2_advancing_question.action: Attach the top-scored advancing question from the CURRENT stage that aligns with the blocked cluster's topic.
step_2_advancing_question.selection: Use the priority_scoring engine with a temporary principle_preference boost for the blocked cluster's preferred_principle. This ensures the follow-up question is thematically connected to what the buyer just said, even though the full cluster response is blocked.
step_2_advancing_question.example: After the pricing reframe, the system surfaces dq-013: 'How do you think about budgeting for something like this — you have a working process today, so the question is what triggers an investment in change?'

blocked_cluster_handling.description: What happens to the blocked cluster's signal after conversion.
blocked_cluster_handling.signal_note: The fact that the buyer raised this topic is logged in the session state. When the rep eventually reaches the cluster's preferred stage, the system remembers the signal and prioritizes the cluster's recommended content.

deferred_signal_schema.blocked_at_stage: string — the stage where the cluster fired but was blocked
deferred_signal_schema.blocked_move: string — the recommended_move that was blocked
deferred_signal_schema.buyer_utterance: string — what the buyer said that triggered the cluster

blocked_cluster_handling.deferred_signal_behavior: When the session reaches the deferred_to_stage, the deferred signal auto-activates. The cluster fires immediately with its original recommended_move, as if the buyer had just said it. This ensures topics raised early in the conversation are addressed at the right time, not forgotten.

example.m1_event: Buyer says 'How much does this cost?' → pc-010 blocked → reframe + dq-013 surfaced
example.m4_event: Rep reaches m4-frame → deferred signal auto-activates → pc-010 fires with roi_prompt as recommended_move → ROI construction prompts begin immediately

stage_order.description: The canonical stage order used to determine whether a stage is 'future.' A stage is considered future if it appears later in this sequence than the current stage.
stage_order.note: A cluster whose preferred_meeting_stages are ALL later than the current stage is blocked. If ANY preferred stage matches the current or an earlier stage, the cluster is not blocked.

exceptions: Competitor responses (pc-003) are NEVER blocked by stage lock. A buyer naming a competitor must be addressed immediately regardless of stage.
Objection responses from top-level objection_handling are NEVER blocked. Objections happen when they happen.
Confirming questions in a confirming stage are NEVER blocked, even if the cluster's preferred_meeting_stages don't include the current confirming stage.

response_question_chaining.description: When the decision engine surfaces a non-question move (reframe, competitor_response, vendor_response, credibility_story, integration_story, objection_response), the system automatically attaches the top-scored advancing discovery question from the same cluster. This creates a response → question chain that maintains conversational momentum. Uses existing DQs only — no new questions generated.

chaining_logic.step_1: After the decision engine produces a card (Steps 0-7), check whether the recommended_move is in chainable_moves.
chaining_logic.step_2: If chainable, identify the active cluster. Look up its decision_rules.preferred_principle.
chaining_logic.step_3: Run priority_scoring on all unasked advancing DQs in the current stage. Apply a +5 bonus to DQs whose principle_tags include the active cluster's preferred_principle. Select the highest-scored DQ.
chaining_logic.step_4: Attach the selected DQ to the card as a secondary element: 'THEN ASK' line below the primary card content.
chaining_logic.step_5: If no unasked advancing DQ matches the cluster's principle, attach the top-scored unasked DQ from the current stage regardless of principle — any question is better than no follow-up.

response_question_chaining.no_cluster_fallback: If no cluster is active (e.g., the response came from the predictive queue), attach the next unasked DQ from the predictive queue's discovery_question entry.

card_format.primary: The non-question response (reframe text, competitor positioning, credibility story, etc.)
card_format.secondary_item_id: The DQ's item_id (for tracking in dq_status)

suppression_rules: Do NOT chain a question if the response is already a question (advancing_question, confirming_question).
Do NOT chain if the rep is in a confirming stage and all advancing DQs are asked — let the confirming flow take over.
Do NOT chain if cluster_reinforcement.reinforcement_count >= 3 — at that depth the system has shifted to proof_point mode, and adding another question would undercut the evidence.

example.buyer: We manage most of this in Excel.

primary_card.text: Every fund has an accounting book of record. The question is: what's your performance book of record?

chained_question.label: THEN ASK
chained_question.text: Walk me through who actually owns the waterfall logic today — and what happens when that person is unavailable.
chained_question.reason: dq-001 scored highest for pc-001's preferred_principle (reframe_status_quo). Signals overlap: status_quo_confidence, ownership_unclear.

story_intelligence.description: Stories are reference material, not verbatim scripts. This layer indexes story ELEMENTS — pain patterns, persona types, outcomes, 'aha' moments, competitive context, ecosystem details — so the response composition engine can pull the right pieces when building a credibility moment. The rep never reads a story. They draw from it to construct their own.
design_principles: Stories are a library, not a teleprompter. Index for retrieval, not for display.
A single story may contain 3-5 usable elements. Each element is independently addressable.
The rep composes in real-time. The system surfaces the ingredients, not the dish.
Story elements combine with product knowledge, ICP context, and ecosystem awareness to form complete response prompts.
Never surface a full story as a card. Surface a composition guide: the relevant element, why it's relevant, and how to frame it for this buyer.

story_element_schema.description: Each story in the customer_story_bank gets decomposed into addressable elements. Elements are tagged for retrieval by the composition engine.

element_types.description: A moment where a client experienced the same pain the current buyer is describing. Used to establish 'you're not alone' credibility.
element_types.example: cs-01: 'Their quarter-end close took 3 weeks, entirely driven by reconciling admin numbers in Excel.'
surfaces_when: cluster matches the story's pain pattern
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'We work with a [similar firm type] who was dealing with the same thing — [pain mirror]. Here's what they found...'

element_types.description: The measurable result the client achieved. Used when the buyer needs evidence that change produces results.
element_types.example: cs-01: 'Quarter-end close went from 3 weeks to days. Controller changed how they think about hiring — technology instead of headcount.'
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'After [timeframe], they saw [specific outcome]. The bigger shift was [strategic change].'

element_types.description: A named role or persona in the story that mirrors someone on the current call. Creates identification — 'that person is like me.'
element_types.example: cs-04: 'Their controller was the one who pushed for the evaluation. Same role, same frustration.'
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'Their [role] was actually the one who drove this. Same situation — [specific parallel].'

element_types.description: The specific insight or discovery that shifted the client's perspective. The moment they saw their situation differently.
element_types.example: cs-02: 'They realized the fund admin wasn't wrong — they just couldn't verify. That gap was the risk.'
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'The thing that changed their mind was [aha moment]. They weren't looking at it that way until [trigger].'

element_types.description: The client's tech stack, admin relationship, or operational setup. Used when the current buyer has a similar environment.
element_types.example: cs-05: 'They were on Yardi for GL, outsourced to Gen II for admin, and running everything else in Excel.'
surfaces_when: vendor detected in conversation matches story ecosystem
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'They had a similar setup — [ecosystem]. The challenge was [specific friction between systems].'

element_types.description: How a past client overcame the same objection the current buyer is raising.
element_types.example: cs-gong-001: '$11B manager tracking admin errors — started as validation, became the system of record after two quarters.'
surfaces_when: active objection matches story's original objection
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'Funny enough, [client] said the same thing. What they did was [resolution approach]. After [timeframe], [what changed].'

element_types.description: What the onboarding/adoption actually looked like. Used to neutralize implementation fear.
element_types.example: cs-gong-002: 'Started with two people, ran parallel for a quarter, no disruption to existing workflow.'
surfaces_when: buyer asks 'how long does this take'
element_types.composition_guidance: Frame as: 'The way [client] approached it was [phased approach]. No big bang. [Specific low-risk starting point].'

element_index.content: Large RE manager spent 3 weeks every quarter-end reconciling admin numbers in Excel before they could close the books.
Quarter-end close went from 3 weeks to days. The controller stopped thinking about hiring and started thinking about technology.
The controller realized the question wasn't 'do I need another person' — it was 'do I need a system.' Technology replaced the headcount conversation entirely.
Mid-market PE firm kept catching their admin getting waterfall calculations wrong on complex preferred return structures. Every quarter was a firefight.

Implemented Maybern as the independent checker. Caught multiple errors in the first quarter alone. Within two quarters, the admin started booking what the system calculated instead of the other way around.
The admin wasn't fired or replaced — they just stopped being the source of truth for calculations. The relationship improved because the reconciliation fights went away.
5 funds live. Team spent 2 days doing hands-on configuration. Now adding new vehicles quarterly. Views infrastructure as competitive edge, not cost center.
2 days of hands-on configuration to go live with 5 funds. Team did the work themselves — not a 6-month consulting engagement.
CAO asked whether they could give KPMG read-only platform access instead of building separate audit workbooks. The auditor now traces calculations directly to the system of record.
Auditor switch from Deloitte to KPMG exposed that the calculation logic wasn't independently traceable — the old auditor knew the spreadsheet, but the new one didn't.
$1.7M annual contract — largest single-phase deal. 44 funds, 12-month implementation. Chose productized solution over Anaplan + Lionpoint custom build.
CFO evaluated Anaplan + Lionpoint ($50-70M internal estimate for custom build) and chose a productized platform instead. Now sits on Customer Advisory Board.
Firm evaluated Anaplan with Lionpoint as implementer. Estimated $50-70M for a custom build. Chose productized because they didn't want a consultant army maintaining fund logic.
CFO described their ops transformation as 'getting out of the nineties.' Chose to keep everything in-house as a strategic differentiator — the platform enabled that without outsourcing.
$200B+ alt manager evaluated building an internal Anaplan solution estimated at $50-70M. Recognized the ongoing maintenance burden would require a permanent engineering team.
$700-900K initial contract to prove concept. Thesis: productized solution without the consultant army or internal engineering team to maintain.
Data spread across eFront, custom CRM, Google Sheets, and Snowflake. New evergreen fund had non-traditional structures that broke existing tools.

AI explainability feature — the system could explain its own calculations in plain English — got the team excited about auditability. They'd never seen a system that could trace and explain simultaneously.
$15B credit manager running SS&C for evergreen funds and a custom app for closed-end. 200 employees, 17 in tech. Data inconsistency across Investran, Geneva, and custom applications.
Build vs buy decision landed on buy — engineering time was better spent on firm differentiation, not maintaining fund calculation logic. 17 engineers freed from ops maintenance.
CFO managing multiple admins, multiple GLs, multiple data sources. Operational inefficiencies from fragmentation — no single view across the portfolio.

CFO described wanting a 'cockpit' — one place to see standardized calculations and reporting across all admins and fund structures. The word 'cockpit' resonated because it implied control without doing the flying.
LP needed to verify that JV partners were honoring fee and waterfall agreements. Alternative was hiring an auditor at massive cost for every JV.
Proposed 5 JV partner pilot. Platform became the automated auditor — verifying partner calculations without external audit cost. Scaled from there.
First outreach April 2024. Weren't ready. Watched improvements over 18 months. Came back, brought latest fund in-house from CBRE admin, and signed.
Started with 3-5 funds. Added more funds to the contract while still in implementation. New funds go into the platform from day one. Expanding within 6 months of go-live.
Team saw enough value during implementation that they expanded scope before go-live. New funds launched directly into the platform — no spreadsheet phase.

Pattern across multiple clients: admin sends distribution calcs, system flags variances. Sometimes rounding, sometimes missed side-letter provisions. One client found a six-figure error on a single distribution in the first quarter.

Six-figure error caught on a single distribution in the first quarter. The admin wasn't lying — they just missed a side-letter provision. Without independent verification, it would have gone out to the LP.
After 6 months on platform, CFO told the admin: just book what's in the system. Stopped shadowing in Excel entirely. Admin became a booking agent instead of a calculation engine.
Admin fee dynamic changed after the fund owned its own calculations. Less work for the admin meant better economics. The admin relationship improved, not worsened.
One person maintained all waterfall models for 15 funds. That person gave notice. CFO called in a panic — institutional knowledge was trapped in someone's head and their Excel files.
Not an edge case — happens constantly across the industry. When the key person walks, they take the logic with them. The spreadsheet stays but nobody can modify it safely.
Portal vendor wanted proprietary data access that felt like a data grab. Fund lost control of the data flow — vendor dictated what got sent to investors.
Switched approach: performance book of record keeps data with the fund. Data flows OUT to portal, not the other way. Fund controls what gets sent to investors.
Lost two internal accountants in quick succession. Admin also lost key staff and started missing deadlines. Key person risk hit from both sides — internal and external.

Controller was skeptical of pricing until it was contextualized: '$37K a year in expenses to help the data become better — the pricing is really not that expensive.' AUM-based pricing landed once framed against validation burden.

Data spread across 5+ systems: proprietary asset management platform, admin's Investran GL, admin's Grid control system, Excel workbooks, and Quantrix. Portfolio management team described the situation as 'chaos we've normalized.'
'Chaos we've normalized' — portfolio management team's description of their data environment. Five systems, no single source of truth, and everyone had accepted it as just how things work.
Emerging manager paying outsourced consulting firm $5-6K/month for manual reconciliation. About to raise larger fund. All reconciliation is manual and tedious.
Booked demo in under 10 minutes. Price sensitivity vanished when framed against $5-6K/month consulting cost they were already paying. Direct cost offset.
Operations team identified the need but couldn't get management buy-in. Management's approach: back-of-napkin math — software cost vs headcount cost. Intangibles rejected.

Value creation model built collaboratively — hard costs front and center, intangibles as upside. Management approved when the math was presented in their language: headcount equivalence + error cost + scalability.

Product team constrained by operational limitations — couldn't offer certain fee structures to investors because the internal system couldn't accommodate them. Dir of Product Solutions: 'there's probably money we're leaving on the table.'

'There's probably money we're leaving on the table because our system can't handle the fee structure.' The operational tool wasn't just slowing things down — it was limiting what the firm could offer to investors.

$11B manager ran Maybern in parallel with their admin for multiple quarters, logging every discrepancy. Plan: go to the admin in Q3 with the evidence and renegotiate the relationship from a position of data.
50-fund shop doing 10-15 distributions per quarter. Team of two. After system was built out, slowed down hiring. Stopped rolling Excel tabs manually. Scaled with the team they had.
After 2-3 quarters of parallel running, team started feeding the admin distributions instead of reviewing admin output. Admin did a high-level check. Saved significant time and fees came down.
Admin fees came down after the fund owned its own calculations. Less work for admin = lower fees. The platform paid for part of itself through admin cost reduction.

retrieval_logic.description: How the composition engine finds the right story elements for the current moment.
steps: 1. CONTEXT ASSEMBLY: Read current state — active problem_type, active cluster, buyer posture, detected signals, ecosystem vendors mentioned, buyer role (if known), firm size (if known), stage.

2. ELEMENT TYPE SELECTION: Based on posture and move type, determine which element_type is most appropriate. Defensive_but_cracking → pain_mirror or aha_moment. Evaluative → outcome_proof. Stalling → implementation_reality. Objection active → objection_resolution.

3. TAG MATCHING: Query the element_index for elements whose tags overlap with current context. Score by overlap count across pain_themes, persona_roles, fund_types, firm_size, ecosystem_vendors, problem_clusters.
4. WEIGHT + RECENCY: Multiply tag match score by retrieval_weight. Deprioritize elements from stories already referenced this session.
5. RETURN TOP ELEMENT: Surface the highest-scored element with its composition_guidance template to the response composition engine.

scoring_formula.persona_match: 5 points if buyer role matches persona_roles
scoring_formula.fund_type_match: 4 points if buyer fund type matches
scoring_formula.firm_size_match: 4 points if buyer firm size matches
scoring_formula.cluster_match: 3 points if active cluster in problem_clusters
scoring_formula.problem_type_match: 3 points if active problem_type in problem_types
scoring_formula.recency_penalty: -50% if this story was referenced in last 5 turns

session_tracking.elements_surfaced: array of element_ids surfaced this session
session_tracking.stories_referenced: array of story_ids whose elements have been used
session_tracking.never_repeat_rule: Same element_id cannot be surfaced twice in a session. Different elements from the same story CAN be surfaced.

deal_signal_tracking.description: Accumulates conversation_signals detected during the session and exposes them to the scoring engine for dynamic question prioritization. When multiple signals converge, the system can infer composite discovery needs.

signals_detected.type: Map of signal_name → { count, first_detected_at, last_detected_at, source_utterances[] }

signal_pairs_detected.type: Array of signal pairs that co-occurred within 120 seconds

signal_pair_rules.description: When specific signal pairs are detected together, the system adjusts question prioritization to address the compound need.

pairs.effect: Boost MEDDPICC-economic_buyer questions. The buyer is describing fragile processes owned by unclear people — the next move is to identify who has authority to change this.
Boost MEDDPICC-pain questions. The buyer is price-anchored before feeling the pain — more diagnosis needed before any ROI discussion.
Boost MEDDPICC-decision_process questions. The buyer is comparing vendors but stalling — map the decision process to understand what's blocking progress.
Boost questions tagged create_future_contrast. The buyer has current friction AND is growing — project the pain forward.
Boost questions tagged make_risk_tangible. The buyer uses Excel AND faces audit pressure — connect the two.
Boost questions tagged shift_ownership_to_economic_buyer. The buyer is interested in ROI but the EB isn't in the room — need to get the EB identified.

integration_with_scoring.description: Deal signal tracking feeds into the priority_scoring engine's MEDDPICC dimension (Step 5-6). When a signal_pair_rule fires, the boosted MEDDPICC element or principle receives additional points during scoring, causing questions addressing that gap to rise to the top.
integration_with_scoring.mechanism: The boost_amount is added to the meddpicc_relevance or principle_match dimension for questions matching the boosted element. This is a session-local scoring adjustment — it does not modify the stored scoring_dimensions.

deal_signal_tracking.reset: All signal tracking resets when the meeting session ends. Signals do NOT persist across sessions (each call starts clean).

exposure.description: The accumulated signal state is visible in the question tracker UI as a 'Signal Summary' bar above the category list. Shows which signals have been detected and which compound patterns have emerged.
exposure.ui_example: 🔥 workflow_fragility (3x) + 👤 ownership_unclear (1x) → Prioritizing: Economic Buyer questions

advancing_confirming_discipline.description: Controls the transition from advancing questions to confirming questions. Confirming questions are gated behind a belief_shift_detected signal. This prevents premature confirmation attempts and ensures discovery is sufficiently deep before testing the belief shift.

belief_shift_detection.description: The system infers whether the belief shift has landed based on conversational evidence. This is NOT a binary flag set by the rep — it is computed from accumulated signals.

detection_methods.description: If 60%+ of advancing DQs in the current stage have been asked, the system infers that discovery is sufficiently deep for confirmation to begin.
If the buyer explicitly acknowledges risk or pain (detected via speaker_detection + conversation_signals), the belief shift may have landed even before 60% coverage.
trigger_phrases: I hadn't thought of it that way

detection_methods.description: The rep can manually signal that the belief shift has landed by advancing to a confirming stage. This is the strongest signal.

state.belief_shift_detected: boolean — computed from the methods above
state.detection_method: string — which method triggered the detection

before_belief_shift.advancing_questions: ELIGIBLE — the primary content type. Scored and surfaced normally.
before_belief_shift.confirming_questions: GATED — not surfaced by the scoring engine, predictive queue, or momentum card. Confirming questions exist in the brain but are invisible to the rep until the gate opens.
before_belief_shift.exception: If the rep is already in a confirming stage (m1-confirming, m2-confirming, m4-advance), confirming questions are always eligible regardless of this gate.

after_belief_shift.advancing_questions: STILL ELIGIBLE — but deprioritized. Their advancing_vs_confirming_fit score drops to 4 (from 10) in the scoring engine.
after_belief_shift.confirming_questions: NOW ELIGIBLE — scored and surfaced normally. The scoring engine's type_fit dimension gives them 10 points.
after_belief_shift.transition_prompt: The teleprompter displays a one-time prompt: 'Belief shift detected. Confirming questions now available.' In Guided mode, this transitions to Phase 5. In Copilot mode, the momentum card begins suggesting confirming questions.

predictive_queue_integration.description: The predictive queue's confirming_question entries respect this gate. Before belief_shift_detected, PQ entries with response_type='confirming_question' are skipped. After detection, they become active.
predictive_queue_integration.override: PQ entries with response_type='next_step_prompt' are always active — the rep can always lock next steps regardless of belief shift state.

predictive_queue_activation.description: Extends the predictive queue to activate on neutral or ambient buyer statements that don't match any trigger or cluster. Ensures the teleprompter always has something to show.
predictive_queue_activation.activation_rule: When a client speech segment passes the speaker filter (Step 0) but matches NO trigger (Step 1 produces no cluster), AND the segment matches a neutral_statement_pattern or contains no conversation_signal keywords, the predictive queue activates immediately (Step 8) without waiting for Steps 2-7.
predictive_queue_activation.content_surfaced: The first unused entry in the current stage's predictive_queue. For most stages, this is the next unasked discovery question ranked by the priority_scoring engine.
predictive_queue_activation.relationship_to_momentum: In Copilot mode, the momentum card handles directional signals. The neutral-statement activation handles the case where there IS no direction — the buyer is ambient and the system needs to keep the conversation moving.

problem_type_registry.description: A diagnostic layer that infers the buyer's underlying situation from accumulated signals and cluster activity. Problem types sit above individual signals and clusters — they represent compound conditions, not single phrase matches. The teleprompter uses the active problem type to bias move selection toward the most effective response pattern for the diagnosed situation.

relationship_to_existing_systems.signals: Problem types are INFERRED from conversation_signals accumulated in deal_signal_tracking. They do not replace signals.
relationship_to_existing_systems.clusters: Problem types may reference pattern_clusters as evidence. When a cluster fires AND a problem type is active, the problem type's preferred_moves take advisory priority — but the cluster's decision_rules still execute. The problem type biases selection within the cluster's output, it does not override it.
relationship_to_existing_systems.scoring: When a problem type is active, its preferred_moves apply a +5 scoring bonus to DQs that match the problem type's focus. This is a session-local bias, not a permanent change to priority_scoring dimensions.
relationship_to_existing_systems.stage_gating: Problem types respect stage_priority_lock. A problem type cannot surface future-stage content.
relationship_to_existing_systems.predictive_queues: When no cluster fires and a problem type is active, the problem type's preferred_moves guide which predictive_queue entry to surface first.
relationship_to_existing_systems.triggers: Problem types do not modify trigger_conditions or trigger_firing_rules.

inference_logic.description: A problem type becomes active when its activation_threshold is met. Thresholds are defined as signal counts, cluster fire counts, or signal pair detections.
inference_logic.evaluation_frequency: Re-evaluated after every client speech segment that passes the speaker filter.
inference_logic.multiple_active: Multiple problem types can be active simultaneously. When they conflict on preferred_moves, the problem type with the higher priority_weight wins.
inference_logic.decay: A problem type deactivates when its signals haven't been detected for 180 seconds (3 minutes). This prevents stale diagnoses from lingering.
inference_logic.reset: All problem types reset when the meeting stage changes.

problem_types.description: The buyer believes their current process is adequate. They are not in pain — or don't recognize the pain as systemic. The conversation needs to surface hidden risk before any product discussion.
problem_types.activation_threshold: status_quo_confidence OR status_quo_defense detected at least once
problem_types.buyer_posture: Defensive. Comfortable. May be politely dismissive. Often says 'it works' or 'we're fine.' Has not yet connected their daily friction to a structural problem.

preferred_moves.reason: The buyer needs their frame disrupted before they'll engage with discovery. Lead with a reframe that surfaces hidden cost or risk.
After the reframe, ask a question tagged expose_hidden_risk or workflow_fragility. Don't ask about solutions — ask about the process.
If the reframe doesn't land, a story about a firm that felt the same way can crack the defense: 'They said the same thing. Here's what they found when they looked closer.'

avoid_moves.reason: The belief shift hasn't happened yet. Confirming too early sounds presumptuous.
The buyer doesn't feel the pain. ROI math is meaningless until they do.
Pushing for next steps when the buyer is comfortable will end the conversation.

problem_types.description: The buyer's operational process has manual steps, single-person dependencies, or Excel-based calculations that don't scale. They may or may not recognize this as a risk. The conversation should map the fragility before quantifying the cost.
problem_types.buyer_posture: Descriptive. Often matter-of-fact about their process without recognizing the fragility. May say 'it takes a while' or 'our controller handles it' without framing these as problems.

preferred_moves.reason: Keep asking. This is the richest discovery vein. Each question reveals another fragility point.
After 2-3 workflow questions, reframe the accumulating picture: 'So what I'm hearing is that the entire distribution cycle depends on one person and one spreadsheet.'
After the reframe, validate with a proof point: 'Firms with your complexity typically have 15-20 manual handoff points per distribution.'

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer isn't comparing vendors. They're describing operations. Stay in their world.
Don't advance until the full workflow is mapped. Premature advancement leaves pain on the table.

problem_types.description: The buyer cannot clearly identify who owns the waterfall logic, the validation process, or the decision authority. Knowledge is concentrated in one person. This is a key-person risk that the buyer may not recognize.
problem_types.activation_threshold: ownership_unclear detected at least once, OR economic_buyer_missing detected at least once
problem_types.buyer_posture: Uncertain. May deflect with 'our admin handles that' or 'I'd have to check.' The uncertainty itself is the signal — if they can't answer who owns it, nobody owns it clearly.

preferred_moves.reason: Probe deeper on ownership. 'What happens when that person is unavailable?' is the highest-emotion question in the system.
If ownership is unclear, the economic buyer is likely distant. Ask who would need to approve a change.
After the buyer admits uncertainty, reframe: 'That's not a process — that's a dependency. And dependencies are risks.'

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer can't quantify the cost of something they can't describe clearly. Map the ownership first, then quantify.
The buyer hasn't acknowledged the risk yet. Confirming prematurely will feel like leading the witness.

problem_types.description: The economic buyer is not in the room, has not been identified by name, or the champion cannot articulate how the EB evaluates investments. The deal cannot progress to proposal without EB alignment.
problem_types.activation_threshold: economic_buyer_missing AND the current stage is m1-advancing or later
problem_types.buyer_posture: Engaged but limited in authority. May say 'I'd need to run this by my CFO' or 'that's above my pay grade.' The champion is interested but cannot commit.

preferred_moves.reason: Learn the EB's framework before proposing anything. 'When your CFO evaluates an investment like this, what framework do they use?'
Map the decision process. 'What has to happen between today and a signed contract? Walk me through the steps.'
Arm the champion with a story they can retell to the EB. 'When you present this internally, here's how a similar firm framed it.'

avoid_moves.reason: Building ROI without knowing the EB's framework is building a case for the wrong audience.
Don't push for next steps until the EB is identified by name and their evaluation criteria are understood.

problem_types.description: The buyer cannot describe the path from evaluation to signed contract. Procurement, legal, security, and stakeholder alignment are undefined or opaque. The deal will stall in later stages unless the process is mapped now.
problem_types.buyer_posture: Vague about next steps. May say 'we'll figure it out' or 'I'll check internally.' The vagueness is the risk — if the buyer can't describe the process, the process will surprise both sides later.

preferred_moves.reason: Map the full process now. 'Who else needs to see this? What's the approval chain? Is there a procurement or security review?'
Ask about paper process. 'Have you been through a software procurement before? What did that look like?'
Once the process is mapped, lock the first step with a date. 'Based on what you've described, the logical next step is X. Can we lock that in for the week of Y?'

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer isn't defending the status quo — they're uncertain about the path forward. A reframe won't help. A process map will.
Evidence about the product doesn't help when the obstacle is procurement ambiguity.

problem_types.description: The buyer is interested in the financial case but hasn't been guided through a structured quantification. They may have asked about cost, mentioned headcount pressure, or expressed interest in 'the numbers.' The opportunity is to channel this interest into a rigorous activity-by-activity cost estimation.
problem_types.activation_threshold: roi_curiosity OR (pricing_discussion AND the buyer is in M4 or later)
problem_types.buyer_posture: Commercially engaged. May ask 'what does this cost?' or 'how do we justify this internally?' This is a positive signal — they're thinking about buying, not just learning.

preferred_moves.reason: Walk through activity-by-activity estimation. Hours → dollars → scaling projection. The ROI construction prompts guide this sequence.
preferred_moves.stage_gate: M4 only. If earlier, use reframe instead.

preferred_moves.reason: If in M1 or M2, don't answer the pricing question directly. Reframe: 'Before we get into numbers, I want to make sure we're solving the right problem.'
If the buyer is curious about ROI but hasn't described the pain in enough detail, redirect to diagnosis: 'Let's build the case together. How many hours does your team spend per quarter on X?'

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer is in commercial mode, not comparison mode. Stay in the ROI lane.
Don't confirm the value case until the numbers are built. Confirming without data feels hollow.

problem_types.description: The buyer is actively comparing Maybern against one or more alternatives. This may be a formal evaluation (RFP, bake-off) or informal (they spoke to another vendor). The conversation needs to shape how the buyer evaluates, not just what they evaluate.
problem_types.activation_threshold: vendor_comparison detected at least once, OR internal_build detected at least once
problem_types.buyer_posture: Evaluative. May name competitors explicitly or hint ('we also looked at something else'). May compare features, pricing, or implementation timelines. The risk is that the buyer uses someone else's criteria to evaluate Maybern.

preferred_moves.reason: Address the competitor directly using canonical positioning from vendor_asset_integration. Never attack — differentiate.
After positioning, shape the evaluation criteria: 'What matters most to you in this decision — is it waterfall depth, implementation speed, or long-term flexibility?'
If the buyer is comparing, a story about a firm that evaluated both and chose Maybern is powerful.

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer is in comparison mode, not quantification mode. Shape the criteria first, then build ROI.
Don't reframe the competitor out of existence. Acknowledge, differentiate, then redirect to the buyer's actual needs.

problem_types.description: The buyer's operational environment involves multiple disconnected systems — GL, admin platform, Excel models, investor portal, data warehouse, and BI tools. The pain isn't any single tool — it's the integration gaps between them.
problem_types.activation_threshold: 3+ of common_signals detected within 180 seconds (buyer describing a multi-system environment)
problem_types.buyer_posture: Descriptive and often resigned. May say 'we use X for this and Y for that and then reconcile in Excel.' The resignation is the opportunity — they've accepted the friction as normal.

preferred_moves.reason: Map the full stack. 'Walk me through the complete data flow — from GL to investor notice. What touches what?'
After mapping, reframe the total picture: 'You've described five systems and twelve handoff points. The question isn't whether each tool works — it's what the total integration cost is.'
A story about a firm that consolidated from a similar stack to Maybern makes the future state tangible.

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer isn't comparing Maybern to one competitor — they're describing an ecosystem. Position Maybern as the layer that connects things, not a replacement for any one tool.

problem_types.description: The buyer acknowledges the problem but resists changing. This may be fear of implementation disruption, political risk of championing a new vendor, or simple inertia. The belief shift has partially landed — they see the risk — but they haven't committed to action.
problem_types.activation_threshold: (status_quo_defense OR timeline_delay) AND at least 3 advancing questions have been asked in the current stage (meaning discovery has happened but commitment hasn't followed)
problem_types.buyer_posture: Aware but passive. May say 'let me think about it,' 'not the right time,' or 'send me materials.' They understand the problem intellectually but haven't felt the urgency emotionally.

preferred_moves.reason: Test where the resistance lives. 'You've described real pain today. What would need to be true for your team to prioritize solving it?'
Reframe inaction as a choice with consequences: 'Every quarter you run the current process is another quarter of the risk you just described.'
A story about a firm that delayed and what it cost them — or a firm that moved quickly and what they gained.

avoid_moves.reason: More discovery won't help. The buyer already knows the problem. They need a reason to act NOW.
If the buyer is resisting, more numbers won't change their mind. Urgency and social proof are more effective than math at this stage.

problem_types.description: The buyer has internalized the problem AND is thinking about the future state. They ask forward-looking questions: 'What would this look like for us?' or 'How long to get live?' This is the signal that the belief shift has landed and the buyer is ready to move toward commitment.
problem_types.activation_threshold: (fund_growth OR scale_discussion) AND belief_shift_detected = true (from advancing_confirming_discipline)
problem_types.buyer_posture: Forward-looking. Engaged. Asking about implementation, integration, and what life looks like post-go-live. This is the most positive buyer posture in the system.

preferred_moves.reason: Confirm the vision: 'Based on what we've discussed, do you see what live on Maybern looks like for your team?'
Paint the future state with evidence: 'A firm that went live six months ago told us their controller now spends Monday mornings on analysis instead of reconciliation.'
The buyer is ready. Lock it: 'Can we schedule the tailored demo with your leadership team for the week of X?'

avoid_moves.reason: The buyer already sees the problem. Reframing now would feel redundant and condescending.
Discovery is done. More questions would feel like the rep isn't listening to the buyer's forward momentum.

problem_types.description: The champion is shielding the economic buyer from vendor access. They want to validate internally before involving leadership. The risk is that the EB never hears the value story directly, the champion's internal pitch is diluted, and the deal dies without the EB ever engaging. The rep must learn the EB's framework, coach the champion's internal presentation, or find a path to get the EB in the room.
problem_types.activation_threshold: Any champion_shielding or eb_gatekeeping signal detected, OR pc-015 fires
problem_types.buyer_posture: Engaged but protective. The champion is interested — they wouldn't be gatekeeping if they weren't. But they're managing risk: political risk, reputation risk, or time risk. They want to be sure before they spend their internal capital.

preferred_moves.reason: Learn what the EB cares about. 'When your CFO evaluates an investment like this, what's the framework — headcount comparison, risk reduction, or something else?'
Map what would unlock the gate. 'What would need to be true for you to feel confident bringing this to leadership?'
Show a firm where early EB involvement accelerated the deal. 'A similar firm brought their CFO into the second meeting. She asked three questions, and the evaluation moved twice as fast.'

avoid_moves.reason: Pushing for next steps while the gate is up will end the conversation. The champion isn't blocking — they're preparing. Help them prepare.
Building ROI without knowing the EB's framework builds the wrong case. Learn the framework first.

problem_types.description: The purchasing decision involves multiple stakeholders, committees, or consensus-driven processes with hidden veto players, internal blockers, and deal vampires. The rep must map the full political structure — who can kill the deal, whose opinion carries the most weight, who resists change, and who needs to be engaged early — before the deal enters committee.
problem_types.activation_threshold: Any committee_complexity or multi_stakeholder_decision signal detected, OR pc-016 fires
problem_types.buyer_posture: Informational but revealing. The buyer is describing their process, not defending it. The danger is that the rep accepts the process description as fixed — 'okay, so it goes to committee' — without mapping WHO is on the committee, WHAT they care about, and WHERE the deal could die.

preferred_moves.reason: Map the political landscape. Use committee_mapping_questions to identify veto players, influencers, and the true power center.
Learn the EB's framework. 'What matters most to leadership' surfaces the criteria the champion's internal pitch must address.
Reframe early engagement as risk reduction, not presumptuousness. Use committee_reframes to transition into discovery.

avoid_moves.reason: Pushing for next steps before the committee is mapped will produce a weak proposal that dies in committee.
Building ROI without knowing what the committee values builds the wrong case.

scoring_integration.description: When a problem type is active, its preferred_moves apply a +5 scoring bonus to DQs that match the problem type's principle_preference or meddpicc_preference. This biases the scoring engine toward questions that address the diagnosed situation.
scoring_integration.mechanism: The bonus is applied to the principle_match dimension (Step 5) or meddpicc_relevance dimension (Step 6) of the priority_scoring engine. It is session-local and does not modify the stored scoring dimensions.
scoring_integration.avoid_move_penalty: DQs that match an avoid_move's content type receive a -5 scoring penalty. This pushes them down the ranked list without excluding them entirely.
scoring_integration.example: pt-002 (workflow_fragility) is active. DQs tagged workflow_fragility get +5 on principle_match. DQs that would surface as confirming_questions get -5 on type_fit. Net effect: workflow discovery questions rise to the top, confirming questions drop.

schema.problem_type_history: Map of problem_type_id → { activated_at, deactivated_at, activation_count, triggering_signals[] }
schema.dominant_problem_type: The active problem_type with the highest priority_weight. Used for tie-breaking.

reporting_stress_signals.description: Extended signal definitions for time-based reporting stress. These signals supplement the existing conversation_signals (workflow_fragility, audit_risk) with more granular detection of reporting cycle pressure. They map to the same cluster (pc-005) and the same signal_bridge targets.

quarter_end_pressure.description: Buyer describes quarter-end as a stress point — painful, hectic, scramble, fire drill.

month_end_pressure.description: Buyer describes month-end close as a stress point.

reporting_cycle_stress.description: Buyer describes the reporting cycle itself as burdensome — deadlines, backlog, workload spikes.

audit_preparation_stress.description: Buyer describes audit prep as a distinct pain point — gathering data, pulling reports, formatting deliverables.

temporal_workload_spike.description: Buyer describes periodic workload spikes — overtime, weekends, all-hands, busy season.

detection_integration.description: These signals are detected by the same mechanism as existing conversation_signals — keyword matching on speaker-filtered buyer speech. When any of these signals are detected, they activate pc-005 (workflow_fragility) via the signal_bridge, exactly as workflow_fragility and audit_risk do today.
detection_integration.cluster_activation: Any reporting_stress_signal detected → pc-005 fires with recommended_move = advancing_question, preferred_principle = workflow_fragility
detection_integration.predictive_queue_suppression: When any reporting_stress_signal is detected, the predictive queue does NOT activate. The cluster match takes priority (Steps 1-7 in the decision engine).
detection_integration.problem_type_activation: All reporting_stress_signals activate pt-002 (workflow_fragility) in the problem_type_registry.

internal_build_signals.description: Extended signal definitions for internal build scenarios. These signals supplement the existing internal_build conversation_signal with granular detection of custom tool development, homegrown systems, and internal engineering dependencies. They map to pc-004 (Internal Build) as primary and pc-005 (Workflow Fragility) as secondary when the internal tool creates process fragility.

internal_tool.description: Buyer describes any tool built by their own team — scripts, dashboards, models, workflows.

homegrown_system.description: Buyer uses 'homegrown' or similar language to describe a proprietary system.

custom_solution.description: Buyer describes a custom-built solution — custom tool, custom reporting, custom model.

built_internally.description: Buyer explicitly states they built something themselves — any 'we built' variant.

internal_workflow_tool.description: Buyer describes a tool built for a specific workflow — narrower than a full system.

internal_script.description: Buyer describes a script or macro they use for calculations — often the most fragile internal build.

internal_dashboard.description: Buyer describes a dashboard or reporting layer they built in-house.

custom_reporting_tool.description: Buyer describes a reporting tool built internally for investor or fund reporting.

internal_process_tool.description: Buyer describes a tool built for a specific operational process — waterfall, allocation, reconciliation.

engineering_dependency.description: Buyer reveals that maintaining the internal tool depends on specific engineering or IT resources.

detection_integration.cluster_activation: Any internal_build_signal detected → pc-004 fires with recommended_move = reframe, preferred_principle = expose_hidden_risk. If the signal also maps to pc-005, both clusters may match — priority_weight resolves which surfaces first.
detection_integration.predictive_queue_suppression: When any internal_build_signal is detected, the predictive queue does NOT activate. The cluster match takes priority.
detection_integration.problem_type_activation: All internal_build_signals activate pt-007 (competitor_evaluation) when the internal build is framed as an alternative, or pt-002 (workflow_fragility) when the internal build creates process fragility.

admin_dependency_signals.description: Extended signal definitions for administrator dependency scenarios. These signals detect when a buyer describes operational tasks being performed by a fund administrator, outsourced provider, or external team. They map to pc-008 (Admin Dependency) and activate problem types pt-001 (status_quo_defense) and pt-003 (ownership_unclear).

fund_admin.description: Buyer mentions a fund administrator by role (not by name). General reference to having an admin.

administrator_dependency.description: Buyer reveals that the admin is the primary performer of a critical operational task — not just a service provider, but a dependency.

admin_managed_process.description: Buyer describes a specific process managed by the admin — broader than reporting, includes reconciliation, calculations, and book maintenance.

admin_runs_reports.description: Buyer describes the admin producing reports — quarterly, monthly, investor, or performance reports.

admin_handles_reporting.description: Buyer uses 'handles' language for reporting — implies delegation with limited visibility.

admin_handles_that.description: Buyer uses the exact dismissive phrase 'the admin handles that' — signals low visibility into the process.

admin_prepares_reports.description: Buyer describes the admin preparing deliverables — implies the GP receives but doesn't produce.

admin_generates_reports.description: Buyer describes the admin generating output — slightly more technical than 'prepares.'

admin_maintains_books.description: Buyer describes the admin maintaining the books of record — deepest form of dependency.
detection_phrases: the books are with the admin

admin_provides_reports.description: Buyer describes receiving reports from the admin — implies the GP is a consumer, not a producer.
detection_phrases: we get it from the admin

admin_runs_calculations.description: Buyer describes the admin performing calculations — waterfall, allocation, distribution.

outsourced_reporting.description: Buyer describes reporting being outsourced — to an admin, BPO, or external team.

outsourced_financials.description: Buyer describes financial operations being outsourced broadly.

detection_integration.cluster_activation: Any admin_dependency_signal detected → pc-008 fires with recommended_move = reframe, preferred_principle = reframe_status_quo.
detection_integration.predictive_queue_suppression: When any admin_dependency_signal is detected, the predictive queue does NOT activate. The cluster match takes priority.
detection_integration.problem_type_activation: All admin_dependency_signals activate pt-001 (status_quo_defense) and pt-003 (ownership_unclear). The dominant problem type's preferred_moves guide question selection toward ownership clarification and workflow visibility.
detection_integration.chained_question_bias: When pc-008 fires with an admin signal, the response_question_chaining system biases the chained advancing question toward ownership_unclear and workflow_fragility tagged DQs — specifically questions about visibility into the admin's process.

status_quo_defense_signals.description: Extended signal definitions for status quo defense scenarios. Three categories: comfort language (buyer expresses satisfaction), change aversion (buyer resists change due to effort/politics/cost), and minimizing pain (buyer acknowledges imperfection but frames it as acceptable). Each phrase classified as direct_trigger (fires pc-001 alone) or supporting_signal_only (strengthens but cannot fire alone).

direct_trigger: we've got a good thing going
not sure leadership wants to change
hard to get people to adopt something new
change management is a nightmare here
nobody wants to learn a new system
takes effort but we handle it
annoying but we deal with it
it's a pain but we've figured it out
could be better but it's not broken
not the most efficient but it works
tedious but we get through it
it's a workaround but it does the job

synonym_normalization.description: A normalization layer that expands the teleprompter's ability to detect buyer signals by mapping alternate words, business-meaning equivalents, and conversational phrasings to existing cluster signals. Three tiers: direct_aliases (same word, different form — act as triggers), concept_aliases (different word, same business meaning — usually supporting signals), and pattern_equivalents (conversational phrasings that imply the signal family — strengthen detection when paired with other signals).

detection_behavior.how_it_works: Before the decision engine runs Step 1 (cluster matching), the normalization layer scans the buyer's speech for aliases and equivalents. Any match is translated to the canonical signal, which is then matched against cluster buyer_language_examples as if the buyer had used the original term.
detection_behavior.priority: The normalization layer runs BEFORE cluster matching but AFTER speaker detection (Step 0). It does not bypass any existing engine step.
detection_behavior.false_positive_control: direct_aliases fire independently. concept_aliases require at least one other signal in the same category to fire (they strengthen, not trigger). pattern_equivalents never fire alone — they only boost confidence when a cluster is already partially matched.

classification_rules.direct_alias: Alternate word or short phrase that means the SAME thing as the canonical term. Unambiguous in any context. Can trigger a cluster on its own. Example: 'workbook' is a direct_alias for 'spreadsheet.'
classification_rules.concept_alias: Word or phrase that implies the same business meaning even if the literal wording differs. Requires context to be unambiguous. Usually acts as a supporting signal. Example: 'side file' is a concept_alias for 'spreadsheet' — it implies a spreadsheet but could mean other things.
classification_rules.pattern_equivalent: A conversational phrasing that implies the signal family but is too indirect to trigger alone. Strengthens cluster detection when paired with other signals. Example: 'outside the system' is a pattern_equivalent for spreadsheet_dependency — it implies data living somewhere unofficial, but could mean many things.

pattern_equivalents: we have a version of that
the source of truth is actually
by the time we see it
we trust them to get it right
they've been doing it for years
we wouldn't know how to do it ourselves
we're pretty removed from that process

aliases: we found a way around it

pattern_equivalents: if you change one thing everything breaks
it works but I don't know why
it's only a matter of time
we're the ones who make it all work together
there's no single place to see everything
depends on which system you look at
every system tells a slightly different story
we spend more time moving data than using it
we've been down this road before
we looked at this a few years ago
the last time we tried to change something
promise me you're not just another vendor
those two weeks are basically lost

aliases: we'd have to figure that out

pattern_equivalents: that's a good question — I'm not sure
a few people would need to weigh in
it's not really anyone's job specifically
it kind of falls between teams
we thought we could do it ourselves
it seemed simpler to just build it
our CTO wanted to keep it in-house
the team preferred to own the code
it started as a side project
it was supposed to be temporary

eb_gatekeeping_signals.description: Signal definitions for economic buyer gatekeeping scenarios. Detects when a champion is delaying or preventing EB involvement. Maps to pc-015 and pt-012.

champion_shielding.description: Champion explicitly delays EB involvement — 'let me build the case,' 'not ready to bring in the CFO,' 'don't want to waste their time.'
detection_phrases: don't want to bring leadership in
don't want to waste their time
make sure this is worth their time

champion_as_filter.description: Champion positions themselves as the intermediary — 'I'll present this,' 'let me take this back,' 'I'll make the case.'
detection_phrases: take this back to the team
loop them in when the time is right
bring them in at the right time
know when it's time to involve

proof_prerequisite.description: Champion requires proof before allowing EB access — 'need more proof,' 'convince myself first,' 'show me more.'
detection_phrases: see it work with our data

political_navigation.description: Champion signals political complexity around EB engagement — 'it's political,' 'leadership is skeptical,' 'need to frame this right.'
detection_phrases: seen a lot of these pitches

detection_integration.cluster_activation: Any eb_gatekeeping signal → pc-015 fires with recommended_move = advancing_question, preferred_principle = shift_ownership_to_economic_buyer.
detection_integration.predictive_queue_suppression: When any eb_gatekeeping signal is detected, the predictive queue does NOT activate. pc-015 takes priority.
detection_integration.differentiation_from_pc011: pc-011 (Executive Alignment) fires when the buyer REFERENCES the EB: 'my CFO would need to see this.' pc-015 fires when the buyer SHIELDS the EB: 'let me build the case first.' The distinction is agency — pc-011 is informational, pc-015 is protective. If both match, pc-015 wins (higher priority_weight: 9 vs 7).

buying_committee_signals.description: Signal definitions for buying committee risk scenarios. Detects committee references, multi-stakeholder consensus language, hidden veto player indicators, staged leadership involvement, internal blocker signals, and deal velocity risk. Maps to pc-016 and pt-013.

committee_decision.description: Buyer references a formal committee or group decision body.
detection_phrases: committee would need to sign off

staged_leadership_exposure.description: Buyer describes a phased process where leadership is involved only after internal validation.

multi_stakeholder_consensus.description: Buyer reveals that multiple people or departments must agree.

functional_weigh_in.description: Buyer names specific functions that would need to review.
detection_phrases: operations would need to weigh in

hidden_veto_player.description: Buyer reveals someone who can unilaterally block the deal.
detection_phrases: if they push back it's done

internal_review_prerequisite.description: Buyer wants team review before proceeding.
detection_phrases: review this with the team first

stakeholder_resistance.description: Buyer signals that some stakeholders will resist or are skeptical.

deal_velocity_risk.description: Buyer signals that the decision process is slow or has multiple approval layers.
detection_phrases: takes time to get everyone comfortable

detection_integration.cluster_activation: Any buying_committee signal → pc-016 fires with recommended_move = advancing_question, preferred_principle = decision_process_visibility.
detection_integration.predictive_queue_suppression: When any buying_committee signal is detected, the predictive queue does NOT activate. pc-016 takes priority.

differentiation.vs_pc015: pc-015 fires when the champion SHIELDS the EB. pc-016 fires when the buyer reveals COMMITTEE COMPLEXITY — multiple stakeholders, consensus requirements, veto players. A buyer can shield AND reveal committee risk in the same conversation — both clusters can be active simultaneously.
differentiation.vs_pc011: pc-011 fires when the buyer REFERENCES the EB. pc-016 fires when the buyer describes the POLITICAL LANDSCAPE around the decision. pc-011 is informational. pc-016 is structural.
differentiation.vs_pc013: pc-013 fires on procurement/legal process language. pc-016 fires on people and politics language. pc-013 asks 'what are the steps?' pc-016 asks 'who are the players?'

gong_question_routing.trigger_context: buyer mentions admin cost or fee offset
gong_question_routing.question: Do you know roughly how much you're paying for that each year? So we can start building that into the business case.
gong_question_routing.effectiveness: Unlocked $60K quantification in a single question

gong_question_routing.trigger_context: buyer mentions multiple workflows or review cycles
gong_question_routing.question: Of these workflows, what takes you the longest amount of time as the reviewer?
gong_question_routing.effectiveness: Surfaced waterfall as the #1 pain in 10 seconds

gong_question_routing.trigger_context: buyer mentions advisory board or external consultants
gong_question_routing.question: Who are those finance advisors? Are they close to the decision?
gong_question_routing.effectiveness: Opened the entire political map — revealed ex-TPG network

gong_question_routing.question: Has this been socialized at all internally? How do you describe this initiative when you talk about it?
gong_question_routing.effectiveness: Revealed champion's internal framing — 'Q3/Q4 initiative' — giving the rep the real timeline

new_intel: Osso pays Gen II for quarterly reporting AND a separate portal (Sensor) at $60K/year for two funds
Gen II using Sage as GL — information flows: Excel spreadsheets → Juniper Square portal
Gen II had 30% employee turnover over 3 years (cited by Teresa at Osso call)
Key buyer concern: 'I feel like we're even more dependent on those people' — the more stable the admin team, the deeper the dependency risk
Gen_II.effective_positioning: We don't replace Gen II. We replace the spreadsheet your team uses to check Gen II's work. And when you're ready, you feed Gen II the data instead of the other way around.

new_intel: Osso uses JSQ for investor portal and notices
Maybern has a direct export in Juniper Square format — 'we have an export from Maybern that goes directly into Juniper Square format'
Buyers see JSQ as a notice/portal tool, not a calculation engine — no conflict with Maybern
GP portal product used by Osso at $60K/year
Immediate cost offset opportunity — Maybern replaces Sensor functionality

gong_enrichment_reframes.text: What gets you here isn't what's gonna get you to the next set of funds. The question is whether to invest in the infrastructure now when you have time, or scramble when fund three forces the issue.

We don't do big bang. You use Maybern as a validation engine against your admin for two or three quarters. No process change. No retraining. Your team keeps doing exactly what they do today — they just have a second check that's automated.

The phased approach means this doesn't compete with your other initiatives. It runs alongside what you're already doing — your team validates against the admin as part of their normal review cycle. No new process until you decide you're ready.
Before we talk about what Maybern costs — how much are you paying your admin for their portal alone? That's usually the first cost offset.

Renamed product from Teleprompter to IVY (Intelligent Voice for You). Updated manifest.

key_findings: Buyers describe admin dependency as delegation ('they do it, we review it'), not frustration. The teleprompter must detect passive operational descriptions, not just complaint language.

The 'it's not broken' defense is almost never stated in those words. Instead, buyers use future-state framing: 'this is more about planning for future state' (Osso). The teleprompter's status quo cluster needs temporal/planning language.

Champion gatekeeping appears through resource-competition framing: 'we have other data automation initiatives' and 'it can't be too much at one time for three or four people' — NOT 'I need to build the case.' The gatekeeping is operational, not political.
The most effective rep move observed is the 'phased implementation' reframe — showing the buyer they don't have to switch all at once. This neutralizes implementation fear better than any rebuttal.
Credibility stories from other clients (Teresa's prior experience) are dramatically more effective than Maybern-told stories. Peer voices outperform vendor voices every time.

Pricing conversations happen naturally when the buyer asks about admin fee reduction. The best deals have a built-in cost offset narrative: 'we can reduce Gen II's scope = cost savings that fund Maybern.'
The decision process is NEVER a clean committee. It's an informal network: advisory boards, ex-TPG consultants, CFO dinner groups. The teleprompter must detect informal authority structures.
biggest_language_gaps: STATUS QUO: 'it's not like there's anything that's not working... this is more about planning for future state' — NOT detected by current pc-001
ADMIN DEPENDENCY: 'people not as close to it are just like, well, your fund admin does all that' — third-party status quo defense, not buyer's own
CHAMPION RESOURCE GATING: 'we have some other data automation things as a firm like priorities' — NOT detected as gatekeeping
INFORMAL AUTHORITY: 'we have external advisory board... if they're kind of on board, we can collectively make a recommendation to Olivia' — invisible committee
WATERFALL AS PAIN CENTER: 'it's a huge excel file which we've shared with your team that is subject to so much human error' — this is THE pain statement and it's deeply specific
SHADOW VALIDATION: 'we still maintain like kind of our full shadow waterfall and they do it as well. And then we rec to each other' — Catalio describing parallel books
biggest_opportunities: Add 'planning for future state' / 'setting us up for' language to pc-001 as a SOFT status quo variant
Add 'other priorities' / 'other initiatives' / 'can't be too much at one time' to pc-015 as resource-competition gatekeeping
Add informal authority detection: 'advisory board' / 'external advisors' / 'consultants who used to be in our position' to pc-016
Add waterfall-as-Excel-model language to pc-005: 'huge excel file subject to human error' / 'rolling excel tabs' / 'multiple rounds of review'
Add admin fee reduction as ROI trigger: 'fee reduction from them' / 'cost offset' / 'drastically reducing the workload for Gen II'
top_strategic_risks: Champion burnout from resource competition: Jamie at Osso explicitly said 'it can't be too much at one time for three or four people.' This is the most common stall mechanism.
Invisible committee: Osso's decision process runs through ex-TPG advisory board members who are NOT employees. The teleprompter currently has no detection for external advisory authority.
The 'planning for future state' frame lets the buyer acknowledge value without committing to action. This is the most dangerous status quo variant because it feels like progress.

rebuttal_additions.reframe: What gets you here isn't what's gonna get you to the next set of funds. And if you want to not have to hire so many people or expand the cost with the admin, it's an investment in the future while mitigating risk today.
rebuttal_additions.effectiveness: HIGH — Jamie responded 'yeah, yeah, yeah' and opened up about internal dynamics

rebuttal_additions.reframe: We don't want to do a big bang where you have to switch over to Maybern. It's really more of a gradual phased implementation. You use Maybern as a validation engine against your admin.

credibility_story_additions.title: CFO tracking admin errors to build the case
credibility_story_additions.text: We have an 11 billion dollar fund manager who's been tracking Maybern against their admin for the last couple quarters. They're logging every discrepancy. Their plan is to go to the admin in Q3 and say: here are all the issues we found, we already have this automated, and here's what we want the operating model to look like going forward.

credibility_story_additions.title: Slowed hiring because the system automated the volume
credibility_story_additions.text: At my previous firm we had about 50 funds doing 10 to 15 distributions a quarter. Originally we were a team of two. Once we built out the system, we actually slowed down hiring. We weren't rolling excel tabs anymore because that takes forever manually. It let us scale the team we had.

credibility_story_additions.title: Admin fee reduction after proving the system
credibility_story_additions.text: After two or three quarters of validation, we got so comfortable with the system that we started feeding the admin the distributions instead of them calculating. They did a high-level check. That saved them a lot of time. We were able to see a reduction in fees based on that.

v4_runtime_layers.purpose: Normalize raw buyer utterances into structured meaning.
Track current buyer belief, required next belief, and belief-gap progression by meeting stage.
Select the next move based on buyer meaning, stage, belief state, and Corporate Visions rules.
Generate bounded natural-language variants from approved logic patterns and content assets.
Track stakeholder completeness, silent influencer risk, skeptic signals, and deal-vampire risk.
Track EB identification, access risk, and champion gatekeeping patterns.
Track live progression risk such as stall risk, status quo risk, committee risk, and EB access risk.

semantic_interpreter_schema_v1.purpose: Normalizes raw buyer utterances into structured meaning for downstream belief-state tracking, Corporate Visions move planning, MEDDPICC routing, and deal-risk updates.
design_principles: Interpret meaning, not just phrases
Separate semantic understanding from content rendering
Use controlled vocabularies where possible
Allow multi-label outputs for ambiguity
Preserve evidence for auditability
Support stage-aware interpretation
Support Corporate Visions planning without requiring manual phrase enumeration

general: Interpret current turn in context of last 3 to 5 buyer-relevant turns.
Prefer meaning over lexical match when determining posture and problem type.
Allow multiple problem types and buying dynamics when evidence supports overlap.
Do not infer strong urgency from curiosity alone.
Treat timing objections as ambiguous until context resolves whether timing is real or cover.
Preserve ambiguity in disambiguation notes rather than forcing false precision.
corporate_visions: If buyer defends current process while admitting friction, interpret as defended_with_admitted_friction.
If buyer connects operational issue to audit, LP trust, leadership, growth, or key person dependency, escalate toward risk_acknowledged or problem_escalated.
If buyer asks solution questions before pain is fully owned, capture interest but constrain downstream planner from premature pitching.

v4_merge_policy.safe_edit_rule: New runtime objects and planner layers should live in the overlay until validated. Existing v3 content assets remain source of truth unless explicitly replaced.

v4_upgrade_goals: Reduce dependence on manually enumerated trigger phrases
Interpret buyer meaning and nuance in context
Make Corporate Visions the runtime planning system
Track belief shifts explicitly by stage
Improve committee and economic buyer detection
Support more natural response composition without methodology drift

posture_assessment.description: Classifies the buyer's current conversational posture from accumulated signal state. Runs locally after every client speech segment. No API required. Posture classification feeds into move_evaluation to bias card selection toward moves appropriate for the buyer's current state.
posture_assessment.processing_model: Rule-based classification against session signal state. Evaluates after every client turn that passes speaker_filter. Runs in <5ms.

posture_states.label: Open
posture_states.description: Buyer is engaged, asking questions, sharing information freely. No defensiveness detected.

classification_rules.required: No status_quo_defense signals AND no status_quo_confidence signals in last 3 turns
classification_rules.supporting: Buyer has asked 1+ questions back OR named specific people/processes OR volunteered pain without prompting

posture_states.label: Curious
posture_states.description: Buyer is interested but non-committal. Asking surface questions. Hasn't revealed pain or process details yet.

classification_rules.required: Session is in first 5 minutes OR fewer than 3 total signals accumulated
classification_rules.supporting: Buyer responses are short, neutral-statement patterns detected, no defensiveness but no depth either

posture_states.confidence_formula: base 0.5 + 0.1 per neutral-statement match in last 2 turns (max 0.8)

posture_states.label: Defensive
posture_states.description: Buyer is protecting their current process. Active resistance to the idea that something is wrong. Status quo defense is dominant.

classification_rules.required: status_quo_defense OR status_quo_confidence detected 2+ times AND no pain acknowledgment detected
classification_rules.supporting: change_aversion signal present, buyer using minimizing language, no workflow_fragility signals despite process discussion

posture_states.label: Defensive but Cracking
posture_states.description: Buyer defends their process but keeps describing friction. They say 'it works' but then describe manual steps, workarounds, or dependencies. The defense and the evidence contradict each other.

classification_rules.required: (status_quo_defense OR status_quo_confidence >= 1) AND (workflow_fragility OR manual_process OR excel_dependency >= 1) co-occurring in session
