level_2.question: That's great it's working today — when was the last time the team felt real pressure during a quarter-end? What caused it?
If you think about the highest-complexity fund in your portfolio — is that one running on the same process, or does it have its own workaround?

trees.parent_question: How would you describe your relationship with your fund admin — what's working and what isn't?

level_2.question: When something isn't working with the admin — who absorbs that on your side?

level_3.question: How much of [person]'s time goes into managing the admin relationship vs. doing higher-value work?
If the admin delivered perfectly every time, what would [person] do with that freed-up time?

level_2.question: What's the turnaround time when you ask the admin for something off-cycle — like a scenario analysis or a custom report?

level_3.question: When your deal team needs that number faster than the admin can deliver, what do they do in the meantime?
Has there been a moment where you needed numbers from the admin and the timing just didn't work?

level_2.question: Sounds like the relationship is strong — do you ever find yourself double-checking their numbers anyway, just to be safe?
Even when the admin is performing well — how dependent are you on specific people on their team? Have you experienced turnover on their side?

trees.parent_question: If I asked your team to show me every manual override made in the last distribution cycle, how quickly could they produce that list?

level_2.question: When an override is made, is there a log that captures who changed it, what it was before, and the justification?

level_3.question: If your auditor asked for that log tomorrow, how would you reconstruct it?
How confident are you that no override from the last year fell through the cracks?
How many overrides happen in a typical quarter — are we talking 5 or 50?
Who reviews the overrides for accuracy before they flow into the final distribution?

level_2.question: That's great — does the system that tracks them capture the 'why' behind each override, or just the 'what'?
When your auditor tests overrides, do they sample or do they test all of them?

trees.parent_question: How many of your fund structures have side-letter economics that live outside your primary Excel model?

level_2.question: For those [X] funds with side-letter economics — where does that logic actually live? Separate tabs, separate files, or someone's institutional knowledge?

level_3.question: When a new LP comes in with a custom provision, how long does it take to model that and validate it won't break the existing calculation?
Has a side-letter provision ever been missed in a distribution — where the economics weren't applied correctly?

level_2.question: When you reconcile the primary model against the side-letter adjustments, is that a manual process?

level_3.question: How much time does that reconciliation add to each distribution cycle?
As you raise the next fund with potentially more side letters, does that reconciliation time grow linearly — or worse?

level_2.question: Even if they're all in one model today — when you add a new LP with custom economics, how does that get incorporated without risking the existing calculations?
Have your LPs ever questioned whether their specific side-letter terms were correctly reflected in a distribution?

trees.parent_question: How long does the full process take from decision to funds out the door?

level_2.question: Of those [X] days, how much is actual calculation vs. review, reconciliation, and approval?

level_3.question: So [Y]% of the time is review and reconciliation — what would it mean if that step took an hour instead of [X] days?
During that review window, are your LPs waiting — or does the communication go out before the review is complete?

level_2.question: Has that timeline ever slipped — and what caused it?

level_3.question: When it slipped, did the LPs notice? How did that affect the relationship?
What was the root cause — data issue, calculation error, or approval bottleneck?

level_2.question: What does that timeline look like during peak season — when you're running distributions across multiple funds simultaneously?

level_3.question: During peak, does quality stay the same — or does speed start winning over accuracy?
What's the overtime situation during those periods — and is that sustainable?

trees.parent_question: Has a delayed or corrected distribution ever created a trust issue with an LP?

level_2.question: How did the LP respond — and what did it take to recover the relationship?

level_3.question: Did that LP bring it up again in subsequent conversations — or during re-up discussions?
Do you think that experience affected their commitment to the next fund?

level_2.question: Was it a one-time thing, or has there been a pattern of corrections going out?

level_3.question: At [X] corrections per year, what does that signal to your LP base about operational maturity?
If an LP advisory committee reviewed your correction history, how would that conversation go?

level_2.question: That's good — but when you think about the next fund raise, how important is operational credibility to the LPs you're targeting?
Even without a correction, have you ever had an LP ask for the detailed calculation behind their distribution — and how smooth was that conversation?

trees.parent_question: [Signal detected: buyer mentions building something internally or having a custom tool]

level_2.question: Who built it — and are they still at the firm?

level_3.question: If [builder] left tomorrow, who could modify the logic or fix a bug?
Is [builder] spending their time maintaining this tool, or building the next version of it?
How much of [builder]'s time goes into this vs. their actual job function?
So the person who built it is gone — who maintains it now, and how comfortable are they making changes?
When was the last time someone made a structural change to the tool — and how confident was the team in the result?

level_2.question: When the fund structures change — new waterfall tiers, new side-letter provisions — how quickly can the tool adapt?

level_3.question: If that change takes [X] weeks every time, what's the total engineering cost per year just to keep up with new fund structures?
Have you ever had to launch a fund before the tool was ready — and what did you fall back to?
That's impressive it adapts well — when your auditor reviews the tool's logic, can they follow it independently, or does someone need to walk them through it?

level_2.question: Can your auditors trace a calculation from the tool all the way back to the LPA terms and source data?

level_3.question: If the answer is no — how are you satisfying audit requirements today?
Has your auditor flagged the internal tool as a control risk in any of their reports?

trees.parent_question: Have you looked at solutions for this before — and if so, what's kept you from moving forward?

level_2.question: What specifically didn't work — was it the product, the price, the implementation timeline, or something else?

level_3.question: Knowing what you know now, what would need to be different about the next solution you evaluate?
Did the team lose appetite for solving this after that experience, or is there still urgency?

level_2.question: Who was involved in that evaluation, and are the same people at the table this time?

level_3.question: Is [person] supportive of looking at this again, or do they carry baggage from the last attempt?
What would make this evaluation different from the last one in [person]'s eyes?

level_2.question: What's different now that made you open to this conversation — did something change in the business or the team?
When you think about evaluating something like this, what would the non-negotiable requirements be?

trees.parent_question: When the product or deal team needs performance data — capital call percentage, inception-to-date IRR, deployment levels — how quickly can your team deliver that?

level_2.question: When the deal team is waiting [X] days for that data, what do they do in the meantime — use stale numbers, estimate, or wait?

level_3.question: If they're using estimates, how often do the actuals come back materially different?
Has a deal or investment decision ever been made on stale performance data?

level_2.question: If the CEO or a partner asks for a number in a board meeting, can someone pull it up in real time — or does it require a request?

level_3.question: How does that gap between 'I need this now' and 'it'll take a few days' land with leadership?
If leadership could self-serve on performance data without going through the ops team, what would that free up for your team?

level_2.question: That's fast — is that for a standard report, or can they get ad-hoc analysis just as quickly?
What about when it's not just one fund — if they need a cross-portfolio view, does the speed hold up?

trees.parent_question: How do you think about budgeting for something like this — you have a working process, time is a pressure point, but it's not broken...

level_2.question: When a purchase like this gets proposed internally, what does it get measured against — headcount cost, risk reduction, or something else?

level_3.question: If we framed this as a risk mitigation investment rather than a technology purchase, does that change who needs to approve it?
What's the threshold where it shifts from a team decision to a CFO decision?

level_2.question: Is there existing budget allocated for ops technology, or would this need to be a new line item?

level_3.question: If we can show a cost offset — like replacing a portal subscription or reducing overtime — does that make the budget conversation easier?
Would it help to quantify the cost of the current process so the ROI case writes itself?

level_2.question: Totally understand — a lot of firms we work with felt the same way before they quantified what the current process was actually costing them. Would it be useful to do that exercise together?
If I could show you that firms your size typically recover the investment within two quarters through time savings and error reduction, would that change the conversation?

composition_integration.description: How follow-up trees wire into the response composition engine.
composition_integration.trigger: When a DQ or signal-detected question is surfaced as a card, the follow-up tree is loaded into the composition engine's working memory. On the next buyer utterance, response_signal_types classify the response, and the appropriate branch's level_2 question is surfaced as the next card.

card_rendering.secondary: The 'purpose' field — shown as a coaching hint (dimmed) so the rep understands WHY they're asking this
card_rendering.available_branches: If multiple level_2 options exist for the detected response type, show the first as primary and make others expandable

decision_engine_interaction.priority: When a follow-up tree is active, it takes priority over the standard cluster → decision_rule pipeline for 1-2 turns. The rep is in a conversational thread — don't interrupt it with a topic change.
exit_conditions: Buyer changes topic (new cluster fires that doesn't match the tree's problem_types)
Level 3 reached — tree exhausted, return to standard pipeline
Buyer deflects twice consecutively — disengage, don't push
Rep explicitly moves to a different topic (next turn fires a different DQ)
decision_engine_interaction.posture_interaction: If buyer posture shifts to defensive during a tree, the posture_modulation step softens the next follow-up. If posture shifts to pain_acknowledged, the tree may skip to implication questions rather than continuing to probe.

deepening_rhythm.description: Sequencing constraint that prevents interrogation creep. Ensures the rep alternates between factual probes and implication/story pulls. Inspired by the useful kernel of SPIN methodology — specifically the discipline of bridging from facts to implications — without importing the rigid S→P→I→N sequence.
deepening_rhythm.core_rule: Never serve two questions with the same deepening_type back to back. After a factual answer, go to implication or story. After an implication, go to metric or story. After a story pull, go to implication or metric.

factual.description: Questions that extract specific data points — numbers, timelines, counts, names, process steps.
factual.buyer_feels: Answering questions. Providing information. Can feel like an interrogation if stacked.

implication.description: Questions that explore consequences, downstream effects, risk magnitude, and what-if scenarios. These make the buyer feel the weight of what they just told you.
implication.buyer_feels: Connecting dots. Realizing the size of what they just described. This is where belief shifts happen.

story_pull.description: Questions that invite the buyer to share a specific experience, anecdote, or memory. These create emotional anchoring and give the rep material to reference later.
story_pull.buyer_feels: Telling their story. Reliving the moment. This is where trust deepens.

contrast.description: Questions that paint the alternative future or test readiness. These bridge from pain to possibility without pitching.
contrast.buyer_feels: Imagining a different world. This is where motivation builds — but only after pain is established.

validation.description: Questions that check whether the problem has been addressed, the risk acknowledged, or the buyer's internal process has changed. These test the durability of the status quo.
validation.buyer_feels: Being asked to evaluate their own situation honestly. Powerful but can feel pointed — use after trust is established.

sequencing_rules.name: no_double_factual
sequencing_rules.rule: If the last follow-up served was deepening_type 'factual', the next follow-up MUST be 'implication', 'story_pull', or 'contrast'. Never two factual questions in a row.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Two factual questions back-to-back feels like a checklist. The buyer answered your question — now make them feel why their answer matters before asking for another fact.

example.bad: 'How many models are you running?' → 'Who maintains them?' (factual → factual = interrogation)
example.good: 'How many models are you running?' → '12' → 'If every new fund adds another model, at what point does that become unmanageable?' (factual → implication = conversation)

sequencing_rules.name: implication_after_metric
sequencing_rules.rule: When the buyer provides a specific metric (detected via gives_metric signal), the next question SHOULD be an implication question that makes the metric feel heavy.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Numbers are inert until connected to consequences. '3 days' means nothing. '3 days where your LPs are waiting and your team is stressed' means everything.
sequencing_rules.example: 'How long does reconciliation take?' → '3 days' → 'During those 3 days, are your LPs waiting for their distribution notice — or does it go out before reconciliation is complete?'

sequencing_rules.name: story_pull_after_implication
sequencing_rules.rule: After an implication question lands (buyer engages, doesn't deflect), a story_pull can deepen the emotional anchor. But only if the buyer seems open.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Once the buyer feels the weight of an implication, asking them to recall a specific moment makes the pain concrete and memorable. But if they're defensive, skip the story pull and soften.
sequencing_rules.posture_gate: Only if posture is NOT defensive or politically_cautious

sequencing_rules.name: contrast_as_bridge
sequencing_rules.rule: Contrast questions should appear after at least one factual AND one implication question have been answered. Never lead with contrast — it feels premature without established pain.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Painting the future only works when the present feels heavy. If you say 'imagine if this took 5 minutes instead' before they've told you it takes 3 days and why that matters, it's a pitch, not a conversation.
sequencing_rules.minimum_depth: At least 1 factual + 1 implication answered before any contrast question

sequencing_rules.name: validation_as_closer
sequencing_rules.rule: Validation questions work best at the end of a thread — after facts, implications, and possibly a story have been surfaced. They test whether the buyer is ready to acknowledge the problem.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Asking 'has your team changed anything about this?' works after the buyer has described the problem and felt its weight. It falls flat as an opener.
sequencing_rules.position: Prefer as level_3 or as thread exit question

sequencing_rules.name: deflection_reframe_rule
sequencing_rules.rule: When the buyer deflects, the next question should be a DIFFERENT deepening_type than the one that prompted the deflection. If a factual question got deflected, try an implication or story_pull angle instead.
sequencing_rules.rationale: If they dodged a direct question, asking another direct question is pushing. Come at it from a different angle — make them feel the implication, or ask for a specific moment, rather than demanding another fact.
sequencing_rules.example: 'How often does this happen?' → 'We manage it fine' → DON'T ask 'But how many times per quarter?' → DO ask 'Even when it's managed well, what does the team's quarter-end look like in terms of hours and stress?'

sequencing_rules.name: max_factual_per_thread
sequencing_rules.rule: No more than 2 factual questions per 5-question thread. If 2 factual questions have been asked, the remaining questions in the thread must be implication, story_pull, contrast, or validation.
sequencing_rules.rationale: Hard cap on data extraction. The point isn't to fill out a form — it's to help the buyer feel the size of what they're living with.

implementation.description: How the deepening_rhythm integrates with tree traversal at runtime.
implementation.tracking: Maintain a rolling window of the last 3 deepening_types served in the current thread. On each follow-up selection, check the candidate's purpose_tag against deepening_types, then validate against sequencing_rules.
implementation.conflict_resolution: If the highest-priority follow-up from the tree violates a sequencing rule, select the next-best follow-up from the same branch that satisfies the rhythm. If no follow-up in the branch satisfies, skip to the next response_type branch.
implementation.override: If the composition engine's posture_modulation step has shifted tone to 'softened' (defensive buyer), rhythm rules are relaxed — the engine prioritizes de-escalation over rhythm compliance.
implementation.performance: Purpose tag → deepening_type lookup is a static map built at initialization. No runtime cost beyond a dictionary lookup per follow-up selection.

objection_follow_through.description: Follow-through logic for objection handling. When a rep delivers a reframe and the buyer responds, the brain classifies the buyer's reaction and serves the appropriate next move — deepen if the reframe landed, pivot if it didn't, escalate if they're testing. Works with the post_move_classifier to close the loop: reframe → buyer reaction → follow-through → next card.
design_principles: A reframe is the beginning of a conversation, not the end
If the reframe lands, deepen — don't move on too fast
If the reframe bounces, don't repeat it louder — come from a different angle
Two deflections on the same objection = park it and move on
Every follow-through has a purpose: deepen, pivot, validate, or park
Respect the deepening_rhythm — don't stack the same question type
Objection follow-through inherits posture sensitivity from move_evaluation

reaction_types.description: How the buyer responds to an objection reframe. Maps to post_move_classifier buckets but with objection-specific refinement.

accepts_reframe.description: Buyer acknowledges the reframe has merit. May not fully agree but is no longer pushing the objection.
signals: I hadn't thought of it that way

partially_accepts.description: Buyer accepts part of the reframe but has a residual concern. 'I get the LP pass-through, but still...'

tests_further.description: Buyer pushes back to test conviction. Not rejecting — stress-testing. 'But what about...' or 'What if...'

deflects_objection.description: Buyer sidesteps the reframe without engaging. Changes topic or goes vague.
signals: let me get back to you

escalates_objection.description: Buyer doubles down. The reframe didn't land and they're more entrenched.

questions.q: If we could quantify the cost of your current process — person-hours, error exposure, key-person risk — would that help frame the investment internally?
Have you confirmed whether this qualifies as an LP expense under your LPA? That changes the math entirely for most firms.
What would the ROI need to look like for your CFO to say yes — are we talking headcount parity, or something beyond that?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They're past the sticker shock. Now anchor the value. Get them building the business case in their own head.

questions.q: What's the piece that still doesn't feel right — is it the absolute number, the timing, or the internal justification?
If the per-fund economics worked out differently at scale — say, all funds versus a subset — does that change the picture?

partially_accepts.coaching_hint: They bought part of the story. Find the specific residual and address it directly.

questions.q: Would it help to see how a firm at your scale structured the ROI case? I can share the framework they used.
If I walked you through the three cost buckets — efficiency, risk reduction, and scalability — which one would carry the most weight with your leadership?

tests_further.coaching_hint: They're testing whether you can back it up. This is buying behavior, not rejection. Meet it with specifics.

deflects_objection.action: Acknowledge the pricing concern will need internal discussion. Redirect to value discovery — the stronger the pain case, the easier the pricing conversation becomes later.
deflects_objection.pivot_question: Totally fair — pricing is always a discussion that happens with more people at the table. Before we get there, can I ask: what's the single biggest operational risk you'd want to address if budget weren't a factor?
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Don't chase the pricing objection. Build more pain and the price becomes relative.

escalates_objection.action: Respect the position. Don't negotiate in the moment. Park pricing and focus on whether the problem is worth solving at all — the price conversation is premature until the value case is built.
escalates_objection.response: I hear you — and I don't want to spend time defending price before we've established whether this solves a problem worth solving. Can we set pricing aside for now and come back to it once we've mapped the full picture?
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Two pricing escalations = stop talking about price. Build the business case first. Price only matters when value is clear.

questions.q: What does your team actually do after the admin delivers the quarterly package — walk me through the verification process.
If the admin's numbers and yours ever disagree, who wins — and how long does it take to reconcile?
How much of your team's quarter-end crunch is actually checking the admin's output?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They accepted that Maybern complements the admin. Now surface the hidden verification work they're doing — that's the value case.

questions.q: What specifically concerns you about adding a verification layer — is it the admin's reaction, the cost, or the internal bandwidth?
Have other firms you know added independent verification without disrupting their admin relationship?

partially_accepts.coaching_hint: The loyalty is emotional, not logical. Respect it. Show that verification is standard practice, not a vote of no confidence.

questions.q: If I could show you how the admin relationship actually improves when the fund owns its own calculation logic — less back-and-forth, fewer corrections — would that change the framing?
What would your admin say if they knew you were independently verifying their work? Most admins prefer it — it reduces their liability.

tests_further.coaching_hint: They're testing whether this creates conflict. Show them it resolves conflict — for both sides.

deflects_objection.pivot_question: Understood — the admin relationship is important and we'd never want to disrupt that. Separate from the admin for a moment: when your deal team needs performance data mid-quarter, how quickly can they get it?
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Redirect from the admin to a pain the admin can't solve — real-time data access, deal team needs, LP reporting speed.

escalates_objection.response: I respect that — a strong admin relationship is an asset and protecting it matters. Let me ask this differently: if the admin relationship stays exactly as it is, what's the one operational area where your team still feels stretched?
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Stop talking about the admin. Find a pain point that exists regardless of the admin — that's the entry.

questions.q: What's the next major event on your calendar — fund raise, audit, fund launch — that would benefit from having this in place?
If we worked backward from that event, when would we need to start to have you live in time?
Is there a window between now and that event where your team has the bandwidth to run a parallel evaluation?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They acknowledged urgency exists somewhere. Anchor to a specific event and work backward.

questions.q: Even if the timing isn't perfect — is there a risk that gets worse the longer you wait? Key person departure, next fund launch, LP pressure?
If you started scoping now and implemented after the busy season, would that feel more realistic?

partially_accepts.coaching_hint: They're not saying no — they're saying not now. Find the event that makes 'now' the right answer.

questions.q: What does waiting six more months cost you in terms of another quarter-end crunch, another audit cycle with the same exposure?
If your key person gave notice next month, what would that six-month delay have cost you?

tests_further.coaching_hint: They're weighing cost of action vs cost of inaction. Make inaction feel expensive.

deflects_objection.pivot_question: No pressure at all — timing has to work for your team. Would it make sense to at least map your current process so that when the timing is right, you're not starting from zero?
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Don't push urgency on someone who isn't feeling it. Offer a low-commitment next step that keeps the door open.

escalates_objection.response: I hear you — and the last thing I want to do is create pressure where there isn't a natural fit. Can I ask one thing before we table this: if something changes — a key person transition, a new fund, an audit finding — can I be the first call you make?
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Two urgency rejections = stop. Plant the seed for when urgency naturally appears. Stay human.

questions.q: If you added up the engineering hours spent maintaining the current tool over the last 12 months, what does that number look like?
When the fund structure changes — new LPA terms, new side letters — how long does it take to update the internal tool?
What's the opportunity cost? If those engineering hours went toward something else, what would the firm build instead?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They acknowledged the build has costs. Now quantify them — maintenance hours, update cycles, opportunity cost.

questions.q: What if the internal tool handled what it's good at, and the platform handled the complex waterfall and compliance pieces — does a hybrid make sense?
Is there a specific piece of the internal build that works really well — and a piece where the team spends most of its maintenance time?

partially_accepts.coaching_hint: Don't position as replace — position as complement. Let Maybern handle the hard parts the build struggles with.

questions.q: Can your internal tool handle a European waterfall with multi-tier carried interest and cross-fund offsets? What happens when an auditor needs the calculation trace?
If the person who built it left tomorrow and a new fund launched with a novel structure, how long before the team is up and running?

tests_further.coaching_hint: They're comparing capabilities. Get specific — the areas where internal builds break are complexity, auditability, and succession.

deflects_objection.pivot_question: The internal build is clearly an investment your team values. Setting that aside — when your auditors review the waterfall calculation logic, how does that process work today?
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Redirect to audit. Internal builds almost always have an auditability gap.

escalates_objection.response: I respect the investment your team has made. The internal build clearly solves real problems. If the question ever becomes whether to extend it further or bring in a purpose-built layer for the next generation of complexity, I'd love to be part of that conversation.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Don't fight the internal build. They built it — it's personal. Plant the seed for when the build hits its ceiling.

questions.q: Would it help to get the DDQ and SOC 2 report in front of your security team now, so that runs in parallel with the evaluation?
Who on your side handles the vendor security review — and what's their typical turnaround?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Security acceptance = move fast. Get the materials in their hands before momentum fades.

questions.q: What specific security requirements does your firm have beyond SOC 2 — are there industry-specific or regulatory frameworks we should address?
How does your current process handle data security — are the Excel files encrypted, access-controlled, and logged?

tests_further.coaching_hint: If they're testing security, flip the mirror — their current spreadsheet process is almost certainly less secure.

deflects_objection.action: Send the DDQ proactively. Don't wait for them to ask. Remove the friction.
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Security deflection usually means 'I need to check with someone else.' Give them the materials to bring to that person.

escalates_objection.response: Data security is non-negotiable — I wouldn't expect you to compromise on it. Would it help to set up a direct call between your security team and our CTO to address specific concerns?
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Escalated security concerns need a technical peer conversation, not a sales response.

questions.q: Would it help to see what the data flow looks like for a firm using the same GL as you? I can show you the exact integration pattern.
What format does your current system export in — CSV, Excel, or something else? That tells us exactly how lightweight the integration is.

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Integration anxiety dissolves with specificity. Show them the exact file format, the exact flow.

questions.q: Is the concern about the technical integration itself, or about the change management of adding another system to the workflow?
Would a technical scoping call with our implementation team help clarify the actual lift on your side?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Integration 'complexity' is usually a proxy for change management anxiety. Diagnose which one it actually is.

escalates_objection.response: I hear you — the last thing we want is to add complexity to an already complex environment. The integration is file-based, not API-based — there's no IT project required. But if the concern is really about adding another tool to the workflow, that's a different conversation worth having.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Integration escalation = they're really saying 'we don't want another system.' Address the real concern.

questions.q: What made previous evaluations stall — was it the product, the internal alignment, or the timing?
If we could run this evaluation on your actual data instead of generic demos, would that feel materially different from what you've seen before?
What would a 'good' evaluation process look like for your team — what would make this one actually reach a decision?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They've been burned by vendor evaluations. Show them this one will be different — real data, real outcomes, clear decision framework.

deflects_objection.pivot_question: I don't want to add to vendor fatigue. What if we just did one thing — took your most complex fund structure and showed you the calculation in our system? Thirty minutes, no commitment. If it doesn't impress you, we part ways.
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Evaluation fatigue needs a tiny next step, not a big commitment. One fund, one calculation, thirty minutes.

escalates_objection.response: I completely understand — evaluation fatigue is real and I don't want to be another vendor that wastes your time. If it helps, I can send over a one-page summary of how we're different and let you decide if it's worth a second look on your timeline.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Respect the fatigue. Leave the door open with zero pressure.

questions.q: If the total ask on your side was one person for a few hours a week during implementation — reviewing outputs, not building anything — does that feel manageable?
What if we started with your highest-complexity fund first — solve the hardest problem, prove value, then expand?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They accepted the reframe but still worry about bandwidth. Make the ask feel tiny.

questions.q: Is the constraint headcount, time, or both? If it's time — is there a seasonal window where bandwidth opens up?
What's consuming the team's bandwidth right now — and is any of that the manual work a platform would eliminate?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Resource constraints are often self-reinforcing — the manual work consuming bandwidth is the problem the platform solves.

escalates_objection.response: I hear you — and I'd never want to add to a stretched team's burden. Would it make sense to scope this now and implement when bandwidth opens up? That way you're ready to move when the window appears.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Don't push implementation on a team that's drowning. Scope now, implement later.

questions.q: Would it help to talk to a client who went through the implementation recently — hear what it was actually like from their side?
What's your biggest concern about the implementation — timeline, disruption, or data accuracy during transition?
The parallel running period is designed so your team never has to trust a number they haven't verified. Does that address the core concern?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Implementation anxiety is fear-based. References and safety nets dissolve it.

questions.q: Want me to walk you through the exact implementation timeline for a firm with your fund count and complexity? I can show you week by week.
What went wrong with past implementations you've been through — what would you want to avoid this time?

tests_further.coaching_hint: They're not saying no — they're saying 'convince me it won't be painful.' Specificity kills anxiety.

escalates_objection.response: Past implementations gone sideways create legitimate caution. What if the first phase was purely diagnostic — we map your process, identify gaps, and you decide whether the implementation makes sense based on what we find? No commitment beyond the scoping.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Offer a step so small it feels risk-free. Scoping is not implementation.

questions.q: Would you be willing to give us your most complex fund — the one with the most side-letter provisions and waterfall tiers — and let us prove the calculation matches?
What would need to be true for your team to trust the system's output — matching Excel to the penny, or something more?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Accuracy trust is earned through proof, not promises. Get their hardest fund and nail it.

questions.q: What specific calculation nuances are you most concerned about — catch-up mechanics, tiered hurdles, cross-fund netting, or something else?
If I showed you the full audit trail for a calculation — from LPA terms to final distribution amount, with every step traceable — would that address the trust question?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Accuracy testing is buying behavior. They're not objecting — they're evaluating. Match their technical depth.

escalates_objection.response: You're right to be skeptical — the math has to be perfect, period. Would you be open to a structured proof-of-concept where we configure your most complex fund and you validate every number against your existing output? If it doesn't match, we part ways.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Accuracy escalation = they want to be proven wrong. Give them the chance. This is your strongest ground.

questions.q: Who on your team would be the first adopter — the person who'd see value fastest and pull others along?
What if the team kept using Excel for the first quarter while the platform ran in parallel — and they only switched when they trusted the numbers?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Change management acceptance needs a champion and a gradual path. Find both.

questions.q: What specifically failed with the last system you implemented — was it the tool or the rollout process?
If the team could use familiar concepts — same fund names, same waterfall structure, same distribution workflow — would that reduce the resistance?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Change management fear is usually about past trauma. Diagnose the specific scar and show why this is different.

escalates_objection.response: That experience clearly left a mark — and it should. Bad implementations waste time and erode trust. What if the next step was just a conversation with a client who had the same concern and went through it? No sales pitch, just their experience.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Peer reference is the only thing that heals implementation trauma. Sales words won't do it.

questions.q: What does your CFO / partner care about most — cost reduction, risk mitigation, or operational scalability? That determines how we frame the business case.
Would it help if I put together a one-page executive summary your champion can walk into that meeting with?
Is there a specific objection you expect from [stakeholder] that we should pre-address?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: They want to sell this internally. Your job shifts from selling to enabling. Give them the tools.

questions.q: Help me understand the decision landscape — who's supportive, who's on the fence, and who's likely to push back?
What would a coordination meeting need to accomplish for the skeptics to feel heard?

partially_accepts.coaching_hint: Internal alignment requires a map. Get the lay of the land before offering strategy.

deflects_objection.pivot_question: Would it be useful if I joined that internal conversation — not to sell, but to answer technical questions that might come up? Sometimes having the vendor in the room prevents a game of telephone.
deflects_objection.coaching_hint: Offer to be a resource, not a salesperson. Reduce the champion's burden.

escalates_objection.response: If alignment is the blocker, it might help to bring the right stakeholders into the next conversation directly — so they hear the value case firsthand rather than secondhand. Would a coordination meeting make sense?
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Single-threaded deals die from stakeholder misalignment. Multi-thread early.

questions.q: Is [feature] a day-one requirement, or something you'd need within the first 12 months?
If everything else worked perfectly and this was the only gap, would that be a deal-breaker or something you'd work around?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Separate must-haves from nice-to-haves. Most product gaps are nice-to-haves disguised as must-haves.

questions.q: Would it help to speak directly with our product team about the roadmap and timeline for [feature]?
What's driving the need for [feature] specifically — is it a current workflow or an anticipated one?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Product gap testing is either genuine need or negotiation leverage. Diagnose which one.

escalates_objection.response: I want to be straightforward — if [feature] is a hard requirement for day-one value, we should talk about whether the timeline works. I'd rather be honest now than overpromise.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Honesty about product gaps builds more trust than spin. If it's a real blocker, own it.

questions.q: Would a reference call with a firm at your scale and complexity be helpful — I can match by strategy type, fund count, and AUM range.
What specific concern would the reference need to address — implementation experience, ongoing support, or product depth?

accepts_reframe.coaching_hint: Trust acceptance = they want to believe but need proof. Give them the most relevant reference possible.

questions.q: What would need to be true about the vendor for you to feel comfortable moving forward — is it track record, team stability, financial backing, or something else?
If I could connect you with our CEO or CTO directly, would that help address the credibility question?

tests_further.coaching_hint: Trust testing means they're close. They want to say yes but need permission. Give them what they need.

escalates_objection.response: That's a fair concern and I won't try to talk you out of it. Would you be open to a reference call with a firm your size that had the same concern and decided to move forward? Their perspective would be more credible than mine.
escalates_objection.coaching_hint: Trust can't be sold — it has to be shown. Peer reference is the only answer.

escalation_tracking.description: Track how many times the same objection category has been raised and deflected or escalated. Determines when to stop pushing.

rules.action: Deliver reframe. Follow through based on reaction.
If deflected or escalated twice on the same category, park it. Note in session state for later reference.
Do not resurface this objection category. It's been heard and parked. Focus on other value angles.

escalation_tracking.session_state: Map of category_id → {raise_count, last_reaction, parked: boolean}

composition_integration.description: How objection follow-through wires into the response composition engine and decision engine.
composition_integration.trigger: When an objection_handling card is surfaced, the corresponding category_follow_through is loaded. On the next buyer utterance, reaction_types classify the response and the follow-through logic determines the next move.
composition_integration.priority: Active objection follow-through takes priority over the standard pipeline for 1-2 turns — same as question_follow_up_trees. The rep is in a thread.

card_rendering.primary: The follow-through question or response text
card_rendering.secondary: The coaching_hint — shown dimmed as guidance for the rep
card_rendering.purpose: Shown as a tag so the rep understands the strategic intent

composition_integration.posture_interaction: If posture is defensive or politically_cautious, follow-through automatically softens. If posture shifts to pain_acknowledged during follow-through, accelerate to value questions.

technical_playbook.description: Sales technical playbook for Maybern's fund finance platform. Synthesized from 6 calls totaling 318 minutes. Captures product capabilities, common client questions, edge cases, demo navigation patterns, and Sophie's exact phrasing for handling technical concerns. Designed as a real-time reference during sales calls.

enrichment_protocol.description: As Maybern releases new features, the sales team should update this playbook to keep it current. Use these steps to maintain consistency and keep Sophie's voice authentic.
steps: When new feature releases: Create new capability entry in appropriate product_area with enrichable=true and link to source call
When demo UI changes: Update corresponding navigation_sequences with new step order and UI path
When client asks new question repeatedly: Add entry to common_questions and note pattern across source_calls
When limitations are resolved: Change status from 'partial' or 'roadmap' to 'supported' in edge_cases
When integration launched: Add capability to integrations_and_apis and document setup steps
Always preserve Sophie's exact phrasing: Direct quotes from calls are authoritative for sales team
Update version and last_enriched timestamp with each change
Maintain ID sequences within each product_area (CAP-001, Q-001, EDGE-001, etc.)

multi_currency.name: Multi-Currency & Exchange Rates
multi_currency.description: Handling international investments, currency conversion, FX hedging, and local vs. base currency tracking

capabilities.name: More the latter, so we have it defined as the bank of englan...
capabilities.what_it_does: More the latter, so we have it defined as the bank of england spot rate between GBP to euros or GBP to Swiss francs. Euros and Swiss francs are the only non GBP deals we've had so far. So we would look to the bank of england. They have a database. You can just pull a file or look it up on Google to see the spot rates on those dates. So that's the lookup date, the actual cash flow will remain in euros and Swiss francs. So the deal will be we will draw euros from the investor to make the deal. The investment will earn euros and pay us back in euros and euros will be given back to the investor. So the actual cash flows of the deal will as far as we can remain in the currency that we're working with and we have multiple currency bank accounts therefore to manage that. So where, yeah, so that we're not the investor, then they deal with the FX on their side. Where the only time we're actually then translating is for reporting and just normal GAAP. And then for the waterfall.

But we've got… four FX hedging instruments out there on each of these foreign deals. So when we're doing our quarter end reporting because with GAAP, you do need to split out the value of the investment separately from the value of the hedging instruments, we have the two unrealized value fields in there. One with hedging and one without hedging. The one with hedging would basically add in the value of that forward contract to the unrealized value without hedging. And that's just going to be for our front section, managerial reporting just so you can see basically upon exit what the true value of that investment will be.
