metadata.deduplication_strategy: Questions merged when asking same concern, capabilities grouped by function

status_quo_confidence.rebuttal: The firms that feel most confident usually haven't had the event yet. One LP dispute, one team member leaving, one fund launch — that's usually what changes the math. We're not here to create urgency. We're here to ask whether the current setup can absorb the next level of complexity.
status_quo_confidence.follow_up_question: What would have to break for you to feel like the current setup wasn't enough?
status_quo_confidence.hard_close: If the current process handles everything perfectly, what made someone on your team take this meeting? Something triggered the conversation. I'd rather understand that than pitch you on something you don't need.
status_quo_confidence.hard_close_note: Redirects to the original trigger. Buyers who say 'we're fine' still took the meeting. The cognitive dissonance between 'we're fine' and 'I'm on this call' is the lever.

status_quo_defense.rebuttal: I'm not suggesting what you have is broken. I'm asking whether it scales. Most firms we talk to aren't in crisis — they're at an inflection point. The question is whether you want to address it before or after the next fund launch.
status_quo_defense.follow_up_question: When you think about Fund V or your next close — does the current process hold up without adding people?

manual_process.rebuttal: Manual is fine until it's not. The question is whether the manual work is growing linearly with your fund count — because if it is, you're either going to cap your growth or grow your team. Neither is the answer you want.
manual_process.follow_up_question: How many hours per quarter would you say your team spends on validation and reconciliation work?

workflow_fragility.rebuttal: When a process depends on specific people executing specific steps in a specific order — that's not a workflow, that's a ritual. Rituals work until the person who knows the ritual isn't there.
workflow_fragility.follow_up_question: If I asked your team to document that process end to end today — how long would it take and how confident would you be it was complete?

excel_dependency.rebuttal: Excel isn't the problem — it's the process Excel enables. When the logic lives in a file that one person maintains, the file becomes the single point of failure. It doesn't fail dramatically. It fails quietly.
excel_dependency.follow_up_question: How many people on your team can open that model and understand exactly what it's doing?

audit_risk.rebuttal: Audit readiness isn't just about having the numbers. It's about being able to show your work — every calculation, every override, every amendment, with a timestamp and a reason. A spreadsheet that's been modified 40 times doesn't give you that.
audit_risk.follow_up_question: When was the last audit and how long did your team spend pulling documentation together?

key_person_risk.rebuttal: The risk isn't that they leave. It's what happens while they're there. You can't promote them freely. You can't have them out during a distribution cycle. You can't hire someone junior to ramp up alongside them because the knowledge isn't transferable.
key_person_risk.follow_up_question: Who else on your team fully understands how the waterfall logic works end to end?

fund_growth.rebuttal: Growth is the multiplier. Every fund you add multiplies the complexity — more structures, more LPs, more side letters, more reconciliation cycles. The question is whether the infrastructure scales with the business or becomes the ceiling on it.
fund_growth.follow_up_question: What does your fund launch roadmap look like over the next two years?

pricing_sensitivity.rebuttal: It is typically the most significant software investment a fund ops team makes. But 100% of our clients pass it through as a fund expense — it is not management company overhead. The question is what the validation process costs you today in person-hours trapped in spreadsheets.
pricing_sensitivity.follow_up_question: What does a fully-loaded FTE cost you in this market? Let me run the real comparison with your numbers.
pricing_sensitivity.hard_close: You are already paying for this. The platform costs a fraction of what you spend on the process it replaces. The math usually surprises people when they run it.
pricing_sensitivity.fund_expense_note: Always lead with fund expense classification. It reframes the entire conversation from 'how much does this cost us' to 'this is already budgeted in the LPA.'

internal_build_mentioned.rebuttal: We've seen this path. The first version works. Then a new fund structure comes in, and someone has to hack the system to make it work. Then another. The engineering team underestimates the complexity of fund accounting logic — not because they're not talented, but because it's genuinely subtle.
internal_build_mentioned.follow_up_question: What's the estimate for the build timeline, and does that include ongoing maintenance as fund structures change?

competitor_mentioned.rebuttal_strategy: DO NOT REBUTTAL FIRST. Ask why they are looking. Their reason constructs the response.

l1.text: That's helpful to know. Before I respond to that — what prompted the conversation with them? I want to understand what you were trying to solve so I can give you a more useful answer.
l1.note: DO NOT jump to rebuttal. The reason they looked at a competitor tells you more than the competitor name. Their answer constructs your L2.

l2.text: Got it. So the core issue is [mirror their reason back]. Here is where we see that differently — [construct response based on what they said]. The gap most firms find with [competitor] is at the investor level: waterfalls, carry, scenario modeling, LP-specific economics. That layer either is not included or requires additional modules.
l2.note: Mirror their reason first, then reframe. This feels like a conversation, not a canned pitch. Adapt the second half based on which competitor they named.

l3.text: You went looking for a solution, which tells me the problem is real even if the alternative did not solve it. What specifically could they not handle?
l3.note: This is the strongest move: their failed evaluation validates the pain. Get them to name the specific gap. That gap becomes your demo anchor.

competitor_mentioned.follow_up_question: What specifically were you hoping they could solve that they could not?
competitor_mentioned.hard_close: I am not asking you to trust me on capability. I am asking if it is worth 45 minutes to put your hardest fund on the table and see whether we can model it.

no_urgency.rebuttal: You're right that there's no fire today. The question is whether the current setup is designed for where you're going or where you've been. Most firms don't add infrastructure when they're in crisis — they add it when they can. The ones that wait usually regret the timing.
no_urgency.follow_up_question: What's your next fund close date, and does the current process get harder or easier from here?
no_urgency.hard_close: What would have to happen between now and Fund V's first close for this to feel urgent enough to act on? I want to understand what the trigger looks like for you, so we're not guessing at timing.
no_urgency.hard_close_note: This forces the buyer to name their own urgency condition rather than defending against yours. Whatever they say becomes the mutual timeline anchor. If they can't name a trigger, the deal may not be real.

competitor_pricing.rebuttal_strategy: Ask what prompted the comparison before responding to price.

l1.text: Before I respond to the pricing comparison — what were you hoping they could solve? I want to make sure we are comparing the same thing.
l1.note: Price comparisons without scope comparisons are meaningless. Get the scope first.

l2.text: Worth understanding what is included in their quote. Most alternative pricing covers the GL layer only. Investor-level performance — waterfalls, carry, scenario modeling — either is not included or requires additional modules at additional cost. Our price includes everything. No add-ons, no user fees, no transaction fees.

l3.text: The hidden cost of choosing a cheaper tool that does not solve the problem is not the license fee — it is the time you lose and the problem that persists.

competitor_pricing.follow_up_question: What specifically is included in their quote — and what would you still need to solve in Excel even with that tool?
competitor_pricing.hard_close: One of our clients spent six months evaluating a cheaper alternative that could not handle their waterfall structures. The hidden cost was not the license — it was the six months.

build_vs_buy_cost.rebuttal: Internal builds are optimized for how things worked when they were built. One client evaluated an internal Anaplan build at $50-70M over five years. The build cost was not the problem — it was the maintenance tail and the 3-5 engineers needed to sustain it.
build_vs_buy_cost.follow_up_question: How many engineering hours would your team need to build waterfall logic for multi-tier structures with side-letter overrides and full audit traceability?
build_vs_buy_cost.hard_close: The question is whether your firm wants to be in the fund accounting software business or the fund management business. We have 50+ engineers who do nothing but this. You share the R&D cost with every client.

allvue_mentioned.rebuttal: Allvue is a strong general ledger for partnership accounting. The gap is at the investor level — waterfalls, carried interest, scenario modeling, LP-specific economics. That is not Allvue's core.
allvue_mentioned.follow_up_question: What are you using Allvue for specifically — the GL, or are you trying to get it to handle the investor-level calculations too?
allvue_mentioned.hard_close: Every firm running Allvue still lives in Excel for the calc layer. Data flows in from Allvue. The performance book of record adds the layer above it.

carta_mentioned.rebuttal: Carta is strong for venture-style funds with pro-rata structures. When complexity increases — deal-by-deal waterfalls, catch-ups, clawbacks, side letters — it breaks.
carta_mentioned.follow_up_question: How many of your fund structures have economics that go beyond a simple pro-rata split?
carta_mentioned.hard_close: Carta's waterfall approach uses rigid templates that require constant Excel validation. How many of your investor arrangements have custom terms?

investran_mentioned.rebuttal: Investran is widely used for fund-level accounting. The pattern is that even with Investran, investor-level performance — waterfalls, fees, carry — still ends up in spreadsheets.
investran_mentioned.follow_up_question: Where does the investor-level calc logic live today — in Investran or in Excel?
investran_mentioned.hard_close: We do not replace Investran. Data flows in. The GL stays. The calc layer adds.

efront_mentioned.rebuttal: eFront is solid for GL and fund accounting — especially customized instances. Investor-level performance, waterfalls, and scenario modeling typically live outside it in Excel.
efront_mentioned.follow_up_question: How much customization have you done on your eFront instance, and what still lives outside it?
efront_mentioned.hard_close: The more customized the eFront instance, the harder it is to change. The calc layer above it gives you flexibility without touching the GL.

pricing_intelligence.description: Complete pricing framework for Maybern. Includes classification logic, rebuttal matrix, negotiation guardrails, and value anchoring sequences. Sourced from internal pricing script and field-tested objection handling.

fund_expense_classification.key_message: 100% of our clients pass the fee through to the fund as a fund expense. This is a performance book of record — it falls in line with your LPA terms for software expenses. It is not management company overhead. It is a legitimate fund operational expense that directly supports fund administration and reporting.
fund_expense_classification.why_this_matters: When the buyer hears 'fund expense,' the cost disappears from the management company P&L. The GP is not paying for it out of management fees. The fund absorbs it like admin fees, audit fees, or legal fees. This reframes the entire pricing conversation from 'how much does this cost us' to 'this is already budgeted for in the LPA.'
fund_expense_classification.rebuttal_when_challenged: Check your LPA language on technology expenses. Most modern LPAs explicitly allow software that supports fund administration, reporting, and investor communications as a pass-through fund expense. Your legal team can confirm, but every client we work with classifies it this way.

pricing_model.philosophy: Our CFO built this pricing model when he joined Maybern because he bought enough software in his career to appreciate transparency and wanted to create something that makes logical sense and is completely clear in how it works.

structure.metric: AUM on a fund basis — NAV plus unfunded commitments
structure.range: 1-3 basis points on AUM, scaled back based on fund size and implementation complexity
structure.implementation: Half a basis point as a one-time charge, paid 50% upfront and 50% when live on platform
structure.co_invest: Small flat annual fee per entity for co-invest vehicles, SMAs, single investor funds
structure.all_inclusive: No add-on fees. No user fees. No transaction fees. No activity-based charges. All in one price.

pricing_model.natural_decline_story: When you start off, AUM is essentially the money you raised. Eventually NAV goes up as you invest, unfunded goes down, you might have write-ups or write-downs. You hover around that initial fund size for a while. Then when you start selling assets, AUM shrinks because there is no unfunded left and NAV is declining. As the fund naturally shrinks, your fee automatically gets lower when you are less active.
pricing_model.scaling_story: As your funds grow, you do not have to hire as quickly for fund operations roles. The platform scales with you and handles the operational complexity.

implementation.fee_framing: There is an implementation fee, but it is a fraction of the first year's annual cost. It is not one of those massive implementation fees that scare people off.
implementation.payment_structure: 50% upfront — so you have skin in the game — and 50% when you are live on the platform. You are not paying for the subscription until you are actually on the platform and your historical data is validated and working.
implementation.team_description: We have an implementation team of fund accountants who have been doing this their whole lives. They speak the same language as you. It is a function of taking in your files, getting them into the system, and asking configuration questions.
implementation.timeline_typical: Firms at typical complexity are live in 4-5 months with parallel running for at least one quarter.

value_positioning.strategic_transformation: When this goes through as a fund expense, you are making your people more efficient and letting them do higher-level tasks. They are not Excel jockeys anymore, they are not just checkers — they are performing much more strategic functions.
value_positioning.platform_growth: This becomes much more than a processing tool. When investments are in there, it becomes so complete that you start bringing in other people. Investor relations can answer their own questions instead of filtering through you. Management can see dashboards. It evolves into a comprehensive platform across the organization.
value_positioning.data_retention: Once funds end, that information stays in the platform for track record purposes and marketing. You are not paying for that fund when it is over. The data is yours.

scenarios.objection: Why basis points instead of a flat fee?
scenarios.l1: The pricing mirrors how public market infrastructure is priced. Clearwater, Aladdin, Bloomberg PORT all use basis points. As NAV goes down, the fee goes down. You are not locked into a flat rate when the fund is winding down and activity is minimal.
scenarios.l2: A flat fee means you are overpaying in the later years of a fund when activity drops and AUM shrinks. BPS pricing self-adjusts. In the early years when you are most active and the fund is largest, you pay more. As the fund matures and shrinks, the cost declines automatically. You never renegotiate.
scenarios.l3: Run the math on a 10-year fund life. A flat fee stays constant while your AUM declines by 60-70% in years 7-10. BPS pricing saves you significant money in the back half of the fund. Which model do you want to be in during year 8?

scenarios.objection: We prefer per-user or per-fund pricing — it's easier to budget.
scenarios.l1: Per-user pricing creates a perverse incentive. We want as many people at your firm using this as possible — IR, management, deal team. Per-user pricing makes you ration access. Our model is all-inclusive: no user fees, no transaction fees, no activity charges. One price, unlimited access.
scenarios.l2: Per-fund pricing penalizes growth. Every new fund launch becomes a cost event. With BPS on AUM, new funds are absorbed into the existing AUM calculation. You do not get a surprise invoice every time you launch.
scenarios.l3: Budget predictability is a fair concern. We can give you a schedule that shows the projected fee based on your committed AUM for each fund, declining over the fund life. You will know within 5% what each year costs before you sign.

scenarios.objection: That number is higher than I expected.
scenarios.l1: It is typically the most significant software investment a fund ops team makes. The question is what the alternative costs. You told me your team spends [X days] per quarter on waterfall validation alone. At fully-loaded headcount costs, that is real money. The platform compresses that time. The math usually surprises people when they run it.
scenarios.l2: Three cost buckets to compare against: first, the person-hours your team spends on manual reconciliation and quarter-end validation — that is quantifiable. Second, key person risk — what does it cost if your lead analyst leaves and you need six months to replace them? Third, error exposure — one miscalculated distribution on a $50M realization is a six-figure problem. The platform cost sits against all three.
scenarios.l3: You are already paying for this. The question is whether you are paying for a system or paying for person-hours trapped in spreadsheets. One of our clients — a healthcare PE controller — ran this exact comparison. Her reaction was: '$37K a year in fund expenses to help the data team? That is a no-brainer.' Your numbers are different but the math works the same way.

scenarios.objection: We need to see ROI before we commit to this price.
scenarios.l1: Completely fair. Let me build the ROI model with you right now using your numbers. How much does a fully-loaded fund accountant cost you? And how much of their time goes to waterfall validation, reconciliation, and ad hoc LP inquiries?
scenarios.l2: Beyond the direct time savings, there are two ROI layers most firms miss: first, the cost of not being able to respond to LP or deal team requests in real-time — that is opportunity cost. Second, the cost of the next hire you would need as fund count grows. If the platform absorbs one headcount worth of operational work, the ROI is immediate.
scenarios.l3: Based on what you have told me today, you are looking at over $1 million in quantified benefits against an annual cost of [amount]. That is roughly a six-month payback period with ongoing value creation. I will send you a copy of this analysis so you can stress-test the assumptions internally.

scenarios.l1: That is worth understanding. What is included in their quote? Most alternative pricing covers the GL layer only. Investor-level performance — waterfalls, carry, scenario modeling, LP-specific economics — either is not included or requires additional modules at additional cost. Our price includes everything. No add-ons.
scenarios.l2: The question is what you are comparing. A GL license versus a performance book of record are different products solving different problems. If you need GL accounting, Allvue may be the right choice and we are complementary. If you need the calc layer above the GL — the one that currently lives in Excel — that is what we price for.
scenarios.l3: One of our clients evaluated Allvue's analytics module and found it could not handle their waterfall structures. They spent six months in that evaluation. The hidden cost of choosing a cheaper tool that does not solve the problem is not the license fee — it is the time you lose and the problem that persists.

scenarios.objection: We could build this internally for less.
scenarios.l1: Internal builds are optimized for how things worked when they were built. The question is what happens when fund structures evolve and who maintains it. One client evaluated an internal Anaplan build at $50-70M over five years versus a commercial platform. The build cost was not the problem — it was the maintenance tail.
scenarios.l2: The real cost of an internal build is not the initial development. It is the 3-5 engineers you need to maintain it, the product manager to prioritize features, the QA team to test updates, and the institutional knowledge that leaves when any of those people leave. We have 50+ engineers who do nothing but this. You share the R&D cost with every client on the platform.
scenarios.l3: How many engineering hours would your team need to build waterfall calculation logic for multi-tier European structures with side-letter overrides, fee offsets by distribution event type, and full audit traceability? That is what we have built over five years. The question is whether your firm wants to be in the fund accounting software business or the fund management business.

scenarios.objection: Our LPs won't accept another fund expense.
scenarios.l1: That is a common concern but rarely plays out in practice. LPs already accept fund admin fees, audit fees, legal fees, and tax preparation fees as fund expenses. A performance book of record falls in the same category — it is infrastructure that supports the accuracy and defensibility of fund calculations that directly benefit LPs.
scenarios.l2: Frame it from the LP perspective: would they rather the GP spend $37K per year on a system that ensures their distribution calculations are accurate and auditable, or rely on one person's Excel model with no traceability? The software protects LP interests. That is why 100% of our clients classify it as a fund expense.
scenarios.l3: Check your LPA language on technology and software expenses. Most modern LPAs drafted in the last five years explicitly include 'technology expenses related to fund administration and reporting.' If your LPA is older, your legal team can advise on whether an amendment or side letter covers it. We can share template language other firms have used.

trigger_phrases: how much before we go live

scenarios.objection: I don't want to pay before I see value.
scenarios.l1: You are not paying for the subscription until you are live on the platform and your historical data is validated and working. The only upfront cost is 50% of the implementation fee — which is a fraction of the first year's annual cost. That gives you skin in the game and us accountability to get you live.
scenarios.l2: The implementation fee covers our team of fund accountants backloading all your historical transactions into the system. These are people who have been doing this their whole lives — they speak your language. The 50/50 split means half is at risk until we deliver a working system you have validated.
scenarios.l3: Compare that to most enterprise software: full annual license upfront before you have even configured it, plus a separate implementation fee that often exceeds the first year's cost. Our structure is designed so that if we do not deliver, you have paid a fraction and you walk away. We are betting on our ability to prove value.

scenarios.objection: Can you come down on the price?
scenarios.l1: I am happy to discuss structure. Before we get to numbers, let me understand scope. How many funds would come on platform? Is there a fund launch timeline that would expand the relationship? The best pricing conversations happen when we understand the full picture.
scenarios.l2: There are a few levers: multi-fund commitment, timeline commitment (signing by a specific date), and bringing additional fund launches onto the platform as they close. Each of those creates value for us that we can reflect in pricing. What combination feels realistic for your firm?
scenarios.l3: I can go back to leadership with a proposal that reflects [specific commitment from buyer — fund count, timeline, Fund V inclusion]. But I need something concrete to work with. What would need to be true for you to move forward this quarter?

pricing_prevention_plays.description: Moves designed to establish the cost-of-inaction anchor BEFORE pricing ever comes up, so the number is compared against their own cost rather than against zero.

play_1_ftes.name: FTE Cost Extraction
play_1_ftes.when: After discovering team size and time burden, before any pricing discussion
sequence: Surface the operational burden: 'How much of your team's time goes to waterfall validation, reconciliation, and ad hoc inquiries?'
Quantify the people: 'Between you and your analyst, what percentage of your week is that?'
Get the dollar number: 'What does a fully-loaded FTE cost you in this market?'
Let them do the math: 'So the validation process alone costs you $X per year in embedded labor — before you factor in key person risk or what happens if someone leaves.'
Only then introduce pricing: 'The platform costs a fraction of what you are already spending on the process it replaces.'

play_2_error_cost.name: Error Cost Anchor
play_2_error_cost.when: After surfacing an admin error or calculation discrepancy
sequence: Surface the error: 'You mentioned you caught an error in the admin's distribution calc.'
Quantify the stakes: 'What was the dollar amount on that distribution?'
Project the risk: 'If that error had gone through to LPs uncorrected, what would that have cost — in dollars and in trust?'

Frame the insurance: 'The platform is essentially insurance against that scenario. Every calculation is traceable, every override is documented, every discrepancy is flagged before it reaches an investor.'

play_3_next_hire.name: Next Hire Deferral
play_3_next_hire.when: When buyer mentions growing fund count or stretched team
sequence: Surface the hiring pressure: 'At what point do you need to add headcount to handle the operational load?'
Get the cost: 'What does that hire cost you — salary, benefits, ramp time?'
Reframe: 'If the platform absorbs one headcount worth of operational work, you have deferred a $250-300K hiring decision. That is the ROI before you count the efficiency gains on your existing team.'

stage_guidance.M1_M2: Do NOT discuss specific pricing in M1 or M2 unless the EB demands it. If pressed, give the range (1-3 BPS) and immediately redirect to value. The goal is to establish the cost-of-inaction anchor before the number comes up.
stage_guidance.M4_value_creation: This is where pricing should first be discussed in depth. Build the ROI model live with the buyer using their own numbers. FTE cost extraction + error cost anchor + next hire deferral. The buyer should see their own cost-of-inaction number before they see your price.
stage_guidance.M7_exec_presentation: Present pricing as part of the business case, not as a standalone slide. Frame: 'Based on our analysis, you are looking at [quantified benefits] against an annual cost of [price]. That is a [X]-month payback period.' The EB should be nodding before they see the number.
stage_guidance.M10_proposal: The proposal price should never be a surprise. Every number in the proposal was discussed in M4 or M7. The proposal is documentation of what was already agreed in principle.

story_surfacing_engine.description: Rules for when and how customer stories surface during a live call. Stories are triggered by pain signals, competitor mentions, or stage transitions. Each story is tagged with use_when conditions that map to conversation signals.

trigger_on_pain_signal.description: When a pain signal fires (key_person_risk, manual_process, audit_risk, etc), find the best matching story and surface it as a proof point.
trigger_on_pain_signal.matching_logic: Match signal type to story use_when tags. Prioritize stories that match the current meeting stage. Prefer stories matching the buyer profile (in-house vs outsourced).

trigger_on_competitor_mention.description: When a competitor is named, surface a story where a client evaluated that competitor and chose Maybern — or where the competitor failed on complexity.
trigger_on_competitor_mention.matching_logic: Match competitor name to story use_when tags containing the competitor name or related terms.

trigger_on_pricing.description: When pricing signals fire, surface the healthcare PE controller story ($37K no-brainer) or a similar ROI validation story.
trigger_on_pricing.matching_logic: Match pricing_sensitivity signal to stories tagged with pricing, ROI, or fund expense.

competitor_talk_track_routing.description: When a specific competitor is named in conversation, surface the corresponding talk track as a blue card. Each competitor has a reframe strategy, talk track, and follow-up question.

responses.concern: We use Carta for fund admin and cap table.
responses.reframe: Acknowledge Carta's strength in venture/simple structures. Surface the complexity ceiling.
responses.talk_track: Carta is strong for venture-style funds with pro rata structures. When complexity increases — deal-by-deal waterfalls, catch-ups, clawbacks, side letter variations — firms have found they need purpose-built depth. We've seen PE firms try Carta's waterfall functionality and revert to Excel within months.
responses.follow_up: What are the most complex waterfall structures across your fund family — and how confident is your team in how those are handled today?

responses.concern: Carta says they can handle waterfall calculations now.
responses.reframe: Shift from feature claims to depth. Rigid templates vs. configurable engine.
responses.talk_track: Carta's waterfall approach uses rigid templates that require constant Excel validation. Firms with negotiated side letters, custom fee structures, or multi-tier waterfalls find they need a configurable engine, not a template.
responses.follow_up: How many of your investor arrangements have custom terms that wouldn't fit neatly into a standard template?

responses.concern: Carta is building out a full fund admin — shouldn't we wait?
responses.reframe: Carta's admin expansion targets simpler funds. Institutional complexity is a different market.
responses.talk_track: Carta is expanding into fund administration, but the target is primarily smaller and simpler fund structures. Institutional-grade complexity — unique investor terms, multi-strategy structures, detailed LPA compliance — requires a different level of depth than what a broad platform can offer.
responses.follow_up: What's the most complex LPA provision your team has to manage — and how would you test whether any platform can actually handle it?

responses.concern: We already use Juniper Square.
responses.reframe: Complementary, not competitive. JSQ = portal/CRM. Maybern = calc engine.
responses.talk_track: Roughly a third of similar clients use both platforms. Juniper Square handles investor communications and the portal. The calculation engine — waterfalls, management fees, carried interest, admin reconciliation — is a separate layer. There are templated exports that connect the two.
responses.follow_up: Are you using JSQ primarily for the portal and CRM, or are you relying on it for fund-level calculations as well?

responses.concern: We're not sure about sharing data with another vendor.
responses.reframe: Address the data ownership concern directly — Maybern keeps data with the fund, not the vendor.
responses.talk_track: Some firms have concerns about how portal vendors handle their proprietary data. With a performance book of record, the fund owns the data directly — it's calculated in your system, and you push it wherever you want. The data flows out to JSQ, not the other way around.
responses.follow_up: How comfortable is your team with how data flows between your current systems — and who ultimately controls what gets sent to investors?

responses.concern: We just implemented Yardi and Juniper Square — our stack is set.
responses.reframe: Additive, not replacement. GL + portal is set. The calc layer in between is the gap.
responses.talk_track: That's actually good news — GL and portal are in place. The piece in between — investor-level waterfalls, management fees, carried interest, admin reconciliation — that's the calculation layer most firms still handle in Excel. Adding a performance book of record completes the stack without displacing either system.
responses.follow_up: For the calculations between Yardi and JSQ — fees, waterfalls, carry — what does that process look like today?

responses.concern: We use Allvue — doesn't it do what you do?
responses.reframe: GL ≠ performance book of record. Complementary.
responses.talk_track: Allvue is a strong general ledger for partnership accounting. The gap is at the investor level — waterfalls, carried interest, scenario modeling, LP performance. That's where teams end up in Excel. A performance layer sits on top of Allvue and handles everything between accounting and the investor.
responses.follow_up: When your team needs investor-level waterfall output, where does that calculation happen — in Allvue or somewhere else?

responses.concern: We already run Investran in-house.
responses.reframe: Everyone with Investran still lives in Excel for calcs. Complementary positioning.
responses.talk_track: Investran is widely used for fund-level accounting. The pattern is that even with Investran, investor-level performance — waterfalls, fees, carry — still lives in Excel. A performance book of record complements Investran by handling that calc layer.
responses.follow_up: For the calculations outside Investran today — waterfalls, carry, investor allocations — where does that logic live?

responses.concern: We can't get data out of Investran easily.
responses.reframe: Validate frustration. Position as the data normalization layer.
responses.talk_track: That's a common pattern — limited API access, difficulty extracting data for analysis. A platform that ingests data from Investran through file exports gives the team accessibility and flexibility without replacing the underlying GL.
responses.follow_up: If you could query and analyze fund data without going through Investran's native interface, what would that unlock?

responses.concern: We use eFront for fund accounting.
responses.reframe: GL complement. eFront does accounting, performance layer does the rest.
responses.talk_track: eFront is solid for GL and fund accounting — especially customized instances. Investor-level performance, waterfalls, and scenario modeling typically still live in Excel or custom solutions. Integration is straightforward — journal entries come in, performance calcs happen above it.
responses.follow_up: How customized is your eFront instance — and what still lives outside of it in Excel?

responses.concern: We use Yardi for everything.
responses.reframe: Yardi = property-level accounting. Fund-level performance is the gap.
responses.talk_track: Yardi handles property-level accounting well. The gap is at the fund level — investor-level waterfalls, promote structures, co-invest allocations, feeder/blocker rollups. Those calculations sit on top of Yardi, not inside it.
responses.follow_up: After data comes out of Yardi, where do fund-level and investor-level calculations happen?

responses.concern: We just implemented Yardi and Juniper Square — our stack is set.
responses.reframe: Additive, not replacement. GL + portal is set. The calc layer in between is the gap.
responses.talk_track: That's actually good news — GL and portal are in place. The piece in between — investor-level waterfalls, management fees, carried interest, admin reconciliation — that's the calculation layer most firms still handle in Excel. Adding a performance book of record completes the stack without displacing either system.
responses.follow_up: For the calculations between Yardi and JSQ — fees, waterfalls, carry — what does that process look like today?

responses.concern: We're looking at Anaplan for fund modeling.
responses.reframe: General-purpose planning tool vs. purpose-built fund calc engine. Consultant army required.
responses.talk_track: Anaplan is powerful for FP&A and general planning. Configuring it for fund accounting — LPA terms, waterfall tiers, investor allocations — requires significant consulting investment and a dedicated team to maintain. Purpose-built platforms handle those concepts natively, without customization overhead.
responses.follow_up: Has the team scoped what an Anaplan implementation would require in terms of consulting and ongoing internal resources?

responses.concern: We looked at Anaplan but it seemed like a big lift.
responses.reframe: Validate their experience. Position as the productized alternative.
responses.talk_track: That's consistent with what other firms have found. Firms that haven't already committed to a custom Anaplan build — like a few of the largest managers did for $50M+ — tend to prefer a productized solution that doesn't require a dedicated internal team to maintain.
responses.follow_up: What specifically made the Anaplan path feel like too much — was it cost, timeline, or internal resource requirements?

responses.concern: SS&C says they have waterfall capabilities through TNR.
responses.reframe: Legacy platform. Robust but resource-intensive. Position as agile, cost-effective alternative.
responses.talk_track: SS&C has built capabilities through decades of acquisitions. TNR supports complex structures. The trade-off is implementation complexity, cost, and limited fund-side visibility. An independent platform gives the fund its own system of record without depending on the admin's tooling.
responses.follow_up: How much control do you have today over how calculations are done — do you set the rules, or does your admin?

responses.concern: Our admin uses Geneva.
responses.reframe: Geneva is the admin's tool, not the fund's. Position as the GP's own system of record.
responses.talk_track: Geneva is a legacy fund accounting platform used primarily by administrators. The fund's visibility is limited to whatever the admin exports. Having an independent system of record gives the fund direct control over calculations and the ability to validate what the admin sends.
responses.follow_up: How much visibility do you have into how your admin arrives at the numbers they send — can you trace any output back to the source?

responses.concern: We use Chronograph for portfolio data.
responses.reframe: Complementary — portfolio management vs. fund performance. They feed us, we don't replace them.
responses.talk_track: Chronograph is a portfolio management tool — strong at deal-level data, company metrics, valuations. It doesn't do investor-level performance, waterfall calculations, or admin reconciliation. A performance book of record takes that portfolio data in and layers fund-to-investor performance on top.
responses.follow_up: How does your portfolio data from Chronograph connect to your investor-level reporting today — is there manual work in between?

responses.concern: We heard Chronograph is partnering with DealsPlus.
responses.reframe: Partnership extends portfolio analytics. Fund-level performance and waterfall calcs remain outside their scope.
responses.talk_track: That partnership extends Chronograph's capabilities in the portfolio analytics and deal sourcing space. Fund-level performance — investor waterfalls, management fees, carried interest, admin reconciliation — remains a separate category. The two are complementary, not overlapping.
responses.follow_up: When you think about what you need most — is it portfolio-level analytics, or investor-level fund performance and waterfall calculations?

responses.concern: We use iLevel for portfolio monitoring.
responses.reframe: Same as Chronograph — portfolio monitoring vs. fund performance.
responses.talk_track: iLevel handles portfolio monitoring and company-level data well. The gap is the same as Chronograph — it doesn't calculate investor-level waterfalls, management fees, or carried interest. Those calculations are what drive distributions and LP reporting, and they typically live in a separate layer.
responses.follow_up: Does your iLevel data flow directly into your investor reporting, or is there a manual step in between where waterfalls and allocations get calculated?

responses.concern: We spoke with Arcesium — they seem enterprise-grade.
responses.reframe: Arcesium is enterprise-focused and broad. Position on specialization depth and right-sizing.
responses.talk_track: Arcesium offers an enterprise platform across multiple asset classes. For firms focused on closed-end fund structures — PE, real estate, credit — the question is whether a broad enterprise platform or a purpose-built fund performance engine is the better fit. Depth in fund-specific calculations often matters more than breadth of asset class coverage.
responses.follow_up: What drew you to Arcesium specifically — was it the breadth of their platform, or specific capabilities around fund calculations?

responses.concern: Our auditor (Deloitte) suggested we look at their Cascade platform.
responses.reframe: Consulting-grade solution. More expensive, longer implementation. Position as agile and cost-efficient.
responses.talk_track: Deloitte's Cascade is designed for large enterprises needing fully integrated solutions with deep compliance and risk management. For firms that don't need the full Deloitte suite, a more agile platform offers quicker implementation, lower total cost of ownership, and similar calculation rigor without the consulting overhead.
responses.follow_up: Is the interest in Cascade driven by your auditor relationship, or by a specific capability you're looking for?

responses.concern: We've looked at Cascata for waterfall calculations.
responses.reframe: Waterfall point solution vs. full performance book of record.
responses.talk_track: Cascata offers solid waterfall calculation tools. The question is whether the need is only the calc, or the full workflow — capital calls, distributions, management fees, admin reconciliation, and reporting all connected. A point solution for waterfalls still leaves gaps in the surrounding process.
responses.follow_up: Is the primary need waterfall calculations specifically, or does it extend into the full fund operations workflow?

responses.concern: We've seen Lemonedge — they position similarly to you.
responses.reframe: Similar thesis, different execution. Differentiate on proven client base and breadth.
responses.talk_track: Lemonedge has a similar thesis — replacing Excel with auditable workflows. The differentiation comes down to depth of the calculation engine, breadth of fund types validated, and the reference base of institutional clients running live on the platform.
responses.follow_up: What matters most to you — breadth of fund type coverage, depth of waterfall logic, or speed of implementation?

workiva.competitor: Workiva / BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Sigma)

responses.concern: We're investing in BI tools — that should solve our reporting problems.
responses.reframe: BI tools need clean data. If the source is Excel, the BI layer inherits the mess.
responses.talk_track: BI tools are great for visualization and reporting. But they need clean, structured data to work with. If performance data lives in 15 different Excel files, no BI tool can fix the underlying data quality. A performance book of record is the structured data layer that feeds BI tools properly.
responses.follow_up: What's the primary data source for your BI dashboards today — and how much manual preparation goes into getting that data ready?

post_call_summary_schema.description: Schema for generating a post-call summary from the teleprompter session. Captures everything the rep needs to log in Salesforce, share with the team, or prep for the next meeting.

auto_captured.questions_asked: list of checked question IDs with their text
auto_captured.questions_skipped: unchecked must-ask questions (gaps to address next call)

meddpicc_coverage.Pain: count of Pain questions asked vs total

auto_captured.signals_detected: list of all signals that fired during the call with timestamps
auto_captured.rebuttals_used: list of rebuttal cards that were expanded (rep used them)
auto_captured.rebuttals_dismissed: list of rebuttal cards dismissed without expanding
auto_captured.vague_answers: list of partial/vague answers detected with the client text
auto_captured.next_steps_entered: manual next steps the rep entered

generated_sections.call_summary: One paragraph summary: who was on the call, what stage was reached, key pain points surfaced, and whether a next step was booked.
generated_sections.pain_points_captured: Bulleted list of specific pain points the client articulated, extracted from notes where pain signals fired.
generated_sections.meddpicc_gaps: Which MEDDPICC elements were NOT covered — these need to be addressed in the next meeting.
generated_sections.competitor_intel: Any competitors named, what the client said about them, and which talk track the rep used.
generated_sections.recommended_next_questions: Top 5 must-ask questions that were skipped, ranked by priority tier, for the next call.
generated_sections.deal_risk_flags: Signals that indicate deal risk: no EB identified, champion gave vague response, pricing came up before value was established, competitor is actively being evaluated.

mobile_config.description: Configuration for mobile rendering. The teleprompter should work on a phone screen during in-person meetings or when Zoom is on the laptop.
mobile_optimizations: Bottom bar mic indicator stays fixed
Swipe left to dismiss rebuttal cards
Single column layout, no side-by-side elements

thread_continuity_engine.description: After every client response, the queue should prefer questions that continue the current conversational thread rather than jumping to an unrelated topic. This makes the conversation feel natural rather than like a checklist.
thread_continuity_engine.scoring_rule: When a question is checked off, identify which thread it belongs to. Boost the next unchecked question in the same thread by +50 points. This keeps discovery flowing on the same topic before jumping to a new one.
thread_continuity_engine.thread_break_signal: If the client changes topic (mentions a new pain area not related to the current thread), reset the thread boost and let the queue re-rank naturally.

talk_time_nudge.description: If the system detects extended periods without client speech (rep is monologuing), surface a gentle nudge to ask a question. Enterprise reps tend to over-explain when nervous. The best calls have a 40/60 rep-to-client talk ratio.
talk_time_nudge.trigger: No client speech detected for 90+ seconds while call is live

nudge_display.text: ASK A QUESTION — let them talk

escalation.180_seconds: Stronger nudge: "YOU ARE MONOLOGUING — pivot to the next question"
escalation.300_seconds: Red warning: "5 MINUTES WITHOUT CLIENT INPUT — this is a presentation, not a conversation"

talk_time_nudge.ideal_ratio: 40% rep / 60% client in discovery. 60% rep / 40% client in demo.
